physicianTask: Medical Knowledge: Applying Foundational Science Concepts
memoryAnchor: HCM murmur: ↑ with Valsalva/standing (↓ preload → smaller LV → more LVOT obstruction). ↓ with squatting (↑ preload → larger LV → less obstruction). Opposite of AS and MR.
commonTrap: Students confuse HCM with aortic stenosis — AS murmur DECREASES with Valsalva (reduced preload reduces transvalvular flow). HCM murmur INCREASES with Valsalva because smaller LV worsens dynamic obstruction.
stem: A 22-year-old man with a family history of sudden cardiac death has exertional dyspnea and near-syncope. A harsh systolic murmur at the left sternal border increases with Valsalva and standing and decreases with squatting. Which pathophysiologic mechanism explains the murmur changes with these maneuvers?

options.text: Dynamic left ventricular outflow tract obstruction worsened by reduced left ventricular preload
Fixed aortic valve stenosis with reduced trans-valvular flow from lower preload
Mitral valve prolapse with dynamic regurgitation from increased leaflet billowing
Hypertrophied ventricular trabeculae obstructing the right ventricular outflow tract

explanation: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) causes asymmetric septal hypertrophy and dynamic left ventricular outflow tract (LVOT) obstruction, worsened by systolic anterior motion (SAM) of the mitral valve leaflets. Maneuvers that reduce left ventricular preload (Valsalva, standing) decrease LV cavity size, allowing the hypertrophied septum to further obstruct the LVOT — increasing the murmur. Squatting and leg elevation increase preload, enlarge the LV cavity, reduce obstruction, and soften the murmur. This dynamic behavior distinguishes HCM from fixed outflow obstruction (aortic stenosis).
pearl: HCM: dynamic LVOT obstruction. Preload ↓ (Valsalva, standing) → murmur louder. Preload ↑ (squatting, passive leg raise) → murmur softer. Screen family members. Risk of SCD — ICD if indicated. Avoid vigorous exercise.

optionExplanations.A: Dynamic LVOT obstruction from asymmetric septal hypertrophy is the defining hemodynamic abnormality of obstructive HCM. The hypertrophied interventricular septum encroaches on the LV outflow tract during systole. Additionally, the abnormal flow dynamics caused by the narrow LVOT create a Venturi effect that pulls the anterior mitral leaflet toward the septum during systole (systolic anterior motion, SAM), further narrowing the outflow tract. The degree of obstruction is exquisitely sensitive to LV cavity size. With Valsalva (strain phase), intrathoracic pressure rises, venous return falls, and LV cavity shrinks — allowing the hypertrophied septum to occupy more of the LVOT, worsening obstruction and increasing the murmur intensity. Standing similarly reduces venous return. Squatting increases venous return (preload) and increases afterload simultaneously, enlarging the LV cavity and reducing obstruction.
optionExplanations.B: Fixed aortic stenosis from valvular pathology (calcified tricuspid or bicuspid valve) produces a fixed outflow obstruction that does not change significantly with maneuvers affecting preload. In AS, the Valsalva maneuver reduces stroke volume and transvalvular flow, which DECREASES the murmur intensity (less flow through a fixed orifice → smaller pressure gradient → softer murmur). This is the opposite of HCM. Aortic stenosis murmurs also peak earlier in systole (crescendo-decrescendo with midsystolic peak) compared to HCM (late-peaking as obstruction worsens with progressive systolic cavity reduction). The maneuver response is the key differential: Valsalva increases HCM murmur and decreases AS murmur.
optionExplanations.C: Mitral valve prolapse (MVP) is characterized by billowing of one or both mitral leaflets beyond the mitral annular plane into the left atrium during systole. MVP produces a mid-systolic click followed by a late systolic murmur of mitral regurgitation. With Valsalva (reduced preload, smaller LV), the click and murmur shift earlier in systole. With squatting (increased preload, larger LV), the click and murmur shift later or disappear. The murmur of MVP is best heard at the apex and radiates to the axilla — not at the left sternal border as described. MVP is not associated with the family history of sudden cardiac death that makes HCM particularly concerning in this young athlete.
optionExplanations.D: Right ventricular outflow tract (RVOT) obstruction could produce a murmur at the left sternal border. However, RVOT obstruction from RV trabeculae hyperplasia is not the mechanism in HCM — HCM predominantly involves asymmetric septal hypertrophy affecting the LEFT ventricular outflow tract. Pulmonic stenosis (most common form of RVOT obstruction) produces a harsh systolic murmur at the upper left sternal border with a widened split of S2, radiation to the back/left shoulder, and associated ECG changes of right heart strain. Pulmonic stenosis murmur does not characteristically increase with Valsalva in the same way as HCM, and pulmonic stenosis is not associated with sudden cardiac death in young athletes. The family history of sudden death in a young relative is the strongest clue to HCM.

subject: Pharmacology
testedConcept: Torsades de pointes — IKr blockade by sotalol and treatment with magnesium
physicianTask: Patient Care: Pharmacotherapy
memoryAnchor: Torsades = polymorphic VT twisting around isoelectric baseline + long QT. QTc-prolongers: sotalol, macrolides, antipsychotics, methadone, antifungals. Treat: IV Mg²⁺ (even if normal level).
commonTrap: Students give synchronized cardioversion immediately — cardioversion can terminate torsades but IV magnesium is the specific first-line treatment that addresses the underlying ionic mechanism and prevents recurrence.
stem: A 55-year-old woman with AF started on sotalol presents 4 days later with syncope. EKG shows polymorphic ventricular tachycardia with QRS complexes rotating around the isoelectric baseline (QTc 545 ms at baseline). She is hemodynamically stable. What is the most appropriate immediate treatment?

options.text: Intravenous magnesium sulfate
Synchronized cardioversion
Oral amiodarone loading dose
Adenosine 6 mg rapid IV push

explanation: Sotalol blocks IKr (rapid delayed rectifier potassium channel), prolonging action potential duration and QT interval. The polymorphic ventricular tachycardia twisting around the isoelectric baseline is torsades de pointes. Intravenous magnesium sulfate is first-line treatment — it shortens QT, stabilizes the cardiac membrane, and terminates torsades even without documented hypomagnesemia. Sotalol must be discontinued. Temporary overdrive pacing may be needed for refractory cases.
pearl: Torsades de pointes: polymorphic VT from long QT. IV magnesium sulfate is first-line (regardless of magnesium level). Stop offending drug. Correct K⁺ and Mg²⁺. Temporary pacing for bradycardia-dependent torsades.

optionExplanations.A: Magnesium sulfate is the specific pharmacologic treatment for torsades de pointes. Magnesium works through multiple mechanisms: it shortens the cardiac action potential duration (particularly the vulnerable phase of repolarization), blocks calcium-channel mediated afterdepolarizations (early afterdepolarizations, or EADs, which trigger torsades), and stabilizes the cardiac cell membrane. Critically, magnesium is effective regardless of the serum magnesium level — even patients with normal magnesium benefit from IV magnesium in torsades because its antiarrhythmic mechanism is independent of replenishing a deficiency. The standard dose is 2g IV over 1–2 minutes (can repeat once). Sotalol must be discontinued simultaneously, and any other QT-prolonging drugs removed. Serum potassium should be maintained above 4.5 mEq/L (hypokalemia potentiates IKr block and worsens QT prolongation).
optionExplanations.B: Synchronized cardioversion can terminate torsades de pointes when it degenerates into a hemodynamically unstable rhythm causing hypotension or pulselessness. However, for a hemodynamically STABLE patient with recurrent torsades (as described — syncope with spontaneous termination, stable enough to present to the hospital), synchronized cardioversion alone is inappropriate first-line treatment. Cardioversion terminates individual arrhythmia episodes but does not prevent recurrence from the underlying prolonged QT — sotalol is still in the system and the QT remains dangerously prolonged. IV magnesium addresses the mechanism. Cardioversion is the correct treatment for hemodynamically unstable or sustained torsades; magnesium is for stable/recurrent drug-induced torsades.
optionExplanations.C: Amiodarone is a class III antiarrhythmic that itself blocks IKr and prolongs the QT interval significantly. Administering amiodarone to a patient who already has drug-induced QT prolongation and torsades de pointes would be expected to worsen QT prolongation and potentially provoke additional or more refractory torsades. This is one of the most dangerous drug choices for this scenario. Amiodarone is the correct antiarrhythmic for unstable ventricular tachycardia or ventricular fibrillation (polymorphic VT associated with ischemia), but it is contraindicated in torsades because of its own QT-prolonging properties. Students must distinguish between polymorphic VT caused by long QT (torsades → treat with magnesium, NOT antiarrhythmics) versus polymorphic VT in the setting of normal QT (ischemia-driven → treat with amiodarone and address ischemia).
optionExplanations.D: Adenosine is the first-line treatment for paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia (PSVT), specifically AV node-dependent reentrant tachycardias — AVNRT and AVRT. Adenosine transiently blocks AV conduction by activating A1 receptors on the AV node, terminating the reentrant loop. Torsades de pointes is a ventricular arrhythmia originating in the ventricles below the AV node — it does not require the AV node as part of its circuit. Adenosine would have no therapeutic effect on torsades and would be ineffective in terminating ventricular arrhythmias of any kind. Additionally, adenosine has an ultra-short half-life (~10 seconds) and even if it briefly changed AV conduction, it would not address the underlying IKr blockade prolonging the QT interval.

subject: Pharmacology
testedConcept: Asthma step-up therapy — low-dose ICS-formoterol maintenance-and-reliever therapy
physicianTask: Patient Care: Pharmacotherapy
memoryAnchor: Preferred adult Step 3 therapy is low-dose ICS-formoterol used for both maintenance and symptom relief. Never use LABA monotherapy in asthma.
commonTrap: Students switch to LABA monotherapy thinking it is more powerful — LABA monotherapy for asthma carries an FDA black box warning for increased asthma-related mortality. LABA must always be combined with ICS in asthma treatment.
stem: A 35-year-old woman with asthma has daytime symptoms most days, nighttime awakenings twice weekly, and daily rescue-inhaler use despite adherence to low-dose inhaled corticosteroid therapy. Inhaler technique and modifiable triggers have been reviewed. FEV1 is 70% predicted. Which preferred Step 3 regimen should replace her current controller and reliever plan?

options.text: Use low-dose ICS-formoterol as both maintenance and reliever therapy
Switch to LABA inhaler monotherapy and discontinue the ICS
Add oral theophylline as bronchodilator adjunct
Start oral prednisone 40 mg daily for 10 days

explanation: After confirming adherence, inhaler technique, and modifiable triggers, the preferred Step 3 strategy for adults is low-dose ICS-formoterol maintenance-and-reliever therapy. Formoterol has sufficiently rapid onset for symptom relief, while every reliever dose also delivers anti-inflammatory corticosteroid. This approach reduces severe exacerbations compared with a maintenance ICS-LABA plus SABA strategy. LABA monotherapy is unsafe in asthma, theophylline is not a preferred step-up agent, and systemic steroids are reserved for exacerbations rather than chronic controller escalation.
pearl: Preferred adult Step 3: low-dose ICS-formoterol MART. A maintenance ICS-LABA plus an appropriate reliever is an alternative when MART is unavailable.

optionExplanations.A: Low-dose ICS-formoterol maintenance-and-reliever therapy is the preferred Step 3 regimen for adults and adolescents. Formoterol provides rapid bronchodilation, and the paired inhaled corticosteroid treats airway inflammation with every maintenance and rescue dose, reducing severe exacerbations.
optionExplanations.B: LABA monotherapy for asthma is prohibited by an FDA black box warning. The SMART trial (2006) demonstrated a statistically significant increase in asthma-related deaths in patients receiving salmeterol without concomitant ICS — particularly in African-American patients. The mechanism: LABAs reduce acute bronchospasm symptoms and may mask progressive worsening airway inflammation, leading patients to delay seeking care or increasing rescue inhaler use without addressing the underlying inflammation. This delayed recognition of serious exacerbation — without the anti-inflammatory protection of ICS — increases mortality risk. The safety concern is specific to asthma (LABAs are safe as monotherapy in COPD, where the mechanism differs).
optionExplanations.C: Theophylline is a methylxanthine bronchodilator with a very narrow therapeutic index (therapeutic range: 5–15 µg/mL; toxicity at >20 µg/mL including nausea, vomiting, seizures, and arrhythmias). Numerous drug interactions affect theophylline levels (ciprofloxacin, erythromycin, cimetidine raise levels; rifampin lowers levels). It requires frequent serum level monitoring. While theophylline has mild anti-inflammatory and bronchodilatory properties, it is substantially inferior to ICS+LABA as a step-up therapy and is considered a last-resort adjunct after multiple preferred agents have been tried. The risk-benefit profile makes theophylline inappropriate as the first step-up choice when safe and effective alternatives (LABA) are available.
optionExplanations.D: Systemic corticosteroids (oral prednisone) are indicated for acute asthma exacerbations to rapidly reduce airway inflammation during a crisis. They are not appropriate for long-term step-up therapy in moderate persistent asthma because of their well-characterized adverse effects: adrenal suppression, Cushingoid features, osteoporosis, hyperglycemia, immunosuppression, cataracts, and growth retardation in children. Inhaled corticosteroids have excellent topical anti-inflammatory effects with minimal systemic bioavailability (first-pass hepatic metabolism) — they are preferred for long-term control precisely to avoid systemic steroid toxicity. Using oral prednisone daily for ongoing asthma control, rather than stepping up inhaled therapy first, exposes the patient to serious chronic toxicity unnecessarily.

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: Primary spontaneous pneumothorax — large stable PSP treated with active pleural decompression
physicianTask: Patient Care: Clinical Interventions
memoryAnchor: Spontaneous pneumothorax: tall thin young male, bleb rupture. Large or symptomatic stable PSP needs active decompression. Tension = hypotension + tracheal deviation away.
commonTrap: Students diagnose tension pneumothorax for any patient with hypoxia and unilateral absent breath sounds — tension pneumothorax requires hemodynamic instability and mediastinal shift. A simple large pneumothorax with stable BP and midline trachea is managed with chest tube, not emergent needle decompression.
stem: A 22-year-old man who is 6'4" and thin presents with sudden right-sided pleuritic chest pain and dyspnea. Breath sounds are absent over the right hemithorax. Trachea is midline. BP is 122/78 mmHg. CXR shows right-sided pneumothorax with 40% lung collapse. Which is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Needle aspiration or small-bore catheter drainage for a large stable primary spontaneous pneumothorax
Observation only — young patients tolerate large pneumothoraces without intervention
Emergency thoracotomy for definitive treatment
Immediate bilateral needle decompression in the 2nd intercostal space

explanation: Primary spontaneous pneumothorax (PSP) occurs in tall, thin young males from apical subpleural bleb rupture. This patient has a large symptomatic PSP but is hemodynamically stable and has a midline trachea, so this is not tension pneumothorax. Large stable PSP requires active pleural decompression, typically needle aspiration or small-bore catheter/chest tube drainage depending on local pathway. Observation alone is appropriate only for small, minimally symptomatic PSP. Thoracotomy is reserved for recurrent PSP, persistent air leak, or failure of less invasive drainage.
pearl: PSP: tall thin male, apical bleb rupture. Large or symptomatic stable PSP needs aspiration or small-bore catheter drainage. Tension PTX requires immediate needle decompression because hypotension/mediastinal shift are present.

optionExplanations.A: Primary spontaneous pneumothorax with 40% lung collapse is large and symptomatic, so active decompression is required. Because the patient is stable and the trachea is midline, this is not tension pneumothorax. Needle aspiration or small-bore catheter/chest tube drainage is appropriate for a large stable PSP. The key elements are: (1) large PSP requires intervention, (2) stable blood pressure means this is not a crash decompression scenario, and (3) midline trachea argues against tension physiology.
optionExplanations.B: Observation alone is appropriate for small primary spontaneous pneumothorax (<20% collapse) in asymptomatic patients who can be monitored closely. The rationale: air in the pleural space is absorbed at approximately 1.25% per day under normal conditions; supplemental oxygen accelerates reabsorption to approximately 4x the normal rate. Repeat chest X-ray in 6–12 hours confirms stability. However, this patient has 40% lung collapse — a large PSP with symptomatic dyspnea and pleuritic pain. Large PSP is not safely managed by observation because the pneumothorax may expand, respiratory compromise may worsen, and the patient is symptomatic. "Young patients tolerate large pneumothoraces" is a dangerous clinical misconception that can lead to delayed decompression.
optionExplanations.C: Emergency thoracotomy (open surgical chest exploration) is reserved for specific scenarios: (1) recurrent PSP (same side, second or subsequent episode) — surgical pleurodesis and bullectomy at thoracotomy or VATS prevents future episodes; (2) persistent bronchopleural fistula (air leak continuing >5 days despite tube drainage); (3) failure of chest tube drainage; (4) bilateral simultaneous pneumothorax; (5) tension pneumothorax not responding to needle decompression. This patient has a first-episode primary spontaneous pneumothorax with no indications for immediate surgery. Video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery (VATS) is preferred over open thoracotomy when surgical intervention is ultimately needed (smaller incisions, faster recovery).
optionExplanations.D: Bilateral needle decompression is the emergency treatment for tension pneumothorax — a life-threatening emergency caused by progressive air accumulation under pressure in the pleural space, causing mediastinal shift, compression of the contralateral lung, and obstruction of venous return to the heart. Tension pneumothorax presents with: severe respiratory distress, absent breath sounds on the affected side, tracheal deviation AWAY from the affected side, hypotension, tachycardia, and jugular venous distension. This patient has a midline trachea and hemodynamically stable BP — specifically excluding tension pneumothorax. Bilateral decompression in a unilateral pneumothorax would create an iatrogenic contralateral pneumothorax, potentially precipitating the tension physiology the procedure was meant to treat.

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: Acute pancreatitis — Ranson criteria and severity prediction
physicianTask: Patient Care: Prognosis and Outcome
memoryAnchor: Ranson's at admission (GALAW): Glucose>200, Age>55, LDH>350, AST>250, WBC>16K. At 48h (CHOBBS): Ca<8, Hct drop>10, O2<60, BUN rise>5, Base deficit>4, fluid Sequestration>6L. ≥3 = severe.
commonTrap: Students use amylase level to predict severity — serum amylase and lipase elevation do NOT predict severity of pancreatitis. Ranson's criteria and CT severity index predict outcomes.
stem: A 46-year-old man with heavy alcohol use has severe epigastric pain and CT showing peripancreatic stranding. At admission: WBC 17,000/µL, glucose 240 mg/dL, LDH 420 U/L, AST 290 U/L, age 46. At 48 hours: serum Ca 7.0 mg/dL, base deficit 5 mEq/L, PaO2 52 mmHg, Hct dropped 12%, BUN increased 7 mg/dL. What does his Ranson's criteria score indicate?

options.text: Severe pancreatitis — Ranson score ≥3 predicts significant morbidity and mortality
Mild pancreatitis — the elevated amylase indicates mild disease only
Chronic pancreatitis — Ranson criteria apply only to acute disease without prior episodes
Moderate severity — antibiotics should be started immediately for infected necrosis

explanation: Ranson's admission criteria: WBC >16K ✓, glucose >200 ✓, LDH >350 ✓, AST >250 ✓ = 4 at admission. At 48h: Ca <8 ✓, base deficit >4 ✓, PaO2 <60 ✓, Hct drop >10% ✓, BUN rise >5 ✓ = 5 at 48 hours. Total = 9. Ranson score ≥3 indicates severe pancreatitis with significant morbidity and mortality risk. This patient requires ICU-level care, aggressive IV fluid resuscitation, NPO with enteral nutrition when possible, and monitoring for complications (infected necrosis, ARDS, acute kidney injury).
pearl: Ranson score ≥3 = severe pancreatitis. Treat: aggressive IV fluids (LR), analgesics, NPO → enteral feeds early. CT-guided FNA for infected necrosis. Antibiotics NOT routinely indicated — only for confirmed infected necrosis. ERCP only for biliary obstruction.

subject: Biochemistry
testedConcept: Sickle cell disease — HbS polymerization via hydrophobic valine substitution when deoxygenated
physicianTask: Medical Knowledge: Applying Foundational Science Concepts
memoryAnchor: SCD: Glu→Val at beta-globin position 6. Deoxygenated HbS polymerizes (T-state) → sickling → vaso-occlusion. HbF inhibits polymerization. Hydroxyurea ↑ HbF. Crisis triggers: hypoxia, dehydration, cold, infection.
commonTrap: Students say HbS polymerizes when oxygenated — HbS polymerizes in the DEOXYGENATED (T-state) conformation only. In the R (oxy) state, the valine residue is hidden and polymerization does not occur.
stem: A 12-year-old boy with sickle cell disease presents with acute severe long bone and joint pain following a mild upper respiratory infection. Hemoglobin electrophoresis shows predominantly HbS. Which molecular event initiates the vaso-occlusive pain crisis?

options.text: Deoxygenation of HbS allows polymerization through hydrophobic interactions between valine-6 residues on adjacent beta-globin chains
Oxygenation of HbS causes conformational changes that trigger platelet-mediated hemolysis
Oxidative stress from infection directly cross-links alpha-globin chains into insoluble precipitates
Decreased 2,3-BPG binding to HbS causes left-shifted ODC and tissue hypoxia

explanation: The Glu→Val substitution at position 6 of the beta-globin chain creates a hydrophobic valine residue on the HbS molecule surface. In the deoxygenated (T-state) conformation, this valine residue fits into a complementary hydrophobic pocket on an adjacent deoxygenated HbS molecule, initiating polymerization. Polymer rods aggregate into long fibers that distort RBCs into rigid sickle shapes, causing vaso-occlusion, endothelial adhesion, and ischemic pain. Oxygenation (R-state) repositions valine away from its binding site, preventing polymerization — explaining why well-oxygenated patients are asymptomatic.
pearl: SCD: Glu→Val at beta-6 → hydrophobic valine. Deoxy HbS polymerizes → sickling. Triggers: hypoxia, cold, dehydration, infection. HbF protective (interrupts polymer). Hydroxyurea ↑ HbF. Vaso-occlusive crisis: pain + organ damage.

optionExplanations.A: In the deoxygenated T-state of HbS, valine-6 on each beta-globin chain is exposed at the molecular surface. This hydrophobic valine fits into a complementary hydrophobic pocket formed by Phe-85 and Leu-88 on an adjacent deoxyHbS beta-chain, initiating nucleation of polymer rods. As polymerization propagates, rigid intracellular rods distort the RBC membrane into sickle shapes. These stiff cells obstruct the microvasculature, causing endothelial injury, vascular inflammation, and ischemic pain. Hypoxia, cold, and dehydration all promote the T-state and polymer formation.
optionExplanations.B: HbS does NOT polymerize in the oxygenated R-state — the valine-6 residue is repositioned and the hydrophobic binding site is inaccessible. Patients with SCD are asymptomatic between crises because HbS in well-oxygenated blood behaves nearly normally. Platelet-mediated hemolysis is not the mechanism of vaso-occlusive crisis; hemolysis in SCD is due to mechanical RBC destruction from rigidity and membrane damage — not platelet aggregation.
optionExplanations.C: Oxidative stress from infection does contribute to chronic hemolysis and membrane oxidative damage in SCD by generating reactive oxygen species. However, oxidative crosslinking of alpha-globin chains (Heinz body formation) is the mechanism in unstable hemoglobinopathies and G6PD deficiency — not HbS polymerization. In SCD, the primary triggering event for vaso-occlusive crisis is the physical polymer formation from hydrophobic T-state interactions, not oxidative chemistry.
optionExplanations.D: HbS actually has a RIGHTWARD shift of the oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve compared to HbA — it releases oxygen more readily to tissues (higher P50, beneficial for oxygen delivery). 2,3-BPG binding characteristics of HbS are similar to HbA. A leftward-shifted curve (increased oxygen affinity, reduced delivery) would represent a different pathology such as fetal hemoglobin or carboxyhemoglobin. This distractor tests whether students understand that the sickling crisis results from polymerization in the T-state, not from an oxygen delivery curve abnormality.

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: Iron deficiency anemia — low ferritin and elevated TIBC distinguishing from anemia of chronic disease
physicianTask: Patient Care: Laboratory and Diagnostic Studies
memoryAnchor: IDA: ↓ferritin (most specific), ↓serum Fe, ↑TIBC, ↓transferrin sat (<16%). ACD: normal-↑ferritin, ↓serum Fe, ↓TIBC. Ferritin is acute phase reactant — can be falsely normal in IDA+inflammation.
commonTrap: Students use only serum iron to diagnose IDA — serum iron is decreased in BOTH IDA and ACD. The key differentiator is TIBC (↑ in IDA, ↓ in ACD) and ferritin (↓ in IDA, normal-↑ in ACD). Ferritin is the most sensitive single test for IDA when not confounded by inflammation.
stem: A 28-year-old woman with heavy menstrual periods has hemoglobin 8.2 g/dL, MCV 68 fL, MCHC 28 g/dL. Peripheral smear shows microcytic hypochromic RBCs with pencil cells. Which iron study profile best confirms iron deficiency anemia over anemia of chronic disease?

options.text: Low ferritin (<12 µg/L) with elevated total iron-binding capacity (TIBC)
Low serum iron with normal TIBC and elevated ferritin
Elevated serum iron with high ferritin and low TIBC
Normal ferritin with low MCV — ferritin is reliable regardless of inflammation

explanation: Iron deficiency anemia is most specifically confirmed by low ferritin (<12 µg/L), which reflects depleted body iron stores, combined with elevated total iron-binding capacity (TIBC). When iron stores are depleted, the liver increases transferrin (the protein measured as TIBC) synthesis to scavenge scarce iron. Ferritin <12 µg/L is pathognomonic for iron depletion when not confounded by inflammation (ferritin is an acute phase reactant that can be falsely elevated). Anemia of chronic disease shows normal or elevated ferritin with low or normal TIBC — because iron is trapped in macrophages, not depleted.
pearl: IDA: ↓ferritin, ↑TIBC, ↓transferrin saturation <16%, microcytic hypochromic anemia. ACD: normal/↑ferritin, ↓TIBC, ↓serum Fe. Treatment of IDA: oral ferrous sulfate (or IV iron if malabsorption). Always identify the underlying source of blood loss.

optionExplanations.A: Ferritin <12 µg/L reflects depleted total body iron stores and is the most sensitive and specific single marker for iron deficiency anemia when not confounded by concurrent inflammation (ferritin is a positive acute phase reactant that rises with infection, malignancy, or liver disease). The elevated TIBC reflects increased hepatic transferrin production in response to iron starvation — as intracellular iron falls, SREBP-mediated transferrin gene expression increases. The combination of low ferritin + elevated TIBC + low transferrin saturation (<16%) is the classic diagnostic triad of IDA.
optionExplanations.B: Low serum iron with normal TIBC and elevated ferritin is the classic pattern of anemia of chronic disease (ACD). In ACD, inflammatory cytokines (predominantly IL-6) stimulate hepcidin secretion from the liver, which internalizes ferroportin on macrophages and enterocytes, trapping iron intracellularly. The result: serum iron falls (exported iron is blocked), ferritin rises (macrophage iron storage increases), and TIBC is reduced or normal (hepatic transferrin production is not stimulated because intracellular iron is actually abundant).
optionExplanations.C: Elevated serum iron with high ferritin and low TIBC describes iron overload states — hereditary hemochromatosis (autosomal recessive, HFE gene mutation), transfusional hemosiderosis, or sideroblastic anemia. In hereditary hemochromatosis, transferrin saturation exceeds 45–50%. This pattern is the direct opposite of iron deficiency and would not produce microcytic anemia from iron depletion; hemochromatosis causes organ damage from iron deposition (liver fibrosis, cardiomyopathy, diabetes).
optionExplanations.D: Normal ferritin does NOT rule out iron deficiency anemia when concurrent inflammation, infection, or liver disease is present. Ferritin is an acute phase reactant — its level rises with inflammatory states, potentially masking true iron depletion. A patient with iron deficiency anemia and concurrent rheumatoid arthritis or chronic infection may have a ferritin level that falls within the "normal" laboratory range while iron stores are actually depleted. In this setting, soluble transferrin receptor (sTfR) level or bone marrow biopsy may be needed.

subject: Pharmacology
testedConcept: Parkinson disease — dopamine deficiency in substantia nigra and levodopa-carbidopa mechanism
physicianTask: Patient Care: Pharmacotherapy
memoryAnchor: Parkinson: TRAP (Tremor at rest, Rigidity, Akinesia/bradykinesia, Postural instability). Loss of dopaminergic neurons in substantia nigra pars compacta. Levodopa+carbidopa (DOPA decarboxylase inhibitor) = mainstay. Carbidopa prevents peripheral conversion → more L-DOPA reaches brain.
commonTrap: Students think dopamine is given directly — dopamine cannot cross the blood-brain barrier. Levodopa (L-DOPA) crosses the BBB and is converted to dopamine in the brain. Carbidopa inhibits peripheral DOPA decarboxylase, preventing conversion before BBB, maximizing CNS delivery and reducing nausea.
stem: A 68-year-old man has a resting tremor of the right hand (pill-rolling), cogwheel rigidity, masked facies, and shuffling gait with reduced arm swing. He tends to stoop and has difficulty initiating movement. His cognition is intact. Which pharmacologic strategy best addresses the underlying pathophysiology?

options.text: Levodopa combined with carbidopa to increase dopamine in the basal ganglia
Haloperidol to block dopamine D2 receptors and reduce motor hyperactivity
Bromocriptine alone without levodopa to avoid dyskinesias from the outset
Intravenous dopamine infusion to replenish dopaminergic neurotransmission

explanation: Parkinson disease results from degeneration of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra pars compacta, reducing dopamine input to the striatum and disrupting the basal ganglia motor circuit. Levodopa combined with carbidopa is the most effective pharmacotherapy. Levodopa (L-DOPA) crosses the blood-brain barrier and is converted to dopamine in residual nigrostriatal neurons. Carbidopa inhibits peripheral aromatic L-amino acid decarboxylase (DOPA decarboxylase), preventing peripheral conversion of levodopa to dopamine — which cannot re-enter the CNS — thereby increasing CNS dopamine delivery and reducing peripheral side effects (nausea, vomiting). Haloperidol worsens parkinsonism by blocking D2 receptors.
pearl: Parkinson pharmacology: Levodopa+carbidopa = most effective (carbidopa blocks peripheral DOPA decarboxylase). MAO-B inhibitors (selegiline, rasagiline): adjunct, neuroprotective potential. COMT inhibitors (entacapone): prolong levodopa effect. Dopamine agonists (pramipexole): younger patients to delay dyskinesias. Avoid antipsychotics (worsen parkinsonism).

optionExplanations.A: Levodopa is the immediate precursor of dopamine that can cross the blood-brain barrier via the large neutral amino acid transporter. Once in the CNS, residual dopaminergic neurons (and glia) convert levodopa to dopamine via DOPA decarboxylase. Carbidopa is a peripheral DOPA decarboxylase inhibitor that does not cross the BBB — it blocks peripheral conversion of levodopa to dopamine, preventing most of the nausea and cardiovascular effects of peripheral dopamine while maximizing the amount of levodopa reaching the CNS. The combination typically allows 75–80% dose reduction in levodopa while improving efficacy.
optionExplanations.B: Haloperidol is a typical antipsychotic that blocks D2 dopamine receptors in the striatum. Reducing dopaminergic activity in a patient with Parkinson disease — which already has dopamine deficiency — would dramatically worsen all motor symptoms (tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia). Drug-induced parkinsonism from antipsychotics is one of the most common causes of secondary parkinsonism. All typical antipsychotics and many atypical antipsychotics (except quetiapine and clozapine) are contraindicated in Parkinson disease.
optionExplanations.C: Bromocriptine and other dopamine agonists (pramipexole, ropinirole) act directly on postsynaptic D2/D3 receptors and are useful for early Parkinson disease in younger patients to delay the dyskinesias associated with long-term levodopa therapy. However, dopamine agonists are less effective than levodopa for motor symptom control, particularly as disease advances. Monotherapy with dopamine agonists is not the most effective initial strategy for a symptomatic patient — levodopa with carbidopa provides superior motor control, especially for a 68-year-old where dyskinesia timing concerns are less pressing.
optionExplanations.D: Intravenous dopamine cannot cross the blood-brain barrier due to its hydrophilic, charged structure at physiologic pH. Peripheral dopamine infusion acts on cardiovascular dopamine receptors (D1 at high doses on vasculature, renal dopamine receptors) but produces no CNS dopaminergic effect. IV dopamine is used in hemodynamic management of shock — not in neurological conditions. This distractor tests whether students understand the BBB selectivity that requires the levodopa prodrug strategy.

subject: Neurology
testedConcept: Broca aphasia — left inferior frontal gyrus lesion and non-fluent expressive aphasia
physicianTask: Patient Care: Diagnosis
memoryAnchor: Broca = Broken speech (non-fluent, telegraphic), INTACT comprehension. Left inferior frontal gyrus (Brodmann 44/45). MCA superior division. Wernicke = Wordy but Wrong (fluent, poor comprehension). Conduction = can't REPEAT.
commonTrap: Students pick Wernicke aphasia because the patient 'can't speak properly' — Wernicke is fluent but meaningless (jargon). Broca is non-fluent but comprehension is intact. The preserved comprehension distinguishes Broca from global aphasia.
stem: A 61-year-old right-handed man with hypertension has sudden onset difficulty speaking. He understands commands well and follows two-step instructions correctly. His speech is labored, telegraphic, and effortful — he says 'want...water...cup.' He is frustrated by his inability to speak. MRI shows an acute infarct in the left posterior inferior frontal gyrus. Which aphasia syndrome is present?

options.text: Broca aphasia — non-fluent expressive aphasia with intact comprehension
Wernicke aphasia — fluent aphasia with impaired comprehension and paraphasic errors
Global aphasia — profound deficit in both fluency and comprehension
Conduction aphasia — intact fluency and comprehension but impaired repetition

explanation: Broca aphasia results from injury to the left inferior frontal gyrus (Broca area, Brodmann areas 44 and 45), supplied by the superior division of the left middle cerebral artery. Key features: non-fluent, effortful, telegraphic speech (content words preserved, function words omitted), INTACT comprehension (follows commands), impaired repetition, and patient awareness causing frustration. The lesion in the left posterior inferior frontal gyrus confirms the localization. Wernicke aphasia (posterior superior temporal gyrus) is fluent with poor comprehension. Global aphasia destroys both production and comprehension. Conduction aphasia spares fluency and comprehension but impairs repetition.
pearl: Broca area: left inferior frontal gyrus → non-fluent speech, intact comprehension, patient frustrated. Wernicke area: left posterior superior temporal → fluent jargon, poor comprehension, unaware. Conduction: arcuate fasciculus → repetition deficit. Global aphasia: both areas → no fluency or comprehension.

subject: Pharmacology
testedConcept: Status epilepticus — benzodiazepine first-line and phenytoin/levetiracetam second-line
physicianTask: Patient Care: Clinical Interventions
memoryAnchor: Status epilepticus (>5 min or 2 seizures without recovery): Lorazepam IV first → fosphenytoin or levetiracetam → propofol/phenobarbital for refractory. Thiamine before glucose in alcoholics. Check glucose STAT.
commonTrap: Students go straight to phenytoin without giving a benzodiazepine first — benzodiazepines enhance GABA-A and terminate seizures fastest (within minutes). Phenytoin/fosphenytoin loads take 15–20 minutes and are second-line. Never delay benzodiazepine.
stem: A 28-year-old man with known epilepsy is brought by ambulance in generalized convulsive status epilepticus that has lasted 8 minutes. He has IV access. Blood glucose is 94 mg/dL. Which is the most appropriate immediate pharmacologic intervention?

options.text: Lorazepam 4 mg intravenous push (benzodiazepine)
Fosphenytoin 20 mg PE/kg intravenous loading dose
Phenobarbital 20 mg/kg intravenous over 30 minutes
Propofol continuous infusion for burst suppression

explanation: Lorazepam intravenous push is the first-line treatment for generalized convulsive status epilepticus. Benzodiazepines rapidly enhance GABA-A receptor chloride conductance, terminating seizure activity within 1–5 minutes. IV lorazepam (0.1 mg/kg, up to 4 mg) is preferred over diazepam due to longer CNS duration of action. If IV access is unavailable, IM midazolam is equivalent in efficacy. After benzodiazepine, second-line treatment is fosphenytoin or levetiracetam (valproate is an alternative). Phenobarbital and propofol (for burst suppression) are reserved for refractory status epilepticus unresponsive to two antiepileptic agents.
pearl: Status epilepticus protocol: (1) Lorazepam IV (or IM midazolam if no IV) → (2) fosphenytoin or levetiracetam → (3) phenobarbital → (4) general anesthesia (propofol/midazolam) for refractory. Always check glucose; give thiamine before glucose if alcoholic. Target <5 min of seizure activity.

subject: Microbiology
testedConcept: Bacterial meningitis empiric treatment — ceftriaxone plus vancomycin plus dexamethasone
physicianTask: Patient Care: Pharmacotherapy
memoryAnchor: Bacterial meningitis empiric: ceftriaxone (S. pneumo, N. meningitidis) + vancomycin (penicillin-resistant S. pneumo) + dexamethasone (reduces hearing loss, mortality in S. pneumo). Add ampicillin if >50y, immunocompromised, or neonates (Listeria). Dexamethasone BEFORE or WITH first antibiotic dose.
commonTrap: Students give only ceftriaxone — in areas with penicillin-resistant S. pneumoniae (most of the US), vancomycin must be added empirically because ceftriaxone resistance exists. Dexamethasone is given simultaneously to reduce meningeal inflammation and hearing loss.
stem: A 19-year-old college student presents to the ED with fever 39.8°C, severe headache, photophobia, neck stiffness, and a petechial rash developing over 2 hours. He lives in a dormitory. LP is performed after CT rules out herniation. While awaiting LP results, which antibiotic regimen is most appropriate to administer immediately?

options.text: Ceftriaxone plus vancomycin plus dexamethasone
Ceftriaxone alone — vancomycin not needed empirically
Ampicillin plus gentamicin — covers gram-negatives and Listeria broadly
Wait for LP results before starting antibiotics to preserve diagnostic yield

explanation: Ceftriaxone plus vancomycin plus dexamethasone is the appropriate empiric regimen for bacterial meningitis. Ceftriaxone covers N. meningitidis and most S. pneumoniae strains. Vancomycin is added because penicillin-resistant S. pneumoniae is endemic in the US and some strains are also ceftriaxone-resistant — vancomycin covers these resistant isolates. Dexamethasone given before or with the first antibiotic dose reduces meningeal inflammation, hearing loss (by 50% in pneumococcal meningitis), and mortality. The petechial rash and dormitory setting strongly suggest N. meningitidis. Antibiotics must not be delayed for LP if CT is needed first — time to antibiotics is critically related to mortality.
pearl: Empiric bacterial meningitis: ceftriaxone + vancomycin + dexamethasone. Add ampicillin if >50y, neonatal, or immunocompromised (Listeria). Dexamethasone before/with antibiotics (reduces hearing loss, mortality). Give antibiotics BEFORE LP if CT is needed or LP will be delayed >30 min.

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: Gout — monosodium urate crystals and negative birefringence under polarized light
physicianTask: Patient Care: Diagnosis
memoryAnchor: Gout: negatively birefringent needle-shaped monosodium urate crystals (yellow parallel to slow ray). Pseudogout: positively birefringent rhomboid calcium pyrophosphate crystals (blue parallel). "GOUT is yeLLow - Gout rhymes with yeLLow - Negative birefringence." Podagra = 1st MTP.
commonTrap: Students confuse birefringence direction — negative birefringence = crystals appear YELLOW when aligned parallel to the polarizer (compensator slow ray). Positive birefringence = crystals appear BLUE when parallel. Needle shape = urate; rhomboid = CPPD.
stem: A 52-year-old obese man who drinks alcohol daily wakes at 3 AM with excruciating pain, swelling, and redness of the right first metatarsophalangeal joint. He recently started hydrochlorothiazide. Serum uric acid is 9.8 mg/dL. Joint fluid aspirated from the first MTP is cloudy with 55,000 WBC/µL (predominantly neutrophils). Polarized light microscopy shows needle-shaped crystals that appear yellow when oriented parallel to the slow ray of the compensator. Which diagnosis is confirmed?

options.text: Gout — negatively birefringent monosodium urate crystals
Pseudogout — positively birefringent calcium pyrophosphate crystals
Septic arthritis — crystals can coexist with infection
Reactive arthritis — synovial fluid crystals confirm autoinflammatory cause

explanation: Gout is confirmed by negatively birefringent monosodium urate crystals in synovial fluid. Negative birefringence means the crystals appear yellow when oriented parallel to the slow ray of the polarizing compensator (and blue when perpendicular). Urate crystals are needle-shaped (acicular), thin, and sharp. The clinical picture — acute first MTP joint inflammation (podagra), hyperuricemia, thiazide use (reduces renal urate excretion), alcohol use, and obesity — is classic. Acute attacks are treated with NSAIDs, colchicine, or corticosteroids. Long-term: allopurinol (xanthine oxidase inhibitor) to lower uric acid once attack resolves.
pearl: Gout crystals: negatively birefringent (yellow parallel) needle-shaped monosodium urate. CPPD/pseudogout: positively birefringent (blue parallel) rhomboid crystals. Acute attack: NSAIDs, colchicine, or steroids. Prophylaxis: allopurinol or febuxostat. Avoid thiazides, aspirin (low-dose), alcohol.

optionExplanations.A: Monosodium urate crystals form when urate concentrations exceed the solubility threshold (approximately 6.8 mg/dL at physiologic temperature and pH). Crystals deposit in cooler peripheral joints (first MTP, ankle, knee) and in tendons and bursae. Polarized light microscopy with a red compensator identifies the crystals by their birefringence: the optical properties of the crystal lattice slow light in a specific direction. Negative birefringence means the crystal has a lower refractive index along its length than perpendicular — producing yellow color when aligned parallel to the slow ray. The inflammatory cascade involves IL-1β secretion from neutrophils engulfing crystals, driving intense synovitis.
optionExplanations.B: Calcium pyrophosphate dihydrate (CPPD) crystals cause pseudogout, which classically affects the knee (most common), wrist, and symphysis pubis — not the first MTP. CPPD crystals are rhomboid-shaped (not needle-shaped), shorter, and exhibit POSITIVE birefringence (appear blue when aligned parallel to the slow ray). Chondrocalcinosis (calcification visible on X-ray in fibrocartilage) supports CPPD disease. Risk factors include hyperparathyroidism, hemochromatosis, hypothyroidism, and hypomagnesemia.
optionExplanations.C: Septic arthritis can coexist with crystal-induced arthritis (known as "crystal-induced septic arthritis"), but the presence of crystals alone does not exclude infection — when WBC >50,000 or clinical suspicion is high, cultures must be sent even if crystals are identified. However, in this clinical scenario with classic podagra, hyperuricemia, thiazide use, and characteristic crystals, gout is the primary diagnosis. The WBC of 55,000 is consistent with gout (often 20,000–100,000 in acute gout) and does not require empiric antibiotics in the absence of other infection signs.
optionExplanations.D: Reactive arthritis is a seronegative spondyloarthropathy triggered by prior infection (Chlamydia, Campylobacter, Shigella) and characterized by asymmetric oligoarthritis, urethritis, and conjunctivitis. Synovial fluid in reactive arthritis shows inflammatory WBC (5,000–50,000) but NO crystals — reactive arthritis is not a crystal deposition disease. The presence of crystals confirms crystal arthropathy, not reactive arthritis.

subject: Pharmacology
testedConcept: Osteoporosis bisphosphonates — osteoclast apoptosis and anti-resorptive mechanism
physicianTask: Patient Care: Pharmacotherapy
memoryAnchor: Bisphosphonates (alendronate, risedronate, zoledronic acid): inhibit farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase in osteoclasts → impair osteoclast function and survival → ↓bone resorption. Take on empty stomach, upright 30 min (esophagitis risk). Rare: osteonecrosis of jaw, atypical femur fracture.
commonTrap: Students say bisphosphonates stimulate osteoblasts — bisphosphonates primarily inhibit OSTEOCLASTS (anti-resorptive). Teriparatide (PTH analogue) is the only agent that primarily stimulates osteoblasts (anabolic).
stem: A 68-year-old postmenopausal woman has a T-score of -2.9 on DXA (lumbar spine). She has no contraindications. Which pharmacologic mechanism best describes alendronate's effect on bone metabolism?

options.text: Inhibits farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase in osteoclasts causing osteoclast apoptosis and reduced bone resorption
Stimulates PTH receptor on osteoblasts to increase bone formation
Binds estrogen receptor in bone tissue to restore pre-menopausal bone density
Inhibits RANKL from osteoblasts to prevent osteoclast differentiation

explanation: Bisphosphonates inhibit farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase in osteoclasts, impairing the mevalonate pathway and preventing prenylation of signaling proteins. This disrupts osteoclast cytoskeletal function, causes osteoclast apoptosis, and reduces bone resorption. Bisphosphonates are incorporated into bone matrix and released during osteoclast-mediated resorption, giving prolonged duration of effect. Alendronate reduces vertebral and hip fracture risk by 40–50%. Key adverse effects: esophageal erosion (must take with water, remain upright 30–60 min), rare osteonecrosis of the jaw, and atypical subtrochanteric femur fractures with prolonged use (>5 years).
pearl: Bisphosphonates: anti-resorptive via osteoclast apoptosis (farnesyl PP synthase inhibition). First-line for osteoporosis. Alendronate/risedronate: oral weekly/daily. Zoledronic acid: annual IV. Teriparatide: anabolic (stimulates osteoblasts). Denosumab: monoclonal anti-RANKL (osteoclast inhibitor).

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: Paget disease of bone — osteoclast hyperactivity and high-turnover mosaic pattern
physicianTask: Patient Care: Diagnosis
memoryAnchor: Paget disease: ↑ALP (massively) + normal Ca and PO4 + normal PTH. X-ray: lytic phase → mixed → sclerotic 'cotton wool.' Bone pain, hearing loss (cranial nerve compression), high-output CHF (AVMs), sarcoma transformation (<1%). Treat: bisphosphonates.
commonTrap: Students confuse Paget disease with metastatic bone disease because both cause elevated ALP and bone lesions — Paget has NORMAL calcium and phosphate, while metastatic disease often causes hypercalcemia. Paget's 'cotton wool' X-ray appearance and the mosaic cement line pattern on biopsy are specific.
stem: A 72-year-old man has progressive right-sided hearing loss and bowing of the right tibia with warmth over the bone. X-ray shows cortical thickening, trabecular coarsening, and patchy sclerosis of the tibia ('cotton wool' appearance). Labs: ALP 1840 U/L, calcium 9.4 mg/dL, phosphate 3.8 mg/dL, normal PTH. Which pathologic mechanism explains these findings?

options.text: Paget disease of bone — disordered osteoclast hyperactivity with subsequent disorganized bone remodeling
Osteosarcoma — rapidly growing malignant tumor with Codman triangle on X-ray
Primary hyperparathyroidism — PTH-mediated osteoclast activation causing bone lesions
Osteopetrosis — impaired osteoclast function causing dense brittle bones

explanation: Paget disease of bone (osteitis deformans) results from disordered osteoclast hyperactivity causing chaotic bone remodeling: excessive resorption followed by disorganized new bone formation. The woven bone produced in the reparative phase creates the pathognomonic mosaic cement line pattern on biopsy. Markedly elevated ALP (bone isoform) with normal calcium, phosphate, and PTH is the defining biochemical profile. Complications: bone deformity (bowing of tibia), fractures, hearing loss (temporal bone involvement compressing CN VIII), high-output heart failure from arteriovenous shunts in highly vascular pagetic bone, and rare sarcomatous transformation (<1%). Treatment: bisphosphonates suppress osteoclast activity.
pearl: Paget disease: ↑↑ALP, normal Ca/PO4/PTH. X-ray: 'cotton wool' sclerosis. Complications: hearing loss, high-output CHF, bone sarcoma. Mosaic cement lines on biopsy. Treat: zoledronic acid (bisphosphonate) — most effective single dose. Asymptomatic disease at non-critical sites may not require treatment.

subject: Pharmacology
testedConcept: Major depressive disorder — SSRI mechanism and serotonin transporter inhibition
physicianTask: Patient Care: Pharmacotherapy
memoryAnchor: SSRIs inhibit serotonin reuptake transporter (SERT) → ↑synaptic serotonin. First-line MDD. Side effects: sexual dysfunction, GI upset, insomnia (early), serotonin syndrome (with MAOIs, linezolid, triptans). Discontinuation syndrome (fluoxetine least likely — long half-life).
commonTrap: Students say SSRIs increase serotonin production — SSRIs block REUPTAKE (SERT transporter) at the presynaptic membrane, increasing synaptic serotonin without affecting synthesis. Synthesis is rate-limited by tryptophan hydroxylase, which SSRIs don't affect.
stem: A 34-year-old woman presents with 7 weeks of depressed mood, anhedonia, hypersomnia, fatigue, difficulty concentrating, and feelings of worthlessness most days. She meets diagnostic criteria for major depressive disorder. Sertraline is prescribed. Which best describes the primary mechanism of action of sertraline?

options.text: Inhibits serotonin reuptake transporter (SERT), increasing synaptic serotonin concentration
Inhibits monoamine oxidase A, preventing serotonin and norepinephrine degradation
Blocks postsynaptic serotonin receptors to reduce serotonergic tone
Stimulates tryptophan hydroxylase to increase serotonin biosynthesis

explanation: Sertraline and all selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) work by blocking the serotonin reuptake transporter (SERT) on the presynaptic neuron terminal. This prevents serotonin from being pumped back into the presynaptic neuron after release, increasing serotonin concentration in the synaptic cleft and enhancing serotonergic neurotransmission. SSRIs do not affect serotonin synthesis. Common side effects: GI upset (nausea, diarrhea), sexual dysfunction (most common cause of non-adherence), insomnia, and weight gain with long-term use. Serotonin syndrome risk when combined with MAOIs, linezolid, tramadol, or triptans.
pearl: SSRIs: inhibit SERT → ↑synaptic serotonin. First-line for MDD, GAD, OCD, PTSD, panic disorder. Onset: 2–4 weeks clinical effect. Sexual dysfunction most common cause of non-adherence. Fluoxetine: longest half-life (least discontinuation syndrome). Serotonin syndrome: hyperthermia + clonus + agitation (treat with cyproheptadine).

optionExplanations.A: SERT (SLC6A4) is a sodium-coupled transporter on serotonergic neuron terminals that normally recycles released serotonin back into the presynaptic cell for repackaging or degradation. SSRIs bind competitively within the serotonin-binding site of SERT, blocking reuptake and prolonging serotonin residence in the synapse. Chronic administration leads to receptor downregulation and adaptive changes in postsynaptic serotonin receptors — explaining the 2–4 week delay to clinical antidepressant effect. SSRIs have high selectivity for SERT over norepinephrine (NET) and dopamine transporters (DAT), distinguishing them from tricyclic antidepressants.
optionExplanations.B: Monoamine oxidase inhibitors (MAOIs) prevent degradation of serotonin, norepinephrine, and dopamine by inhibiting the intracellular enzyme monoamine oxidase. MAOIs (phenelzine, tranylcypromine, selegiline) are effective antidepressants but have severe drug interactions (serotonin syndrome with serotonergic drugs, hypertensive crisis with tyramine-containing foods). They are generally reserved for treatment-resistant MDD. SSRIs do not inhibit MAO — they act upstream on reuptake, not degradation.
optionExplanations.C: Blocking postsynaptic serotonin receptors would REDUCE serotonergic neurotransmission — the opposite of what is needed in MDD. Atypical antipsychotics like quetiapine partially block 5-HT2A receptors, which paradoxically can augment antidepressant response through complex network mechanisms. However, SSRIs are not receptor antagonists — they increase synaptic serotonin, which then acts on all receptor subtypes. Cyproheptadine, a serotonin receptor antagonist, is actually used to TREAT serotonin syndrome.
optionExplanations.D: Tryptophan hydroxylase is the rate-limiting enzyme in serotonin biosynthesis (converting tryptophan to 5-hydroxytryptophan). No current antidepressant works by stimulating this enzyme — doing so would increase serotonin synthesis but would be difficult to regulate and could cause serotonin toxicity. Dietary supplementation with L-tryptophan or 5-HTP (5-hydroxytryptophan) can modestly increase serotonin and has some evidence as mild antidepressants, but SSRIs act purely at the transporter level.

subject: Pharmacology
testedConcept: Schizophrenia — dopamine hypothesis and D2 receptor blockade by antipsychotics
physicianTask: Patient Care: Pharmacotherapy
memoryAnchor: Schizophrenia: ↑dopamine in mesolimbic (positive sx: hallucinations, delusions), ↓dopamine in mesocortical (negative sx: flat affect, alogia). Typical antipsychotics: D2 blocker → extrapyramidal side effects (EPS). Atypical: D2+5HT2A block → fewer EPS, treats negative sx better.
commonTrap: Students say antipsychotics increase serotonin to treat schizophrenia — typical antipsychotics work ONLY by blocking D2 receptors. Atypical antipsychotics ALSO block 5-HT2A, which reduces EPS risk and modestly improves negative symptoms.
stem: A 22-year-old man believes the government has implanted a microchip in his brain and that television shows are broadcasting messages specifically to him. He also hears voices commenting on his behavior. These symptoms began 8 months ago. He has no history of substance use. Haloperidol is started. Which receptor mechanism explains its therapeutic effect?

options.text: Dopamine D2 receptor blockade in the mesolimbic pathway reducing psychotic symptoms
Serotonin 5-HT2A receptor agonism in the prefrontal cortex
NMDA receptor antagonism blocking glutamate excitotoxicity
GABA-A receptor potentiation reducing limbic system hyperactivity

explanation: Haloperidol is a typical (first-generation) antipsychotic whose primary mechanism is dopamine D2 receptor blockade in the mesolimbic pathway. The dopamine hypothesis of schizophrenia posits that excess dopaminergic activity in the mesolimbic system drives positive symptoms (hallucinations, delusions, thought disorder). Blocking D2 receptors reduces this hyperactivity and alleviates positive symptoms. However, D2 blockade in the nigrostriatal pathway causes extrapyramidal side effects (EPS: dystonia, akathisia, parkinsonism, tardive dyskinesia). D2 blockade in the tuberoinfundibular pathway causes hyperprolactinemia. Atypical antipsychotics additionally block 5-HT2A, reducing EPS and modestly improving negative symptoms.
pearl: Antipsychotic D2 blockade: mesolimbic (↓positive sx) + nigrostriatal (→EPS) + tuberoinfundibular (→hyperprolactinemia) + mesocortical (↓worsens negative sx). Atypicals (clozapine, olanzapine, risperidone): D2+5HT2A block → fewer EPS. Clozapine: most effective (reserved for treatment-resistant — agranulocytosis risk).

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: PTSD — DSM-5 criteria including hyperarousal and duration beyond 1 month
physicianTask: Patient Care: Diagnosis
memoryAnchor: PTSD DSM-5: Exposure to trauma + (1) Re-experiencing (flashbacks, nightmares) + (2) Avoidance + (3) Negative cognitions/mood + (4) Hyperarousal (startle, insomnia, irritability). Duration >1 month. Acute stress disorder: 3 days–1 month post-trauma.
commonTrap: Students diagnose PTSD for any trauma reaction in the first month — acute stress disorder (ASD) is the diagnosis for trauma symptoms lasting 3 days to 1 month. PTSD requires symptoms persisting >1 month. Both require the same four symptom clusters.
stem: A 29-year-old combat veteran returned from deployment 6 months ago. He has recurring intrusive nightmares about combat, flashbacks triggered by loud noises, avoids crowds and fireworks, feels emotionally numb and detached from family, and has exaggerated startle response with sleep disturbance. These symptoms have been present for 5 months and significantly impair his function. Which diagnosis best applies?

options.text: Post-traumatic stress disorder — trauma with re-experiencing, avoidance, negative cognitions, and hyperarousal lasting >1 month
Acute stress disorder — same symptom clusters as PTSD but limited to <1 month duration
Adjustment disorder — stress response out of proportion to identifiable stressor
Generalized anxiety disorder — excessive worry across multiple domains lasting >6 months

explanation: Post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) requires: exposure to actual or threatened death/serious injury/sexual violence, followed by all four symptom clusters: (1) re-experiencing (intrusive memories, flashbacks, nightmares), (2) avoidance of trauma-related stimuli, (3) negative alterations in cognition and mood (emotional numbing, detachment, negative beliefs), and (4) alterations in arousal and reactivity (hypervigilance, exaggerated startle, sleep disturbance, irritability). Duration must exceed 1 month with significant functional impairment. This veteran meets all criteria with 5-month duration. First-line treatment: prolonged exposure therapy or EMDR; medications: sertraline or paroxetine (FDA-approved).
pearl: PTSD: trauma + 4 clusters (re-experiencing + avoidance + negative cognitions + hyperarousal) + >1 month + functional impairment. Acute stress disorder: same + 3 days to 1 month. First-line Tx: trauma-focused CBT (prolonged exposure), EMDR. Medications: sertraline or paroxetine (SSRIs, FDA-approved).

subject: Pharmacology
testedConcept: Bipolar disorder — lithium mechanism and narrow therapeutic index monitoring
physicianTask: Patient Care: Pharmacotherapy
memoryAnchor: Lithium: narrow TI (0.6–1.2 mEq/L), renal elimination, toxicity: tremor, GI, polyuria (nephrogenic DI), T-wave changes → seizures, arrhythmias, encephalopathy. Monitor: levels, Cr, TFTs. Contraindicated in pregnancy (Ebstein). NSAIDs/thiazides raise lithium levels.
commonTrap: Students forget that NSAIDs and thiazide diuretics significantly increase lithium levels by reducing renal lithium clearance — ibuprofen for a headache in a lithium patient can cause lithium toxicity. ACE inhibitors also raise levels. These drug interactions cause most lithium toxicity.
stem: A 32-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder has been stable on lithium for 3 years. She develops a urinary tract infection and is prescribed trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole. The next week she starts taking ibuprofen for headaches. She presents with coarse tremor, confusion, and muscle twitching. Her lithium level is 2.4 mEq/L. Which pharmacokinetic interaction best explains the toxicity?

options.text: NSAIDs reduce renal prostaglandin synthesis, decreasing lithium clearance and raising serum lithium levels
Trimethoprim inhibits hepatic cytochrome P450 enzymes responsible for lithium metabolism
Ibuprofen displaces lithium from plasma protein binding, increasing free fraction
NSAIDs increase gastrointestinal absorption of lithium by enhancing ileal transport

explanation: NSAIDs reduce prostaglandin synthesis by inhibiting cyclooxygenase. Renal prostaglandins (especially PGE2) promote natriuresis — by reducing prostaglandins, NSAIDs cause sodium retention. Lithium is eliminated renally in close parallel to sodium (reabsorbed by the same proximal tubule transporters). When sodium is retained, the kidney also retains lithium, raising serum lithium levels. Lithium has a narrow therapeutic index (0.6–1.2 mEq/L therapeutic; >1.5 mEq/L toxic). The level of 2.4 mEq/L explains the coarse tremor, confusion, and fasciculations. Trimethoprim also modestly reduces lithium clearance by blocking renal tubular secretion — both drugs combined caused the toxicity. Treatment: stop offending agents, IV fluids, and hemodialysis for severe toxicity (level >3.5 mEq/L or hemodynamic instability).
pearl: Lithium toxicity interactions: NSAIDs + thiazides + ACEi/ARBs + dehydration all ↓renal clearance → ↑levels. Lithium levels: 0.6–1.2 therapeutic, >1.5 early toxicity (tremor, GI), >2.0 severe (confusion, seizure, arrhythmia). Treat: stop drugs, IV NS, dialysis if severe.

subject: Biostatistics
testedConcept: Sensitivity and specificity — 2x2 table calculations and clinical utility
usmleContentArea: Biostatistics, Epidemiology/Population Health, & Interpretation of the Medical Literature
physicianTask: Medical Knowledge: Applying Foundational Science Concepts
memoryAnchor: Sensitivity = TP/(TP+FN). Specificity = TN/(TN+FP). SnNout: high Sensitivity, Negative test rules OUT disease. SpPin: high Specificity, Positive test rules IN disease. PPV depends on prevalence. High sensitivity = screening. High specificity = confirmation.
commonTrap: Students confuse sensitivity with PPV — sensitivity is fixed by the test, but PPV changes with disease prevalence. A test with 99% sensitivity has low PPV in a low-prevalence population because most 'positives' are false positives.
stem: A new biomarker for pancreatic cancer is studied in 1000 patients: 100 have cancer, 900 do not. The test is positive in 90 of 100 patients with cancer and negative in 810 of 900 patients without cancer. What are the sensitivity and specificity of this test?

options.text: Sensitivity 90%, specificity 90%
Sensitivity 90%, specificity 10%
Sensitivity 47%, specificity 90%
Sensitivity 90%, specificity 47%

explanation: From the 2x2 table: TP=90 (cancer, test+), FN=10 (cancer, test-), FP=90 (no cancer, test+), TN=810 (no cancer, test-). Sensitivity = TP/(TP+FN) = 90/(90+10) = 90/100 = 90%. Specificity = TN/(TN+FP) = 810/(810+90) = 810/900 = 90%. Both are 90% in this example. PPV = TP/(TP+FP) = 90/180 = 50% — despite the excellent sensitivity and specificity, PPV is only 50% because of the 10% disease prevalence. This illustrates why PPV depends critically on prevalence, even with a good test.
pearl: Sensitivity = TP/(TP+FN) — how good at finding true positives (SnNout). Specificity = TN/(TN+FP) — how good at excluding false positives (SpPin). PPV = TP/(TP+FP) — probability disease given positive test (prevalence-dependent). NPV = TN/(TN+FN). Increasing prevalence → ↑PPV, ↓NPV.

subject: Biostatistics
testedConcept: Relative risk and number needed to treat — calculating from 2x2 table data
usmleContentArea: Biostatistics, Epidemiology/Population Health, & Interpretation of the Medical Literature
physicianTask: Practice-Based Learning and Improvement
memoryAnchor: RR = (risk in exposed)/(risk in unexposed) = [a/(a+b)] / [c/(c+d)]. ARR = risk unexposed − risk exposed. NNT = 1/ARR. RR >1 = harm. RR <1 = protection. Cohort study = can calculate RR. Case-control = calculate OR only (rare disease, retrospective).
commonTrap: Students try to calculate RR from a case-control study — you CANNOT directly calculate RR from case-control studies (exposure is artificially set, not naturally occurring). Case-control studies calculate the odds ratio (OR), which approximates RR only for rare diseases.
stem: A randomized trial of statin therapy versus placebo in 10,000 patients (5,000 per arm) shows: 200 statin patients had MI vs 400 placebo patients. What is the relative risk of MI with statin therapy and the number needed to treat (NNT) to prevent one MI?

options.text: RR = 0.5 and NNT = 25
RR = 2.0 and NNT = 50
RR = 0.5 and NNT = 50
RR = 0.04 and NNT = 25

explanation: Risk in statin group = 200/5000 = 4%. Risk in placebo group = 400/5000 = 8%. Relative risk (RR) = 4%/8% = 0.5 — so RR = 0.5 and NNT = 25, confirming the correct answer. Absolute risk reduction (ARR) = 8% − 4% = 4% = 0.04. Number needed to treat (NNT) = 1/ARR = 1/0.04 = 25. This means 25 patients must be treated with a statin for 1 MI to be prevented. NNT is clinically meaningful: lower NNT = more effective or more clinically significant intervention. Relative risk is used for etiologic studies; NNT is used for clinical decision-making.
pearl: RR = risk in exposed / risk in unexposed. RR=0.5 means 50% relative risk reduction. ARR = risk control − risk treatment. NNT = 1/ARR. NNT <10 = highly effective. Relative risk reduction (RRR) = 1-RR = 50% (appears large even when ARR is small — pharmaceutical companies prefer RRR).

subject: Biostatistics
testedConcept: Type I and Type II errors — alpha, beta, and statistical power in hypothesis testing
usmleContentArea: Biostatistics, Epidemiology/Population Health, & Interpretation of the Medical Literature
physicianTask: Practice-Based Learning and Improvement
memoryAnchor: Type I error = false positive = alpha (p-value). Type II = false negative = beta. Power = 1-beta. ↑sample size → ↑power, ↓both error types. p<0.05: 5% chance of Type I error. Power typically set at 0.8 (80% chance of detecting true effect).
commonTrap: Students say a p-value of 0.04 proves the treatment works — p<0.05 means there's a 4% chance of observing this result IF the null hypothesis is true (Type I error rate). It does NOT confirm the treatment works; it just makes the null hypothesis unlikely. Effect size and clinical significance are separate from statistical significance.
stem: A clinical trial of a new antihypertensive finds no statistically significant difference in blood pressure reduction compared to placebo (p=0.18) in 50 patients per group. The investigators are concerned they may have missed a true treatment effect. Which type of error occurred and what is the most direct way to reduce it?

options.text: Type II error (false negative) — increase the sample size to increase statistical power
Type I error (false positive) — lower the alpha threshold to reduce false positives
Type II error (false negative) — lower the alpha threshold to 0.01 to increase sensitivity
Type I error (false positive) — replicate the study in a larger population

explanation: The investigators concluded there was no treatment effect when there may actually be one — this is a Type II error (false negative). The probability of a Type II error is beta (β); power = 1 − β = the probability of detecting a true effect when it exists. A small sample size (50 per group) is the most common cause of Type II error because insufficient sample size reduces power. The most direct way to increase power is to increase sample size. Other methods: increase the effect size being studied, increase alpha (accept higher Type I error risk), use a within-subject design, or reduce outcome variability. Type I error (alpha, false positive) is the probability of finding a significant result when the null hypothesis is true.
pearl: Type I error (α): reject true null = false positive. Type II error (β): fail to reject false null = false negative. Power = 1−β. ↑sample size → ↓both errors, ↑power. To ↑power: ↑n, ↑α, ↑effect size, ↓variability. Standard: α=0.05, power=0.8 (β=0.2).

subject: Biostatistics
testedConcept: Randomized controlled trial vs cohort study — hierarchy of evidence and causality
usmleContentArea: Biostatistics, Epidemiology/Population Health, & Interpretation of the Medical Literature
physicianTask: Practice-Based Learning and Improvement
memoryAnchor: Study hierarchy (strongest → weakest): Systematic review/meta-analysis → RCT → Cohort → Case-control → Cross-sectional → Case report. Only RCTs can prove causality (randomization controls confounders). Cohort → relative risk. Case-control → odds ratio. Cross-sectional → prevalence.
commonTrap: Students say a large cohort study proves causation — cohort studies (and all observational studies) show ASSOCIATION, not causation, because unmeasured confounders could explain the relationship. Only randomization in an RCT eliminates systematic confounding bias.
stem: A cohort study of 50,000 nurses over 20 years shows that those who took aspirin daily had a 35% lower rate of colorectal cancer (RR 0.65, 95% CI 0.52–0.82). A pharmaceutical company claims this proves that aspirin prevents colorectal cancer. Which methodologic limitation is most significant?

options.text: Confounding — self-selected aspirin users may differ systematically in other health behaviors
Selection bias — the study excluded men, limiting external validity only
Information bias — aspirin use was not objectively verified with pill counts
Recall bias — participants may not accurately remember past aspirin use

explanation: Confounding is the primary methodologic limitation in cohort studies. People who choose to take aspirin may differ systematically from non-users in many other ways — they may be more health-conscious, exercise more, eat better diets, smoke less, and have more regular medical care. These "healthy user" confounders could explain the reduced cancer rate independent of aspirin. Despite the strong association (RR 0.65) and statistical significance (95% CI excludes 1.0), cohort studies cannot prove causality because randomization does not occur. An RCT would address confounding by randomly assigning aspirin vs. placebo. Selection bias (excluding men) limits generalizability but does not affect internal validity. Recall bias applies to retrospective studies (case-control), not prospective cohort studies.
pearl: Confounding: third variable associated with both exposure and outcome. Controlled by: randomization (RCT), restriction, matching, stratification, multivariable regression. Only RCTs prove causality through randomization. Cohort studies prove association. Evidence hierarchy: RCT > cohort > case-control > cross-sectional.

subject: Ethics
testedConcept: Decision-making capacity — appreciation and reasoning beyond simple factual repetition
physicianTask: Professionalism, Legal, and Ethical Principles
memoryAnchor: Informed consent: (1) Disclosure (diagnosis, treatment, risks, alternatives, prognosis), (2) Understanding (patient comprehends), (3) Voluntariness (no coercion), (4) Decision-making capacity (not competence — judge decides competence). Exceptions: emergencies, therapeutic privilege, waiver.
commonTrap: Students say a patient who refuses a clearly beneficial treatment lacks capacity — refusal alone does NOT indicate incapacity. Patients have the right to refuse any treatment if they have capacity, even if the refusal may lead to serious harm or death. Capacity assessment is task-specific, not global.
stem: A 58-year-old man with newly diagnosed colon cancer is considering right hemicolectomy. The surgeon explains the procedure, risks, alternatives, and expected prognosis. The patient accurately repeats these facts. Which additional statement would best demonstrate that he can appreciate the consequences and reason about the decision?

options.text: Without surgery the cancer may spread; I accept the operative risks because cure is my main goal
I can repeat the names of the operation and its complications
My daughter wants surgery, so I will let her decide for me
Signing the consent form proves that I have capacity

explanation: Decision-making capacity requires the ability to communicate a choice, understand relevant information, appreciate how the information applies personally, and reason about options. Repeating facts demonstrates understanding but not necessarily appreciation or reasoning. Relating the risk of progression and operative harms to a personal goal demonstrates both. A signature alone does not establish capacity, and deferring automatically to a relative does not demonstrate independent reasoning.
pearl: Capacity is decision-specific and clinical: choice, understanding, appreciation, and reasoning. Competence is a legal determination.

subject: Ethics
testedConcept: Advance directives — living will vs durable power of attorney for healthcare
physicianTask: Professionalism, Legal, and Ethical Principles
memoryAnchor: Living will: specific instructions for specific scenarios (no CPR if terminal). Durable POA for healthcare (healthcare proxy): person named to make decisions when patient lacks capacity. DPOA > living will in conflicts. DNR = physician order (≠ advance directive). Substitute decision-maker hierarchy when no AD.
commonTrap: Students think a DNR order is the same as a living will — a DNR is a physician order written based on the patient's or surrogate's wishes; it is not an advance directive itself. A living will provides written preferences; a DNR is the physician's implementation of those preferences.
stem: A 76-year-old woman with end-stage COPD is admitted in respiratory failure. She is unable to communicate. Her daughter presents a document signed by her mother 3 years ago stating: "If I am terminally ill and unable to make decisions, I wish to avoid mechanical ventilation." Her son arrives and insists his mother would want all possible measures. The document also names a neighbor as the healthcare proxy (durable power of attorney for healthcare). Who has decision-making authority?

options.text: The designated healthcare proxy (neighbor) — their authority supersedes family members when capacity is lost
The son — nearest family member takes precedence over a named proxy
The physician — treating team makes decisions when family members disagree
Ethics committee — all family disputes require committee consultation before any decision

explanation: When a patient has designated a healthcare proxy (durable power of attorney for healthcare), that person has legal authority to make medical decisions when the patient loses capacity. This authority supersedes family members who have not been legally designated, regardless of their relationship to the patient. The neighbor, as the named healthcare proxy, has the legal and ethical obligation to make decisions consistent with the patient's stated wishes (as also expressed in the living will preferring no mechanical ventilation). The son's wishes, while emotionally understandable, do not override the legally designated proxy. The living will provides guidance on the patient's values, and the healthcare proxy is to honor them.
pearl: Healthcare proxy (DPOA for healthcare): legal decision-making authority when patient lacks capacity — supersedes all family members. Living will: specific treatment instructions for specific circumstances. When they conflict: healthcare proxy interprets and applies living will in context. No advance directive: use substitute decision-maker hierarchy (spouse → adult child → parent → sibling).

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: Ovarian torsion — Doppler blood flow and timing of surgical intervention
usmleContentArea: Female and Transgender Reproductive System & Breast
physicianTask: Patient Care: Clinical Interventions
memoryAnchor: Ovarian torsion: sudden severe unilateral pelvic pain + nausea/vomiting + adnexal mass. Doppler US: absent or reduced blood flow (NOT always absent — torsion can have intermittent flow). Treat: emergent laparoscopy. Detorsion and ovarian conservation preferred if viable. Time-sensitive: ischemia within 4–6 hours.
commonTrap: Students think absent Doppler flow must be present to diagnose torsion — Doppler can show preserved or intermittent flow in up to 60% of torsion cases (dual blood supply, partial torsion). The diagnosis is clinical + US adnexal mass; do not wait for absent Doppler to proceed surgically when clinical suspicion is high.
stem: A 17-year-old girl presents with 5 hours of acute severe right lower quadrant pain, nausea, and vomiting. She has no fever. Transvaginal US shows a 6-cm right ovarian mass with edema and significantly reduced Doppler blood flow to the right ovary. Which is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Emergent laparoscopy with ovarian detorsion and conservation if ovary is viable
CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis to confirm diagnosis before operating
IV antibiotics for pelvic inflammatory disease pending culture results
Observation with serial ultrasound — reduced flow may resolve spontaneously

explanation: Ovarian torsion is a surgical emergency. The classic presentation — acute severe unilateral pelvic pain, nausea/vomiting, adnexal mass on ultrasound with reduced Doppler flow — warrants emergent laparoscopy.
