Delay in surgical intervention causes progressive ischemia and irreversible ovarian necrosis, risking loss of the ovary. Laparoscopic detorsion (untwisting the ovary) is the preferred approach, with conservation of the ovary if it appears viable (many discolored-appearing ovaries recover function after detorsion). Salpingo-oophorectomy is reserved for clearly necrotic tissue. CT scanning delays treatment. Antibiotics treat infection, not torsion. Observation risks permanent ovarian loss.
pearl: Ovarian torsion: most common with ovarian mass >5 cm. Doppler can be normal — do not exclude torsion based on preserved flow. Treat: emergent laparoscopy, detorsion + ovarian conservation when viable. Time is critical: <6 hours = best chance of viability. Young patients: ovary conservation is a priority.

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: Cervical cancer — HPV 16/18 and squamocolumnar junction transformation zone pathogenesis
usmleContentArea: Female and Transgender Reproductive System & Breast
physicianTask: Medical Knowledge: Applying Foundational Science Concepts
memoryAnchor: Cervical cancer: HPV 16 (squamous) and 18 (adenocarcinoma) most carcinogenic. Transformation zone (squamocolumnar junction) is most vulnerable — metaplastic squamous epithelium replaces columnar epithelium, susceptible to HPV integration. E6 (inhibits p53) + E7 (inhibits Rb) = key oncoproteins. Gardasil covers 16, 18, 6, 11.
commonTrap: Students say HPV causes cancer immediately — HPV integrates viral DNA, causing CIN (cervical intraepithelial neoplasia). CIN progresses to invasive cancer over 10–20 years. High-risk HPV types integrate E6 (p53 inhibitor) and E7 (Rb inhibitor) into host genome.
stem: A 32-year-old woman has a Pap smear showing high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion (HSIL). HPV co-testing is positive for HPV type 16. Colposcopy with biopsy shows CIN 3 at the transformation zone. Which molecular mechanism most directly explains HPV 16's carcinogenic potential?

options.text: E6 protein degrades p53 and E7 protein inactivates Rb, preventing cell cycle arrest and apoptosis
HPV surface capsid proteins directly bind and inhibit DNA repair enzymes
HPV LI protein acts as a dominant oncogene upregulating VEGF-mediated angiogenesis
HPV integrates into the BRCA1 locus, causing genomic instability specifically in cervical cells

explanation: High-risk HPV types (especially 16 and 18) encode two key oncoproteins: E6 and E7. The E6 protein binds and targets p53 for ubiquitin-mediated proteasomal degradation — eliminating the cell's primary tumor suppressor and apoptosis regulator. The E7 protein binds and inactivates Rb (retinoblastoma protein), releasing transcription factor E2F and driving uncontrolled cell cycle progression from G1 into S phase. Together, E6 and E7 disable both the p53 apoptosis pathway and the Rb cell cycle checkpoint, allowing accumulation of additional mutations. Integration of HPV DNA (not episomal) into the host genome is associated with progression from CIN to invasive cancer. Transformation zone metaplastic cells are particularly susceptible to HPV integration.
pearl: HPV high-risk oncoproteins: E6 → p53 degradation (bypasses apoptosis); E7 → Rb inactivation (bypasses G1/S checkpoint). Transformation zone: squamocolumnar junction, most vulnerable. CIN 1-2-3 → invasive ca over 10-20 years. Prevention: Gardasil 9 (HPV 6,11,16,18,31,33,45,52,58). Screen: Pap ± HPV co-test every 3–5 years.

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: CML — BCR-ABL fusion oncogene and imatinib tyrosine kinase inhibitor mechanism
physicianTask: Medical Knowledge: Applying Foundational Science Concepts
memoryAnchor: CML: t(9;22) Philadelphia chromosome → BCR-ABL fusion → constitutively active tyrosine kinase → uncontrolled myeloid proliferation. Imatinib (Gleevec): competitive ATP-site inhibitor of BCR-ABL. Blast crisis = transformation to AML or ALL (worst prognosis).
commonTrap: Students confuse CML with CLL — CML is a MYELOID proliferation (mature granulocytes, basophilia, splenomegaly, low LAP score), median age 50–60. CLL is a LYMPHOID (mature B cells, smudge cells, isolated lymphocytosis, older patients). Philadelphia chromosome = CML (and some ALL).
stem: A 52-year-old man has fatigue and early satiety. WBC 145,000/µL with a differential showing predominantly mature neutrophils, bands, metamyelocytes, myelocytes, eosinophilia, and basophilia. Platelets 620,000. Spleen is massively enlarged. LAP score is very low. Cytogenetics shows t(9;22) in 95% of metaphases. Which targeted therapy and its mechanism are most appropriate?

options.text: Imatinib — competitive inhibitor of the BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase ATP-binding site
Rituximab — anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody depleting malignant B cells
All-trans retinoic acid (ATRA) — promotes differentiation of malignant promyelocytes
Hydroxyurea — DNA synthesis inhibitor for cytoreduction only, without targeted activity

explanation: The t(9;22) Philadelphia chromosome creates the BCR-ABL fusion gene, encoding a constitutively active tyrosine kinase that drives autonomous myeloid proliferation. Imatinib (a TKI) competitively inhibits the ATP-binding site of the BCR-ABL kinase, preventing substrate phosphorylation and blocking the proliferative signal. Imatinib achieves complete cytogenetic remission in >80% of CML chronic phase patients and is the first example of rationally designed targeted oncology therapy. Rituximab targets CD20 on B cells (used in CLL, lymphoma — not CML). ATRA is used in acute promyelocytic leukemia (APL/AML-M3 with t(15;17), PML-RARA fusion). Hydroxyurea is cytoreductive but non-targeted.
pearl: CML: t(9;22) Philadelphia chromosome → BCR-ABL → tyrosine kinase overactivity. Imatinib: ATP-site competitive inhibitor. Phases: chronic → accelerated → blast crisis (AML/ALL transformation). BCR-ABL resistance (T315I gating mutation) → ponatinib. CML without Ph chromosome is rare and has worse prognosis.

optionExplanations.A: BCR-ABL encodes a constitutively active tyrosine kinase that continuously signals through RAS/MAPK and PI3K/AKT pathways, driving myeloid cell proliferation and survival. Imatinib occupies the ATP-binding pocket of BCR-ABL in an inactive conformation, preventing ATP from providing phosphate groups for substrate phosphorylation. Without phosphorylation signals, the proliferative cascade is blocked. Imatinib also inhibits c-KIT and PDGFR-beta, explaining its utility in gastrointestinal stromal tumors (GISTs). Second-generation TKIs (dasatinib, nilotinib) overcome many imatinib-resistant mutations; the T315I "gatekeeper" mutation requires third-generation ponatinib.
optionExplanations.B: Rituximab is a chimeric monoclonal antibody against CD20, a surface antigen expressed on mature B cells and most B cell lymphomas. CML is a myeloid malignancy — the malignant clone expresses myeloid markers (CD13, CD33, myeloperoxidase). CD20 is not expressed on granulocytes or their precursors. Rituximab is the backbone of therapy for CLL (CD20+ B cell malignancy), non-Hodgkin lymphoma, and follicular lymphoma — not CML.
optionExplanations.C: All-trans retinoic acid (ATRA) is specifically used in acute promyelocytic leukemia (APL, AML-M3), which is caused by the t(15;17) translocation creating PML-RARA fusion. This fusion blocks promyelocyte differentiation at the promyelocyte stage. ATRA binds and disrupts the PML-RARA fusion protein, restoring normal differentiation signaling and causing malignant promyelocytes to mature into normal granulocytes. CML has none of the t(15;17) biology — ATRA would have no mechanism of action in BCR-ABL-driven CML.
optionExplanations.D: Hydroxyurea inhibits ribonucleotide reductase (the enzyme converting ribonucleotides to deoxyribonucleotides), broadly suppressing DNA synthesis and myeloid proliferation. It is used for rapid cytoreduction (WBC control) in CML while awaiting TKI titration, and for symptom management in myeloproliferative disorders. However, hydroxyurea does not address the underlying BCR-ABL driver, does not achieve cytogenetic remission, and does not alter the natural history of CML progression to blast crisis. TKIs are the definitive targeted treatment.

subject: Pharmacology
testedConcept: Warfarin mechanism and vitamin K reversal — factor synthesis inhibition and antidote
physicianTask: Patient Care: Pharmacotherapy
memoryAnchor: Warfarin: inhibits vitamin K epoxide reductase → cannot recycle vitamin K → ↓synthesis of factors II, VII, IX, X, protein C, protein S. ↑PT/INR. Reversal: stop warfarin + vitamin K (slow) + FFP (immediate factors) + 4-factor PCC (fastest). Factor VII shortest half-life → INR rises first.
commonTrap: Students give FFP for all warfarin reversal — for non-urgent reversal, vitamin K (oral or IV) alone is sufficient. For urgent/life-threatening bleeding, 4-factor prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC) reverses INR fastest and with less volume than FFP.
stem: A 74-year-old woman on warfarin for atrial fibrillation presents with acute intracranial hemorrhage after falling. INR is 3.8. She is awake but hemiparetic. Which is the most appropriate immediate warfarin reversal strategy?

options.text: 4-factor prothrombin complex concentrate (4F-PCC) plus vitamin K intravenous
Vitamin K 10 mg oral alone — oral vitamin K reverses warfarin within 24 hours
Fresh frozen plasma (FFP) alone — FFP replaces all clotting factors immediately
Protamine sulfate — reverses warfarin by binding the drug directly

explanation: 4-factor prothrombin complex concentrate (4F-PCC) is the fastest and most effective reversal agent for life-threatening warfarin-related bleeding. 4F-PCC contains concentrated factors II, VII, IX, and X (plus protein C and S) and reverses INR within minutes, with a fixed small volume (avoiding fluid overload of FFP). Vitamin K IV is co-administered to sustain reversal by restoring endogenous factor synthesis (takes hours to days). Oral vitamin K alone takes 12–24 hours — unacceptable for intracranial hemorrhage. FFP provides all clotting factors but requires large volumes (often 10–15 mL/kg) and takes 30–60 minutes to infuse. Protamine reverses heparin (by ionic interaction), not warfarin — warfarin has no direct antidote via ionic binding.
pearl: Warfarin reversal for life-threatening bleeding: 4F-PCC (fastest, concentrated) + vitamin K IV (sustains reversal). FFP: if 4F-PCC unavailable. Oral vitamin K: non-urgent, supratherapeutic INR without bleeding. Andexanet alfa reverses apixaban/rivaroxaban. Idarucizumab reverses dabigatran.

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: Hodgkin lymphoma — Reed-Sternberg cells and EBV association
physicianTask: Patient Care: Diagnosis
memoryAnchor: Hodgkin lymphoma: Reed-Sternberg cells (owl-eye cells, CD30+/CD15+, CD45-). Bimodal age distribution (15-35 and >55). B symptoms: fever >38°C, drenching night sweats, >10% weight loss. EBV association (especially mixed cellularity). Excellent prognosis with ABVD (doxorubicin, bleomycin, vinblastine, dacarbazine).
commonTrap: Students confuse Reed-Sternberg cells with non-Hodgkin lymphoma — Reed-Sternberg cells are the pathognomonic malignant cells of Hodgkin lymphoma. They are CD30+ and CD15+ (Hodgkin markers) but CD45- (leukocyte common antigen, which is positive on virtually all other lymphomas and leukemias).
stem: A 24-year-old man has 3 months of painless cervical lymphadenopathy, drenching night sweats, 12-lb weight loss, and low-grade fevers. CT chest shows mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Excisional lymph node biopsy shows a cellular background of eosinophils, lymphocytes, and plasma cells with large cells having large bilobed nuclei with prominent eosinophilic nucleoli, described as "owl eyes." These cells are CD30+ and CD15+ but CD45-. Which diagnosis and preferred initial treatment are correct?

options.text: Hodgkin lymphoma — treat with ABVD (doxorubicin, bleomycin, vinblastine, dacarbazine)
Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma — treat with R-CHOP (rituximab, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, prednisone)
Anaplastic large cell lymphoma — treat with CHOP plus brentuximab vedotin
Burkitt lymphoma — treat with high-intensity chemotherapy with rituximab

explanation: Hodgkin lymphoma is diagnosed by the pathognomonic Reed-Sternberg (RS) cells — large binucleated cells with prominent eosinophilic nucleoli ("owl eyes") — within a reactive inflammatory background. The immunophenotype (CD30+, CD15+, CD45-) is diagnostic. Bimodal age distribution with peak in young adults, painless mediastinal/cervical lymphadenopathy, and B symptoms (fever, night sweats, >10% weight loss) are classic. ABVD is standard first-line chemotherapy for most stages of classical Hodgkin lymphoma, achieving 80–90% cure rates. R-CHOP treats non-Hodgkin B-cell lymphomas (DLBCL). Burkitt lymphoma has mature B-cell immunophenotype (CD20+, sIg+, TdT-, Ki-67 nearly 100%). Anaplastic large cell lymphoma is CD30+ but lacks RS cell morphology and CD15.
pearl: Hodgkin lymphoma: Reed-Sternberg cells (CD30+/CD15+, CD45-). Bimodal: ages 15-35 and >55. B symptoms: fever/nights sweats/>10% weight loss. ABVD first-line. Excellent prognosis (80-90% cure). EBV association especially mixed cellularity subtype. Brentuximab vedotin: anti-CD30 for relapsed/refractory.

subject: Pharmacology
testedConcept: Hypothyroidism — levothyroxine dosing and T4 to T3 peripheral conversion
physicianTask: Patient Care: Pharmacotherapy
memoryAnchor: Levothyroxine (T4): takes 4-6 weeks to reach steady state. Adjust dose based on TSH (target 0.5-2.5 mIU/L). T4→T3 by 5-deiodinase in peripheral tissues. Take on empty stomach (absorption reduced by Ca, Fe, antacids). Start low-dose in elderly and CAD patients (avoid precipitating angina).
commonTrap: Students wonder why we give T4 when T3 is the active hormone — T4 (levothyroxine) is preferred because it has a longer half-life (~7 days), more stable serum levels, and undergoes peripheral conversion to T3 by 5'-deiodinase in target tissues. This allows tissue-specific regulation of T3 activity.
stem: A 45-year-old woman with Hashimoto thyroiditis has TSH 18 mIU/L (elevated) and free T4 0.6 ng/dL (low). She has fatigue, cold intolerance, constipation, and dry skin. She is started on levothyroxine 75 µg daily. Which best explains why T4 (levothyroxine) is preferred over T3 (liothyronine) for long-term hypothyroid replacement?

options.text: Levothyroxine has a longer half-life (~7 days) and undergoes peripheral conversion to T3 providing stable tissue-level thyroid hormone
T4 has greater potency per microgram than T3 at the nuclear thyroid hormone receptor
T4 avoids interaction with thyroid peroxidase antibodies that would destroy exogenous T3
T4 is the only form of thyroid hormone that crosses the blood-brain barrier to restore cognitive function

explanation: Levothyroxine (T4) is preferred for hypothyroid replacement because of its long half-life (approximately 7 days), which allows once-daily dosing and produces stable serum levels without the peaks and troughs seen with T3 (liothyronine, half-life 1 day). T4 is converted to the biologically active T3 by 5'-deiodinase in peripheral tissues (liver, kidney, muscle), providing tissue-specific regulation of thyroid hormone activity. This conversion allows tissues to modulate local T3 availability. T3 is more potent per molecule at the nuclear receptor, but the unstable pharmacokinetics (short half-life, peaks causing palpitations and anxiety) make it unsuitable for primary replacement. Monitor TSH every 6–8 weeks after dose adjustments; steady state requires 4–6 weeks.
pearl: Levothyroxine (T4): half-life 7 days, once daily, stable levels, converted to T3 peripherally by 5-deiodinase. Target TSH 0.5-2.5 mIU/L. Take on empty stomach (Ca, Fe, antacids reduce absorption). Start low in elderly/CAD. T3/T4 combination: occasionally used in persistent symptoms despite normal TSH.

subject: Pharmacology
testedConcept: Exogenous Cushing syndrome — chronic glucocorticoid suppression of CRH and ACTH
physicianTask: Patient Care: Diagnosis
memoryAnchor: Exogenous glucocorticoids are the most common cause of Cushing syndrome. Chronic exposure suppresses CRH and ACTH and can cause adrenal cortical atrophy.
commonTrap: Students don't distinguish Cushing disease from Cushing syndrome — Cushing syndrome = any cause of hypercortisolism (exogenous, adrenal, pituitary, ectopic). Cushing DISEASE = specifically pituitary ACTH-secreting adenoma (Cushing syndrome caused by pituitary). Exogenous steroids = most common cause of Cushing syndrome overall.
stem: A 38-year-old woman with rheumatoid arthritis has taken prednisone 20 mg daily for 6 months. She develops central weight gain, facial rounding, proximal muscle weakness, purple abdominal striae, and hyperglycemia. Morning endogenous cortisol and ACTH are both low. Which diagnosis and mechanism best explain these findings?

options.text: Exogenous Cushing syndrome — prednisone suppresses hypothalamic CRH and pituitary ACTH
Pituitary Cushing disease — ACTH-secreting adenoma with partial dexamethasone suppression
Adrenal adenoma — autonomous cortisol secretion suppresses ACTH while endogenous cortisol remains elevated
Ectopic ACTH syndrome — small cell lung cancer producing autonomous ACTH

explanation: This patient has exogenous Cushing syndrome from chronic supraphysiologic prednisone. Exogenous glucocorticoids activate glucocorticoid receptors in the hypothalamus and pituitary, suppressing CRH and ACTH secretion. Reduced ACTH stimulation lowers endogenous adrenal cortisol production and can produce bilateral adrenal cortical atrophy. A medication history should identify this iatrogenic cause before biochemical testing for endogenous Cushing syndrome. Prednisone must be tapered rather than stopped abruptly because the suppressed HPA axis may not mount an adequate stress response.
pearl: Exclude exogenous glucocorticoid exposure before testing for endogenous Cushing syndrome. Chronic therapy suppresses CRH, ACTH, and endogenous cortisol and creates adrenal-insufficiency risk during withdrawal.

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: Diabetes insipidus — central vs nephrogenic and water deprivation test with desmopressin
physicianTask: Patient Care: Diagnosis
memoryAnchor: DI: polyuria + polydipsia + dilute urine (urine osm <300). Central DI: ↓ADH (post-surgical, traumatic, idiopathic). Nephrogenic DI: ADH present but kidney unresponsive (lithium, hypercalcemia, hypokalemia, V2R mutation). Water deprivation: urine fails to concentrate in both. Desmopressin (synthetic ADH): concentrates urine in central DI only, not nephrogenic.
commonTrap: Students confuse DI with primary polydipsia — in primary polydipsia (compulsive water drinking), urine will concentrate appropriately after water deprivation (no ADH or renal defect). In DI, urine does not concentrate after water deprivation. Serum sodium is typically low-normal in primary polydipsia, hypernatremic in DI.
stem: A 28-year-old man 2 weeks after transsphenoidal surgery for a pituitary adenoma has polyuria (8 L/day) and polydipsia. Serum Na 148 mEq/L, plasma osmolality 305 mOsm/kg, urine osmolality 85 mOsm/kg. After 8 hours of water deprivation, urine osmolality remains 95 mOsm/kg. After desmopressin administration, urine osmolality rises to 680 mOsm/kg within 2 hours. Which type of diabetes insipidus is present?

options.text: Central diabetes insipidus — posterior pituitary ADH secretion impaired after surgery; desmopressin restores urinary concentration
Nephrogenic diabetes insipidus — collecting duct V2 receptor or aquaporin-2 unresponsive to ADH; desmopressin has no effect
Primary polydipsia — excessive water intake suppresses ADH; water deprivation would concentrate urine
SIADH — syndrome of inappropriate ADH causing water retention and concentrated urine

explanation: Central diabetes insipidus results from inadequate ADH (vasopressin) secretion by the posterior pituitary — common after transsphenoidal pituitary surgery (which can damage the posterior pituitary or pituitary stalk). The water deprivation test confirms the diagnosis: both central and nephrogenic DI fail to concentrate urine with water deprivation. The definitive distinction is the desmopressin test: in central DI, exogenous desmopressin (synthetic ADH) activates intact V2 receptors on the collecting duct, triggering aquaporin-2 insertion and robust urinary concentration (680 mOsm/kg here). In nephrogenic DI, the collecting duct cannot respond to desmopressin — urine osmolality remains low despite the drug. The marked response to desmopressin confirms central DI.
pearl: Water deprivation test: both central and nephrogenic DI fail to concentrate urine. Desmopressin test: Central DI = concentrates urine (intact renal V2R). Nephrogenic DI = no response (V2R/AQP2 defect). Central DI causes: post-surgery, trauma, tumors, autoimmune, idiopathic. Treat central DI: desmopressin nasal spray/oral.

subject: Pharmacology
testedConcept: H. pylori eradication — universal test of cure after guideline-concordant therapy
physicianTask: Patient Care: Pharmacotherapy
memoryAnchor: H. pylori requires a test of cure at least 4 weeks after therapy. Hold PPIs for 2 weeks and antibiotics or bismuth for 4 weeks before testing.
commonTrap: Students forget to verify eradication after treatment — H. pylori treatment failure or reinfection requires retesting. Serology (IgG antibodies) cannot confirm eradication (remains positive after cure). Only urea breath test, stool antigen test, or repeat endoscopy with biopsies confirm eradication.
stem: A 44-year-old man with an H. pylori-positive duodenal ulcer completes 14 days of optimized bismuth quadruple therapy. His symptoms resolve. What should be done at least 4 weeks after completing therapy?

options.text: Urea breath test or stool H. pylori antigen test to confirm eradication
Repeat serology (H. pylori IgG) to confirm antibody levels have fallen to zero
Repeat endoscopy with biopsy for all patients after H. pylori treatment regardless of symptoms
No follow-up testing required — triple therapy achieves >95% eradication and failure is rare

explanation: All treated patients should undergo confirmation of H. pylori eradication at least 4 weeks after antibiotics are completed. A urea breath test or monoclonal stool antigen test detects active infection; serology cannot distinguish previous from persistent infection. To reduce false-negative results, PPIs should be held for 2 weeks and antibiotics and bismuth for 4 weeks before testing. Empiric clarithromycin-containing therapy should be avoided unless susceptibility is documented.
pearl: Confirm H. pylori eradication with urea breath testing or stool antigen at least 4 weeks after therapy. Do not use serology for test of cure, and avoid clarithromycin regimens without demonstrated susceptibility.

subject: Anatomy
testedConcept: Carpal tunnel syndrome — median nerve compression at flexor retinaculum
physicianTask: Patient Care: Diagnosis
memoryAnchor: CTS: median nerve at carpal tunnel (flexor retinaculum). Thenar wasting + paresthesias in lateral 3.5 digits + worse at night. LOAF: Lumbricals (1st and 2nd), Opponens pollicis, Abductor pollicis brevis, Flexor pollicis brevis. Phalen test + Tinel sign. Treat: wrist splint → steroid injection → surgical release.
commonTrap: Students say ulnar nerve causes CTS — CTS is the MEDIAN nerve. The ulnar nerve passes behind the medial epicondyle (cubital tunnel) and causes numbness in the 4th and 5th fingers with intrinsic hand weakness (claw hand). Median nerve: lateral 3.5 fingers, thenar eminence (LOAF muscles). Remember: the "median" nerve serves the "median" (central) three and a half fingers.
stem: A 38-year-old pregnant woman has 6 weeks of burning pain and paresthesias in the thumb, index, and middle fingers and the radial half of the ring finger, worse at night. She has difficulty buttoning clothes. Exam shows Tinel sign positive at the volar wrist and a positive Phalen test. Which nerve is compressed and which specific muscles are weak?

options.text: Median nerve — abductor pollicis brevis and opponens pollicis are weak (thenar atrophy)
Ulnar nerve — hypothenar muscles and intrinsic interossei are weak
Radial nerve — extensor carpi radialis and wrist extensors are weak
Anterior interosseous nerve — flexor pollicis longus and FDP to index finger are weak

explanation: Carpal tunnel syndrome is caused by compression of the median nerve under the flexor retinaculum at the wrist. The median nerve innervates the lateral 3.5 fingers (thumb, index, middle, and radial half of ring finger) through its digital branches, and provides motor innervation to the LOAF muscles of the thenar eminence: Lumbricals 1 and 2, Opponens pollicis, Abductor pollicis brevis, and Flexor pollicis brevis. The most consistently affected muscle is abductor pollicis brevis — tested by asking the patient to abduct the thumb perpendicular to the palm. Thenar wasting is visible in severe cases. Pregnancy increases carpal tunnel incidence due to fluid retention and median nerve edema. Tinel (tapping over carpal tunnel reproduces paresthesias) and Phalen (wrist flexion for 60s reproduces symptoms) are provocative tests.
pearl: CTS: median nerve compression under flexor retinaculum. Lateral 3.5 fingers numb + thenar (LOAF) weakness. Pregnancy, hypothyroidism, diabetes, amyloid, RA increase risk. EMG/NCS confirms. Treat: neutral wrist splint especially at night, corticosteroid injection, surgical carpal tunnel release for refractory cases.

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: Osteosarcoma — metaphyseal location, Codman triangle, and sunburst pattern
physicianTask: Patient Care: Diagnosis
memoryAnchor: Osteosarcoma: most common primary malignant bone tumor, age 10-20, metaphysis of distal femur/proximal tibia. Codman triangle (periosteal elevation) + sunburst pattern on X-ray. ↑ALP. Li-Fraumeni (p53) + retinoblastoma (Rb) mutation associations. Treat: neoadjuvant chemo + surgery. Ewing sarcoma: diaphysis, onion-skin periosteal reaction, t(11;22).
commonTrap: Students diagnose Ewing sarcoma when they see a bone tumor in a child — Ewing and osteosarcoma are both adolescent bone tumors. Key distinction: osteosarcoma = METAPHYSIS + Codman triangle + sunburst. Ewing = DIAPHYSIS + onion-skin periosteal reaction + t(11;22) EWS-FLI1 fusion + 'looks like osteomyelitis' (fever, elevated WBC, pain).
stem: A 16-year-old boy has 3 months of worsening pain and swelling around the right knee. He denies fever. ALP is 680 U/L (elevated). X-ray of the distal femur shows an aggressive metaphyseal lesion with periosteal elevation creating a triangular opacity (Codman triangle) and spiculated "sunburst" pattern of new bone formation extending into surrounding soft tissue. Biopsy confirms malignant spindle cells producing osteoid. Which diagnosis is confirmed?

options.text: Osteosarcoma — metaphyseal location, Codman triangle, sunburst pattern, osteoid-producing malignant cells
Ewing sarcoma — small round blue cell tumor with t(11;22) involving the diaphysis
Chondrosarcoma — cartilage-producing malignant cells with calcification matrix
Giant cell tumor — epiphyseal location with soap-bubble appearance, benign behavior

explanation: Osteosarcoma is the most common primary malignant bone tumor, peaking in adolescents (10–20 years) during rapid bone growth. The classic location is the metaphysis of long bones, especially the distal femur (50%), proximal tibia, and proximal humerus. The Codman triangle forms when the aggressive tumor elevates the periosteum, creating a triangular area of new bone at the margin of periosteal elevation. The sunburst pattern represents perpendicular new bone spicules formed as the tumor breaches the cortex. Malignant cells produce osteoid (immature bone matrix) — the histologic hallmark. Elevated ALP reflects high bone turnover. Treatment: neoadjuvant chemotherapy (MAP protocol: methotrexate, doxorubicin, cisplatin) followed by limb-salvage surgery.
pearl: Osteosarcoma: adolescent + metaphysis + Codman triangle + sunburst pattern + ↑ALP + osteoid production. Bimodal: adolescents (sporadic) + elderly (Paget disease, radiation-associated). Treatment: neoadjuvant chemo (MAP) + limb-salvage surgery. Ewing sarcoma: diaphysis + onion-skin + t(11;22) + small round blue cells. Prognosis: 5-year survival ~70% with treatment.

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: Melanoma — asymmetry, BRAF V600E mutation, and sentinel lymph node biopsy
physicianTask: Patient Care: Diagnosis
memoryAnchor: Melanoma ABCDEs: Asymmetry, Border (irregular), Color (multiple), Diameter (>6mm), Evolution. Breslow thickness = most important prognostic factor (vertical invasion depth). BRAF V600E (50%) = targeted therapy (vemurafenib, dabrafenib). Sentinel lymph node biopsy for Breslow >0.8 mm. Sun exposure + dysplastic nevi = risk.
commonTrap: Students say Breslow thickness is the same as Clark level — Clark level describes anatomic invasion (epidermis→dermis layers→subcutaneous), while Breslow thickness measures the actual depth in millimeters. Breslow thickness is the preferred prognostic tool in current AJCC staging, having replaced Clark level for most clinical decisions.
stem: A 47-year-old man has a 1.4-cm pigmented skin lesion on his back with irregular borders, multiple shades of brown and black, and a raised nodular area. Excisional biopsy confirms melanoma with Breslow thickness of 2.2 mm and no ulceration. Lymph nodes are clinically negative on exam. Which is the most appropriate next step in staging and management?

options.text: Sentinel lymph node biopsy to evaluate regional nodal status
Wide local excision with 2-cm margins only — no further staging needed for intermediate-thickness melanoma
CT-PET scan of the entire body before sentinel lymph node biopsy
Immediate systemic immunotherapy (pembrolizumab) without surgical staging

explanation: Sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB) is recommended for melanomas with Breslow thickness >0.8 mm (or >0.75 mm with adverse features) to evaluate regional nodal status. This patient has a Breslow thickness of 2.2 mm — SLNB is standard of care. The sentinel node (first draining node from the tumor) is identified using lymphoscintigraphy and blue dye, excised, and analyzed. If negative, no further lymph node dissection is required (SLNB has >95% negative predictive value). If positive, it provides critical staging information and may guide adjuvant therapy decisions. Wide local excision (2-cm margins for Breslow >2 mm) is performed simultaneously. CT-PET is not required before SLNB for clinically node-negative intermediate-thickness melanoma. Systemic therapy without surgical staging is premature.
pearl: Melanoma Breslow thickness: <0.8 mm → WLE only. >0.8 mm → WLE + sentinel lymph node biopsy. >2 mm margins for >2 mm thick. BRAF V600E mutation (50%): vemurafenib + cobimetinib. Immunotherapy: pembrolizumab or ipilimumab+nivolumab for stage III/IV. Breslow most important prognostic factor. Annual full-skin exam + sun protection.

subject: Anatomy
testedConcept: needle decompression site — insert above the rib to avoid the intercostal neurovascular bundle
physicianTask: Patient Care: Clinical Interventions
memoryAnchor: Intercostal VAN runs along the inferior rib border. Enter the pleural space just above the rib below the intercostal space.
commonTrap: Students choose the lower edge of the rib above because it feels closer to the intercostal space, but the neurovascular bundle runs along that inferior rib margin.
stem: A 29-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after a motorcycle collision. He has severe dyspnea, hypotension, distended neck veins, tracheal deviation to the left, and absent breath sounds over the right hemithorax. Needle decompression is performed in the right second intercostal space at the midclavicular line. To minimize the risk of intercostal vessel injury, where should the needle be inserted?

options.text: Immediately superior to the upper border of the third rib
Immediately inferior to the lower border of the second rib
Directly through the center of the second intercostal space
At the costochondral junction to avoid the intercostal bundle

explanation: This patient has tension pneumothorax: hypotension, distended neck veins, tracheal deviation away from the affected side, and absent unilateral breath sounds. Needle decompression can be performed at the second intercostal space in the midclavicular line. The intercostal vein, artery, and nerve run in the costal groove along the inferior border of each rib, so pleural access should be made just superior to the upper border of the rib below the intercostal space. In the second intercostal space, that means entering immediately above the third rib.
pearl: Intercostal VAN runs under each rib. Procedures through an intercostal space go over the rib below, not under the rib above.

optionExplanations.A: Correct. The intercostal neurovascular bundle runs along the inferior border of each rib, so inserting just above the rib below the intercostal space avoids the bundle.
optionExplanations.B: Wrong. The lower border of the second rib is where the intercostal vein, artery, and nerve travel, so this approach increases the risk of vascular or nerve injury.
optionExplanations.C: Wrong. The center of the intercostal space is less precise and can still place the needle near the neurovascular bundle; the safe landmark is just superior to the rib below.
optionExplanations.D: Wrong. The costochondral junction is not the safety principle being tested. The key anatomic relationship is the neurovascular bundle along the inferior rib border.

subject: Genetics
testedConcept: Knudson two-hit hypothesis for retinoblastoma tumor suppression
physicianTask: Medical Knowledge: Applying Foundational Science Concepts
memoryAnchor: Tumor suppressor genes usually require loss of both alleles; oncogenes usually require activation of one allele.
commonTrap: Proto-oncogene activation is a gain-of-function mechanism and does not explain inherited bilateral retinoblastoma.
stem: A 2-year-old boy has leukocoria and is found to have bilateral retinal tumors. Genetic testing shows one inherited RB1 pathogenic variant and loss of the remaining normal allele in tumor cells. Which mechanism best explains the development of his tumors?

options.text: Loss of both tumor suppressor alleles
Activation of one proto-oncogene allele
Expansion of a trinucleotide repeat
Mitochondrial heteroplasmy

explanation: The best answer is loss of both tumor suppressor alleles because RB1 follows the Knudson two-hit model: an inherited first hit plus somatic loss of the second allele permits retinal tumor formation. Clinical pearl: Tumor suppressor genes usually require loss of both alleles; oncogenes usually require activation of one allele. Key distinction: Proto-oncogene activation is a gain-of-function mechanism and does not explain inherited bilateral retinoblastoma.

optionExplanations.A: The best answer is loss of both tumor suppressor alleles because RB1 follows the Knudson two-hit model: an inherited first hit plus somatic loss of the second allele permits retinal tumor formation. Clinical pearl: Tumor suppressor genes usually require loss of both alleles; oncogenes usually require activation of one allele. Key distinction: Proto-oncogene activation is a gain-of-function mechanism and does not explain inherited bilateral retinoblastoma.
optionExplanations.B: Activation of one proto-oncogene allele is incorrect because it does not match the key mechanism: Knudson two-hit hypothesis for retinoblastoma tumor suppression. Proto-oncogene activation is a gain-of-function mechanism and does not explain inherited bilateral retinoblastoma.
optionExplanations.C: Expansion of a trinucleotide repeat is incorrect because it does not explain the clinical finding tested here: Knudson two-hit hypothesis for retinoblastoma tumor suppression.
optionExplanations.D: Mitochondrial heteroplasmy is incorrect because the vignette is best explained by Knudson two-hit hypothesis for retinoblastoma tumor suppression, not this distractor.

pearl: Tumor suppressor genes usually require loss of both alleles; oncogenes usually require activation of one allele.

subject: Genetics
testedConcept: Anticipation from trinucleotide repeat expansion in Huntington disease
physicianTask: Medical Knowledge: Applying Foundational Science Concepts
memoryAnchor: CAG repeat expansion in Huntington disease encodes an expanded polyglutamine tract.
commonTrap: Nondisjunction changes chromosome number, not repeat length.
stem: A 32-year-old man develops choreiform movements and personality changes. His father had similar symptoms beginning at age 48. Genetic testing shows an expanded CAG repeat. Which genetic principle explains the earlier onset in this patient?

options.text: Anticipation due to repeat expansion
Genomic imprinting from maternal silencing
Loss of heterozygosity in neurons
Nondisjunction during meiosis I

explanation: The best answer is anticipation due to repeat expansion because Huntington disease can show worsening severity or earlier onset in successive generations as CAG repeats expand. Clinical pearl: CAG repeat expansion in Huntington disease encodes an expanded polyglutamine tract. Key distinction: Nondisjunction changes chromosome number, not repeat length.

optionExplanations.A: The best answer is anticipation due to repeat expansion because Huntington disease can show worsening severity or earlier onset in successive generations as CAG repeats expand. Clinical pearl: CAG repeat expansion in Huntington disease encodes an expanded polyglutamine tract. Key distinction: Nondisjunction changes chromosome number, not repeat length.
optionExplanations.B: Genomic imprinting from maternal silencing is incorrect because it does not match the key mechanism: Anticipation from trinucleotide repeat expansion in Huntington disease. Nondisjunction changes chromosome number, not repeat length.
optionExplanations.C: Loss of heterozygosity in neurons is incorrect because it does not explain the clinical finding tested here: Anticipation from trinucleotide repeat expansion in Huntington disease.
optionExplanations.D: Nondisjunction during meiosis I is incorrect because the vignette is best explained by Anticipation from trinucleotide repeat expansion in Huntington disease, not this distractor.

pearl: CAG repeat expansion in Huntington disease encodes an expanded polyglutamine tract.

subject: Genetics
testedConcept: Autosomal recessive inheritance risk in sickle cell disease
physicianTask: Medical Knowledge: Applying Foundational Science Concepts
memoryAnchor: For two carriers of an autosomal recessive disease: 25% affected, 50% carrier, 25% unaffected noncarrier.
commonTrap: Carrier parents are typically clinically well, so the pattern can be missed if only symptoms are considered.
stem: A healthy 28-year-old woman and her 30-year-old husband have a child with sickle cell disease. They ask about the risk that their next child will also be affected. Which inheritance pattern and recurrence risk are most consistent with this disorder?

options.text: Autosomal recessive inheritance with a 25% affected risk
Autosomal dominant inheritance with a 50% affected risk
X-linked recessive inheritance with affected sons only
Mitochondrial inheritance with all children affected

explanation: The best answer is autosomal recessive inheritance with a 25% affected risk because two carrier parents have a one-in-four chance of having an affected child in each pregnancy. Clinical pearl: For two carriers of an autosomal recessive disease: 25% affected, 50% carrier, 25% unaffected noncarrier. Key distinction: Carrier parents are typically clinically well, so the pattern can be missed if only symptoms are considered.

optionExplanations.A: The best answer is autosomal recessive inheritance with a 25% affected risk because two carrier parents have a one-in-four chance of having an affected child in each pregnancy. Clinical pearl: For two carriers of an autosomal recessive disease: 25% affected, 50% carrier, 25% unaffected noncarrier. Key distinction: Carrier parents are typically clinically well, so the pattern can be missed if only symptoms are considered.
optionExplanations.B: Autosomal dominant inheritance with a 50% affected risk is incorrect because it does not match the key mechanism: Autosomal recessive inheritance risk in sickle cell disease. Carrier parents are typically clinically well, so the pattern can be missed if only symptoms are considered.
optionExplanations.C: X-linked recessive inheritance with affected sons only is incorrect because it does not explain the clinical finding tested here: Autosomal recessive inheritance risk in sickle cell disease.
optionExplanations.D: Mitochondrial inheritance with all children affected is incorrect because the vignette is best explained by Autosomal recessive inheritance risk in sickle cell disease, not this distractor.

pearl: For two carriers of an autosomal recessive disease: 25% affected, 50% carrier, 25% unaffected noncarrier.

subject: Genetics
testedConcept: Nondisjunction causing trisomy 21
physicianTask: Medical Knowledge: Applying Foundational Science Concepts
memoryAnchor: Trisomy 21 risk increases with maternal age due to meiotic nondisjunction.
commonTrap: Robertsonian translocation can cause Down syndrome, but nondisjunction is the most common mechanism.
stem: A newborn boy has hypotonia, a single palmar crease, upslanting palpebral fissures, and a complete atrioventricular canal defect. Karyotype shows 47,XY,+21. Which meiotic error most commonly causes this condition?

options.text: Maternal meiotic nondisjunction
Paternal mitochondrial mutation
Balanced reciprocal translocation only
Somatic loss of a tumor suppressor allele

explanation: The best answer is maternal meiotic nondisjunction because most cases of trisomy 21 result from failure of chromosome 21 separation during maternal meiosis. Clinical pearl: Trisomy 21 risk increases with maternal age due to meiotic nondisjunction. Key distinction: Robertsonian translocation can cause Down syndrome, but nondisjunction is the most common mechanism.

optionExplanations.A: The best answer is maternal meiotic nondisjunction because most cases of trisomy 21 result from failure of chromosome 21 separation during maternal meiosis. Clinical pearl: Trisomy 21 risk increases with maternal age due to meiotic nondisjunction. Key distinction: Robertsonian translocation can cause Down syndrome, but nondisjunction is the most common mechanism.
optionExplanations.B: Paternal mitochondrial mutation is incorrect because it does not match the key mechanism: Nondisjunction causing trisomy 21. Robertsonian translocation can cause Down syndrome, but nondisjunction is the most common mechanism.
optionExplanations.C: Balanced reciprocal translocation only is incorrect because it does not explain the clinical finding tested here: Nondisjunction causing trisomy 21.
optionExplanations.D: Somatic loss of a tumor suppressor allele is incorrect because the vignette is best explained by Nondisjunction causing trisomy 21, not this distractor.

pearl: Trisomy 21 risk increases with maternal age due to meiotic nondisjunction.

subject: Genetics
testedConcept: MEN2 RET proto-oncogene gain of function
physicianTask: Medical Knowledge: Applying Foundational Science Concepts

physicianTask: Medical Knowledge: Applying Foundational Science Concepts
memoryAnchor: Prions are proteinaceous infectious particles: beta-sheet-rich, protease-resistant, and capable of templating further misfolding.
commonTrap: Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease is not caused by a conventional virus and does not require nucleic acid replication.
stem: A 64-year-old man develops rapidly progressive dementia, myoclonus, gait ataxia, and akinetic mutism over 5 months. EEG shows periodic sharp-wave complexes, and MRI shows cortical ribboning. Which molecular event drives the underlying disease?

options.text: Templated conversion of normal alpha-helical prion protein into a beta-sheet-rich protease-resistant form
Insertion of viral DNA into cortical neuron chromosomes
Autoantibody-mediated destruction of postsynaptic acetylcholine receptors
Expansion of mitochondrial DNA repeats within oligodendrocytes

explanation: The best answer is templated prion-protein conversion because pathogenic PrP promotes misfolding of normal cellular PrP into beta-sheet-rich aggregates, causing neuronal loss, gliosis, and spongiform degeneration without nucleic acid. Clinical pearl: Prions are proteinaceous infectious particles: beta-sheet-rich, protease-resistant, and capable of templating further misfolding. Key distinction: Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease is not caused by a conventional virus and does not require nucleic acid replication.

optionExplanations.A: The best answer is templated prion-protein conversion because pathogenic PrP promotes misfolding of normal cellular PrP into beta-sheet-rich aggregates, causing neuronal loss, gliosis, and spongiform degeneration without nucleic acid. Clinical pearl: Prions are proteinaceous infectious particles: beta-sheet-rich, protease-resistant, and capable of templating further misfolding. Key distinction: Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease is not caused by a conventional virus and does not require nucleic acid replication.
optionExplanations.B: Insertion of viral DNA into cortical neuron chromosomes is incorrect because it does not match the key mechanism: Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease from beta-sheet-rich misfolded prion protein. Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease is not caused by a conventional virus and does not require nucleic acid replication.
optionExplanations.C: Autoantibody-mediated destruction of postsynaptic acetylcholine receptors is incorrect because it does not explain the clinical finding tested here: Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease from beta-sheet-rich misfolded prion protein.
optionExplanations.D: Expansion of mitochondrial DNA repeats within oligodendrocytes is incorrect because the vignette is best explained by Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease from beta-sheet-rich misfolded prion protein, not this distractor.

pearl: Prions are proteinaceous infectious particles: beta-sheet-rich, protease-resistant, and capable of templating further misfolding.

subject: Biochemistry
testedConcept: Periconceptional folic acid reducing neural tube defect risk
physicianTask: Patient Care: Health Maintenance and Disease Prevention
memoryAnchor: Folate prevention must begin before conception because the neural tube closes during the first weeks of development.
commonTrap: Starting supplementation only after pregnancy confirmation or an anatomy scan can miss the critical window for neural tube closure.
stem: A healthy 24-year-old woman plans to become pregnant within the next several months. She takes no medications and has no personal or family history of a neural tube defect. Which recommendation most directly reduces the risk of spina bifida and anencephaly in a future pregnancy?

options.text: Begin taking 400 micrograms of folic acid daily before conception
Wait until pregnancy is confirmed before starting folic acid
Take high-dose vitamin A throughout the first trimester
Begin folic acid only after the fetal anatomy ultrasound

explanation: The best answer is daily folic acid before conception because neural tube closure occurs early in embryogenesis, often before pregnancy is recognized. For a person at average risk, 400 micrograms daily before and during early pregnancy reduces the risk of spina bifida and anencephaly. Clinical pearl: Folate prevention must begin before conception because the neural tube closes during the first weeks of development. Key distinction: Starting supplementation only after pregnancy confirmation or an anatomy scan can miss the critical window for neural tube closure.

optionExplanations.B: Wait until pregnancy is confirmed before starting folic acid is incorrect because it does not match the key mechanism: Periconceptional folic acid reducing neural tube defect risk. subject: Microbiology
testedConcept: Preconception MMR vaccination preventing congenital rubella syndrome
physicianTask: Patient Care: Health Maintenance and Disease Prevention
memoryAnchor: Confirm rubella immunity before pregnancy; vaccinate susceptible patients before conception or postpartum, not during pregnancy.
commonTrap: MMR is contraindicated during pregnancy and is not effective as post-exposure prophylaxis for an already exposed pregnant patient.
stem: A 27-year-old woman is planning pregnancy. Prenatal records are unavailable, and rubella IgG testing shows that she is not immune. She is not currently pregnant. Which intervention is most appropriate to prevent congenital rubella syndrome?

options.text: Administer MMR vaccine now and avoid pregnancy for 4 weeks
Defer MMR vaccination until the second trimester
Give rubella immune globulin after conception as routine prophylaxis
Administer MMR immediately after any rubella exposure during pregnancy

explanation: The best answer is preconception MMR vaccination because rubella infection early in pregnancy can cause deafness, cataracts, cardiac defects, and other manifestations of congenital rubella syndrome. MMR is a live attenuated vaccine and should be given before pregnancy, followed by avoidance of pregnancy for 4 weeks. Clinical pearl: Confirm rubella immunity before pregnancy; vaccinate susceptible patients before conception or postpartum, not during pregnancy. Key distinction: MMR is contraindicated during pregnancy and is not effective as post-exposure prophylaxis for an already exposed pregnant patient.

commonTrap: A normal examination and absence of parental concern do not replace age-appropriate standardized screening.
stem: An 18-month-old boy is brought for a routine well-child visit. Growth and physical examination are normal, and his parents report no specific developmental concerns. In addition to ongoing developmental surveillance, which screening is recommended at this visit?

options.text: Standardized autism spectrum disorder screening
Formal intelligence quotient testing
Routine electroencephalography
Universal brain MRI screening

explanation: The best answer is standardized autism spectrum disorder screening because autism-specific screening is recommended for all children at the 18-month and 24-month well-child visits, even when parents have not raised a concern. Screening identifies children who need a more complete developmental evaluation; it does not itself establish the diagnosis. Clinical pearl: Developmental surveillance occurs at every visit; standardized general developmental and autism-specific screens occur at recommended ages. Key distinction: A normal examination and absence of parental concern do not replace age-appropriate standardized screening.

subject: Microbiology
testedConcept: Delayed or progressive sensorineural hearing loss after congenital CMV infection
physicianTask: Patient Care: Prognosis and Outcome
memoryAnchor: A normal newborn hearing screen does not eliminate future hearing risk in congenital CMV.
commonTrap: The characteristic auditory complication is sensorineural and may evolve over time; it is not a conductive ossicular disorder.
stem: A newborn has congenital cytomegalovirus infection confirmed within the first 3 weeks of life. The infant has no symptoms, and the newborn hearing screen is normal. The parents ask why continued follow-up is needed. Which long-term outcome remains an important concern?

options.text: Delayed-onset or progressive sensorineural hearing loss
Late development of hypertrophic pyloric stenosis
Progressive conductive hearing loss from ossicle fixation
Delayed closure of the ductus arteriosus in adolescence

explanation: The best answer is delayed-onset or progressive sensorineural hearing loss because congenital CMV can cause hearing loss that is absent on the newborn screen, appears later, affects the other ear, or worsens over time. Regular audiologic surveillance is therefore important even for an initially asymptomatic infant. Clinical pearl: A normal newborn hearing screen does not eliminate future hearing risk in congenital CMV. Key distinction: The characteristic auditory complication is sensorineural and may evolve over time; it is not a conductive ossicular disorder.

subject: Anatomy
testedConcept: Pulmonary hypoplasia determining prognosis in congenital diaphragmatic hernia
physicianTask: Patient Care: Prognosis and Outcome
memoryAnchor: In congenital diaphragmatic hernia, outcome tracks lung size, liver herniation, pulmonary hypoplasia, pulmonary hypertension, and associated anomalies.
commonTrap: The central problem is underdeveloped lungs and pulmonary vasculature, not an isolated surfactant deficiency in otherwise normally developed lungs.
stem: Prenatal ultrasonography shows a large left posterolateral diaphragmatic defect with herniation of abdominal viscera into the fetal thorax. The observed-to-expected lung-to-head ratio is markedly reduced, and the liver is positioned in the thorax. Which pathophysiologic feature most directly accounts for the poor prognosis?

options.text: Severe pulmonary hypoplasia with abnormal pulmonary vascular development
Isolated loss of surfactant production by mature type II pneumocytes
Compression of the recurrent laryngeal nerve causing airway obstruction
Failure of fetal hemoglobin to bind oxygen in the placenta

explanation: The best answer is severe pulmonary hypoplasia with abnormal pulmonary vascular development because thoracic compression in congenital diaphragmatic hernia impairs airway and vascular growth. The resulting small pulmonary vascular bed and pulmonary hypertension are major determinants of morbidity and mortality. Clinical pearl: In congenital diaphragmatic hernia, outcome tracks lung size, liver herniation, pulmonary hypoplasia, pulmonary hypertension, and associated anomalies. Key distinction: The central problem is underdeveloped lungs and pulmonary vasculature, not an isolated surfactant deficiency in otherwise normally developed lungs.

stem: The parents of an 11-year-old boy ask why human papillomavirus vaccination is recommended before he is sexually active. Which benefit best explains the recommendation?

options.text: Prevention of new HPV infections that can later cause penile, anal, and oropharyngeal cancers
Eradication of latent HPV already integrated into host cells
Prevention of all sexually transmitted infections after vaccination
Treatment of established genital warts and dysplastic lesions

explanation: The best answer is prevention of new oncogenic HPV infections because vaccination is most effective before exposure and protects against HPV types responsible for most HPV-associated cancers and genital warts. It is prophylactic and does not treat an established infection or lesion. Clinical pearl: HPV vaccination is cancer prevention for all adolescents and works best before viral exposure. Key distinction: The vaccine prevents new infection; it does not clear persistent HPV or treat existing warts, dysplasia, or cancer.

subject: Anatomy
testedConcept: Timely orchiopexy reducing long-term cryptorchidism complications
physicianTask: Patient Care: Health Maintenance and Disease Prevention
memoryAnchor: A testis still undescended at 6 months warrants surgical referral; orchiopexy should not be deferred until puberty.
commonTrap: Imaging usually does not change management of a palpable undescended testis, and hormonal therapy is not routine first-line treatment.
stem: A 6-month-old boy has a right testis that is palpable in the inguinal canal and cannot be brought into the scrotum. The left testis is normal. Which management best protects long-term reproductive health?

options.text: Refer for orchiopexy during infancy, with surgery completed by about 18 months
Observe until puberty because most testes descend after infancy
Begin long-term testosterone therapy to force testicular descent
Perform routine serial CT scans before considering surgery

explanation: The best answer is timely orchiopexy because spontaneous descent after 6 months is unlikely and persistent cryptorchidism increases risks of subfertility, testicular malignancy, torsion, and inguinal hernia. Early scrotal placement may reduce long-term risk and facilitates examination, although it does not eliminate malignancy risk. Clinical pearl: A testis still undescended at 6 months warrants surgical referral; orchiopexy should not be deferred until puberty. Key distinction: Imaging usually does not change management of a palpable undescended testis, and hormonal therapy is not routine first-line treatment.

physicianTask: Patient Care: Prognosis and Outcome

commonTrap: Laboratory testing does not determine viability and should not postpone urgent detorsion in a classic presentation.
stem: A 15-year-old boy develops abrupt severe left scrotal pain and vomiting 2 hours before arrival. Examination shows a high-riding horizontal testis and an absent cremasteric reflex. Which factor is most important for determining whether the affected testis can be salvaged?

options.text: Duration of ischemia before surgical detorsion
Serum testosterone concentration at presentation
Presence of microscopic hematuria
Patient height relative to age-matched peers

explanation: The best answer is duration of ischemia because testicular torsion compromises blood flow and salvage declines rapidly as treatment is delayed. A strongly suggestive presentation requires immediate urologic management, with the highest salvage likelihood when detorsion occurs within roughly 6 hours. Clinical pearl: Testicular torsion is a time-critical surgical emergency; do not delay definitive care when clinical suspicion is high. Key distinction: Laboratory testing does not determine viability and should not postpone urgent detorsion in a classic presentation.

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: High Gleason Grade Group predicting aggressive prostate cancer behavior
physicianTask: Patient Care: Prognosis and Outcome
memoryAnchor: For prostate adenocarcinoma, higher Gleason score and Grade Group indicate less differentiation and worse prognosis.
commonTrap: A total Gleason score of 9 is not low risk; the order of patterns adds nuance, but both patterns are high grade in this case.
stem: A 67-year-old man undergoes prostate biopsy after an elevated prostate-specific antigen level. Adenocarcinoma has a predominant Gleason pattern 4 and a secondary pattern 5, for a total score of 9. What does this histologic result most strongly imply?

options.text: A high-grade tumor with greater likelihood of rapid growth and spread
A benign proliferation with no metastatic potential
A low-grade tumor expected to remain indolent
A neuroendocrine tumor whose prognosis is unrelated to histology

explanation: The best answer is a high-grade aggressive tumor because Gleason 4 plus 5 equals 9, corresponding to Grade Group 5. Higher Gleason scores reflect poorer differentiation and are associated with a greater probability of growth, extension, and metastasis than lower-grade prostate adenocarcinoma. Clinical pearl: For prostate adenocarcinoma, higher Gleason score and Grade Group indicate less differentiation and worse prognosis. Key distinction: A total Gleason score of 9 is not low risk; the order of patterns adds nuance, but both patterns are high grade in this case.

stem: A 34-year-old man undergoes radical inguinal orchiectomy for pure seminoma. Alpha-fetoprotein is normal. Imaging shows retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy and pulmonary metastases. Which additional finding would change the tumor from a good-prognosis to an intermediate-prognosis seminoma?

options.text: Metastasis to the liver
An elevated beta-hCG concentration
An elevated lactate dehydrogenase concentration
Additional retroperitoneal lymph nodes

explanation: The best answer is liver metastasis because seminoma remains in the good-prognosis group when spread is limited to lymph nodes and lungs with a normal alpha-fetoprotein level. Nonpulmonary visceral metastasis, such as liver involvement, places seminoma in the intermediate-prognosis group; seminoma has no poor-prognosis category. Clinical pearl: Seminoma prognosis hinges on nonpulmonary visceral spread and a normal AFP; beta-hCG and LDH may be elevated. Key distinction: A true seminoma should not elevate AFP, while beta-hCG or LDH elevation alone does not create the intermediate-prognosis category.

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: Placenta previa presenting with painless third-trimester bleeding
physicianTask: Patient Care: History and Physical Examination
memoryAnchor: Painless late-pregnancy bleeding suggests placenta previa; painful bleeding with uterine tenderness suggests placental abruption.
commonTrap: Abruption usually produces abdominal or back pain, uterine tenderness or hypertonicity, and possible fetal compromise.
stem: A 32-year-old woman at 33 weeks of gestation has sudden bright-red vaginal bleeding without abdominal pain. Blood pressure and fetal heart rate are normal. The uterus is soft and nontender, and contractions are absent. Which diagnosis best matches this clinical pattern?

options.text: Placenta previa
Placental abruption
Uterine rupture
Intraamniotic infection

explanation: The best answer is placenta previa because placental tissue over or near the cervical opening classically causes painless bleeding in the second half of pregnancy with a soft, nontender uterus. Placental location should be assessed by ultrasonography before any digital cervical examination. Clinical pearl: Painless late-pregnancy bleeding suggests placenta previa; painful bleeding with uterine tenderness suggests placental abruption. Key distinction: Abruption usually produces abdominal or back pain, uterine tenderness or hypertonicity, and possible fetal compromise.

subject: Immunology
testedConcept: Rho(D) immune globulin preventing maternal Rh alloimmunization
physicianTask: Patient Care: Health Maintenance and Disease Prevention
memoryAnchor: Unsensitized Rh-negative patients generally receive Rh immune globulin at 28 weeks and after delivery of an Rh-positive newborn, as well as after sensitizing events.
commonTrap: Once a maternal anti-D immune response is established, prophylactic Rho(D) immune globulin cannot reverse it.
stem: A 28-week pregnant woman has type O, Rh-negative blood and a negative antibody screen. The biologic father is Rh positive. Which plan most effectively prevents maternal anti-D alloimmunization?

options.text: Give Rho(D) immune globulin now and again within 72 hours after delivery if the newborn is Rh positive
Wait until maternal anti-D antibodies appear before giving immune globulin
Give the newborn Rho(D) immune globulin after every delivery regardless of blood type
Administer maternal packed red cells at 28 weeks to dilute fetal erythrocytes

explanation: The best answer is antepartum and indicated postpartum Rho(D) immune globulin because passive anti-D clears fetal Rh-positive erythrocytes before the maternal immune system becomes sensitized. It is useful only before alloimmunization has occurred. Clinical pearl: Unsensitized Rh-negative patients generally receive Rh immune globulin at 28 weeks and after delivery of an Rh-positive newborn, as well as after sensitizing events. Key distinction: Once a maternal anti-D immune response is established, prophylactic Rho(D) immune globulin cannot reverse it.

Key distinction: Once a maternal anti-D immune response is established, prophylactic Rho(D) immune globulin cannot reverse it.

Once a maternal anti-D immune response is established, prophylactic Rho(D) immune globulin cannot reverse it.
optionExplanations.C: Give the newborn Rho(D) immune globulin after every delivery regardless of blood type is incorrect because it does not explain the clinical finding tested here: Rho(D) immune globulin preventing maternal Rh alloimmunization.
optionExplanations.D: Administer maternal packed red cells at 28 weeks to dilute fetal erythrocytes is incorrect because the vignette is best explained by Rho(D) immune globulin preventing maternal Rh alloimmunization, not this distractor.

pearl: Unsensitized Rh-negative patients generally receive Rh immune globulin at 28 weeks and after delivery of an Rh-positive newborn, as well as after sensitizing events.

subject: Pharmacology
testedConcept: Uterine atony causing postpartum hemorrhage and responding to uterine massage plus oxytocin
physicianTask: Patient Care: Clinical Interventions
memoryAnchor: For postpartum hemorrhage, think tone, trauma, tissue, and thrombin; a boggy uterus points first to deficient tone.
commonTrap: Normal lochia does not cause hemodynamic instability, and antibiotics alone do not correct atony-related blood loss.
stem: Minutes after a vaginal delivery, a 29-year-old woman develops heavy vaginal bleeding and tachycardia. The placenta appears intact. Examination shows an enlarged, boggy uterus. Which immediate intervention is most appropriate?

options.text: Bimanual uterine massage and oxytocin administration
Immediate broad-spectrum antibiotics without uterine treatment
Methotrexate to stop residual trophoblast proliferation
Expectant observation because postpartum bleeding is physiologic

explanation: The best answer is bimanual uterine massage and oxytocin because a boggy enlarged uterus after delivery indicates uterine atony, the most common cause of postpartum hemorrhage. Massage and a first-line uterotonic promote myometrial contraction and compression of placental-bed vessels while resuscitation proceeds. Clinical pearl: For postpartum hemorrhage, think tone, trauma, tissue, and thrombin; a boggy uterus points first to deficient tone. Key distinction: Normal lochia does not cause hemodynamic instability, and antibiotics alone do not correct atony-related blood loss.

subject: Endocrinology
testedConcept: Routine gestational diabetes screening at 24 to 28 weeks
physicianTask: Patient Care: Laboratory and Diagnostic Studies
memoryAnchor: Routine gestational diabetes screening occurs at 24 to 28 weeks, with earlier testing considered when risk factors for preexisting diabetes are present.
commonTrap: Amniocentesis and repeated genetic testing are not routine screening procedures in an otherwise uncomplicated pregnancy.
stem: A 30-year-old woman at 26 weeks of gestation has had an uncomplicated pregnancy. She has no known diabetes and early prenatal laboratory studies were normal. Which routine assessment is appropriate at this stage of pregnancy?

options.text: Screening for gestational diabetes with a glucose-based test
Repeat fetal karyotyping in every pregnancy
Routine maternal serum alpha-fetoprotein measurement every week until delivery
Diagnostic amniocentesis for fetal lung maturity

explanation: The best answer is glucose-based screening because pregnant patients without previously diagnosed diabetes are routinely assessed for gestational diabetes at 24 to 28 weeks. Identifying and controlling maternal hyperglycemia reduces complications such as excessive fetal growth, birth trauma, and neonatal hypoglycemia. Clinical pearl: Routine gestational diabetes screening occurs at 24 to 28 weeks, with earlier testing considered when risk factors for preexisting diabetes are present. Key distinction: Amniocentesis and repeated genetic testing are not routine screening procedures in an otherwise uncomplicated pregnancy.

subject: Microbiology
testedConcept: Suspected intraamniotic infection requiring intrapartum antibiotics without automatic cesarean delivery
physicianTask: Patient Care: Clinical Interventions
memoryAnchor: Suspected intraamniotic infection is treated during labor; route of delivery still follows obstetric indications.
commonTrap: Waiting for definitive culture exposes both mother and fetus to avoidable infectious morbidity, while automatic cesarean delivery adds surgical risk without treating the infection itself.
stem: A woman at 39 weeks of gestation has rupture of membranes followed 20 hours later by a temperature of 39.1 C, uterine tenderness, and a fetal heart rate of 175/min. Which management is most appropriate?

options.text: Begin intrapartum broad-spectrum antibiotics and continue delivery based on standard obstetric indications
Observe without antibiotics until an amniotic fluid culture confirms infection
Perform immediate cesarean delivery solely because infection is suspected
Give a single postpartum antibiotic dose and delay labor indefinitely

explanation: The best answer is intrapartum antibiotics with obstetrically appropriate delivery because maternal fever plus fetal tachycardia and uterine tenderness after prolonged membrane rupture strongly suggests intraamniotic infection. Treatment should not await culture confirmation, but infection alone is rarely an indication for cesarean delivery. Clinical pearl: Suspected intraamniotic infection is treated during labor; route of delivery still follows obstetric indications. Key distinction: Waiting for definitive culture exposes both mother and fetus to avoidable infectious morbidity, while automatic cesarean delivery adds surgical risk without treating the infection itself.

subject: Pharmacology
testedConcept: Thiamine administration during prolonged hyperemesis to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy
physicianTask: Patient Care: Pharmacotherapy
memoryAnchor: In prolonged hyperemesis requiring IV hydration, give thiamine before or with dextrose to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy.
commonTrap: Folate is important in pregnancy but does not prevent the acute encephalopathy caused by thiamine depletion during prolonged vomiting.
stem: A woman at 11 weeks of gestation is hospitalized after more than 3 weeks of persistent vomiting, weight loss, and dehydration. Intravenous fluids containing dextrose are planned. Which supplement should be administered with initial rehydration to prevent a serious neurologic complication?

options.text: Thiamine
Vitamin K
Vitamin D
Folic acid as the only acute therapy

explanation: The best answer is thiamine because prolonged vomiting can deplete thiamine stores, and carbohydrate administration can precipitate Wernicke encephalopathy in a deficient patient. Intravenous thiamine should accompany initial rehydration when vomiting has been prolonged. Clinical pearl: In prolonged hyperemesis requiring IV hydration, give thiamine before or with dextrose to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy. Key distinction: Folate is important in pregnancy but does not prevent the acute encephalopathy caused by thiamine depletion during prolonged vomiting.

subject: Microbiology
testedConcept: Polymicrobial postpartum endometritis treated with broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics
physicianTask: Patient Care: Pharmacotherapy
memoryAnchor: Cesarean delivery and prolonged labor or membrane rupture increase postpartum endometritis risk; treat broadly for mixed aerobic and anaerobic flora.
commonTrap: Postpartum fever with uterine tenderness and foul lochia is not a normal recovery finding and requires systemic rather than topical therapy.
stem: Two days after an unplanned cesarean delivery following prolonged labor, a 25-year-old woman develops fever, lower abdominal pain, uterine tenderness, and foul-smelling lochia. Which treatment is most appropriate?

options.text: Intravenous clindamycin plus gentamicin
Oral fluconazole alone
Topical metronidazole without systemic therapy
No treatment because fever is expected after cesarean delivery

explanation: The best answer is intravenous clindamycin plus gentamicin because postpartum endometritis is usually a polymicrobial ascending infection involving aerobic and anaerobic genital-tract organisms. Broad-spectrum intravenous therapy is appropriate for a febrile patient with uterine tenderness and malodorous lochia. Clinical pearl: Cesarean delivery and prolonged labor or membrane rupture increase postpartum endometritis risk; treat broadly for mixed aerobic and anaerobic flora. Key distinction: Postpartum fever with uterine tenderness and foul lochia is not a normal recovery finding and requires systemic rather than topical therapy.

Key distinction: Postpartum fever with uterine tenderness and foul lochia is not a normal recovery finding and requires systemic rather than topical therapy.

subject: Physiology
testedConcept: Aortic dissection — branch-vessel malperfusion from left subclavian involvement
physicianTask: Patient Care: Diagnosis
memoryAnchor: Aortic dissection can obstruct an arch branch and cause asymmetric pulses or arm blood pressures.
commonTrap: Students choose atherosclerotic brachial occlusion because it also reduces arm flow — but atherosclerosis is chronic and cannot explain an acute BP asymmetry in a patient with known dissection on CT.
stem: A 68-year-old man with hypertension presents with sudden tearing chest pain radiating to his back. Blood pressure is 180/110 mmHg in the right arm and 140/90 mmHg in the left arm. CT angiography shows an aortic dissection flap extending through the arch and narrowing the origin of the left subclavian artery. Which mechanism best explains the blood pressure difference between arms?

options.text: Compression of the left subclavian artery by the dissection flap
Atherosclerotic occlusion of the left brachial artery
Reflex vasodilation of left upper extremity vasculature
Increased cardiac output to the right subclavian territory

explanation: The dissection flap compromises the left subclavian origin, reducing perfusion pressure to the left arm and producing asymmetric arm blood pressures. A dissection that begins strictly distal to the left subclavian artery would not, by itself, explain obstruction of the branch origin; the imaging must demonstrate branch-vessel involvement as it does here.
pearl: BP difference >20 mmHg between arms = red flag for aortic dissection. Always use CT angiography — not ultrasound — when dissection is suspected.

optionExplanations.A: The CT directly shows the flap narrowing the left subclavian origin. This lowers perfusion pressure distal to that branch while right-arm pressure remains higher, producing the measured inter-arm difference.
optionExplanations.B: Atherosclerotic brachial occlusion can reduce distal arm perfusion, but this is a chronic, progressive process — not an acute finding in a patient presenting with sudden tearing pain and a dissection confirmed on CT. The temporal relationship (acute onset) and CT findings directly exclude this explanation.
optionExplanations.C: Reflex vasodilation is a downstream response to ischemia, not a primary driver of BP asymmetry. No reflex mechanism would selectively dilate one extremity enough to create a 40 mmHg bilateral pressure difference in the acute setting described here.
optionExplanations.D: Cardiac output distributes bilaterally via the aorta and both subclavian arteries symmetrically. There is no mechanism by which the heart selectively increases output to only the right subclavian without a structural obstruction — like the dissection flap — explaining the asymmetry.

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: Cor pulmonale — hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction mechanism
physicianTask: Medical Knowledge: Applying Foundational Science Concepts
memoryAnchor: COPD → chronic hypoxia → HPV → pulmonary HTN → RV strain = cor pulmonale. WHO Group 3.
commonTrap: Students pick LV failure because bilateral leg edema suggests left-sided failure, but the normal LV on echo and hyperinflation on CXR indicate RV overload from lung disease — not retrograde LHF.
stem: A 55-year-old man with a 30 pack-year smoking history presents with dyspnea and bilateral leg edema. Echocardiography shows right ventricular hypertrophy and estimated pulmonary artery systolic pressure of 60 mmHg. Chest X-ray shows hyperinflation. What is the most likely mechanism for his cardiac findings?

options.text: Chronic hypoxic vasoconstriction increasing pulmonary vascular resistance
Left ventricular failure causing retrograde pulmonary hypertension
Primary pulmonary arterial hypertension from an idiopathic mutation
Recurrent pulmonary emboli obliterating the vascular bed

explanation: COPD causes chronic alveolar hypoxia, triggering hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction. Sustained vasoconstriction leads to irreversible vascular remodeling, pulmonary hypertension, and right ventricular hypertrophy (cor pulmonale). This is WHO Group 3 secondary pulmonary hypertension.
pearl: Cor pulmonale = RV failure from lung disease. Key mechanism: hypoxia → vasoconstriction → pulmonary HTN → RV overload.

optionExplanations.A: COPD destroys alveolar architecture and causes chronic alveolar hypoxia. Hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction is protective initially, but sustained hypoxia drives vascular remodeling — smooth muscle hypertrophy and intimal thickening — creating fixed pulmonary hypertension. This RV pressure overload (WHO Group 3 PH) explains the RVH and elevated PASP on echo.
optionExplanations.B: LV failure (WHO Group 2 PH) elevates pulmonary venous pressure, backing up into the pulmonary arterial system. However, the clinical picture here — hyperinflation on CXR, 30 pack-year history, RVH without LV dysfunction on echo — points to lung disease as the primary driver, not left-sided heart failure.
optionExplanations.C: Primary PAH (WHO Group 1) is idiopathic or heritable (BMPR2 mutations), typically affecting young women without underlying lung disease. The 30 pack-year smoking history and CXR hyperinflation point overwhelmingly toward COPD-related secondary PH, not an idiopathic primary process.
optionExplanations.D: Chronic thromboembolic PH (WHO Group 4) requires a history of recurrent pulmonary emboli and characteristic imaging showing organized thrombus. This patient has no such history, and the COPD explanation accounts for all findings more parsimoniously.

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: UIP / IPF — biopsy features (temporal heterogeneity, fibroblastic foci)
physicianTask: Patient Care: Diagnosis
memoryAnchor: UIP/IPF: Temporal heterogeneity + fibroblastic foci + honeycombing. Basal-subpleural on CT. Restrictive PFTs.
commonTrap: Students pick NSIP because it also causes bilateral fibrosis and restrictive disease, but NSIP shows temporal homogeneity (uniform fibrosis) — temporal heterogeneity with fibroblastic foci is the key UIP differentiator.
stem: A 52-year-old woman with progressive dyspnea over 2 years has PFTs showing reduced FVC, reduced TLC, and normal FEV1/FVC ratio. CT shows bilateral basal reticular opacities with honeycombing. Biopsy shows temporally heterogeneous fibrosis with fibroblastic foci. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Usual interstitial pneumonia pattern (IPF)
Nonspecific interstitial pneumonia (NSIP)
Cryptogenic organizing pneumonia (COP)
Hypersensitivity pneumonitis

explanation: The usual interstitial pneumonia pattern (UIP/IPF) is defined by temporal heterogeneity (old and new fibrosis coexisting), fibroblastic foci (active scarring at the leading edge), and honeycomb change. CT shows basal-subpleural predominance. PFTs are restrictive. Prognosis is poor (median survival 3–5 years). Antifibrotic therapy (pirfenidone or nintedanib) slows progression.
pearl: UIP/IPF: temporal heterogeneity + fibroblastic foci + honeycomb (basal-subpleural on CT). Restrictive PFTs. Treat with pirfenidone or nintedanib.

optionExplanations.A: The usual interstitial pneumonia pattern (UIP/IPF) is uniquely defined by temporal heterogeneity — coexistence of fibroblastic foci (active new fibrosis), established dense collagen fibrosis, and cystic honeycomb spaces in the same biopsy specimen.
