This patchwork of old and new lesions at different stages of fibrosis reflects ongoing episodic injury rather than a single insult. CT characteristically shows bilateral basal-subpleural honeycombing with or without traction bronchiectasis. Restrictive PFTs with reduced DLCO confirm physiologic impairment. Antifibrotic therapy (pirfenidone or nintedanib) slows the rate of FVC decline but does not reverse disease.
optionExplanations.B: Nonspecific interstitial pneumonia (NSIP) also produces bilateral lung fibrosis with a restrictive PFT pattern but is distinguished from UIP by temporal homogeneity — all areas of the biopsy show fibrosis at the same stage of development (either cellular inflammation or fibrosis, but not both simultaneously). On CT, NSIP shows bilateral ground-glass opacities with subpleural sparing (not honeycombing). NSIP is strongly associated with connective tissue diseases (particularly systemic sclerosis and rheumatoid arthritis). It generally has better prognosis than UIP and responds better to immunosuppression.
optionExplanations.C: Cryptogenic organizing pneumonia (COP, formerly BOOP) presents with subacute onset of cough and dyspnea following what often resembles a respiratory infection. Biopsy shows intraluminal polyps of granulation tissue filling alveoli and bronchioles (Masson bodies) in a temporally homogeneous pattern — no fibroblastic foci or honeycombing. CT shows bilateral consolidative or ground-glass opacities with a peribronchovascular or subpleural distribution. COP typically responds dramatically to corticosteroids (distinguishing it from IPF), and relapses upon steroid taper. The chronic 2-year course and honeycombing on CT argue strongly against COP.
optionExplanations.D: Hypersensitivity pneumonitis (HP) is caused by repeated inhalation of organic antigens (bird proteins, mold, occupational exposures) with subsequent immune-mediated lung injury. Biopsy shows poorly formed non-caseating granulomas in a peribronchiolar distribution, lymphocytic interstitial infiltrate, and bridging fibrosis in chronic HP. In chronic fibrosing HP, the fibrosis can resemble UIP histologically, but the upper-lobe and peribronchovascular distribution on CT, presence of granulomas, and careful occupational/exposure history distinguish it from IPF. Antigen avoidance and immunosuppression are used in HP, while antifibrotics are used in IPF.

subject: Physiology
testedConcept: Pyelonephritis — mechanism of acute creatinine elevation (prerenal)
physicianTask: Medical Knowledge: Applying Foundational Science Concepts
memoryAnchor: Pyelonephritis + elevated Cr = prerenal most common (dehydration + fever-induced vasodilation). IV fluids restore renal function.
commonTrap: Students pick intrinsic renal injury from bacterial toxins because the kidney is clearly infected, but direct tubular toxin injury is uncommon in uncomplicated pyelonephritis — prerenal from reduced intake and vasodilation is the dominant mechanism.
stem: A 24-year-old woman with recurrent UTIs presents with 3 days of dysuria, urinary frequency, fever, nausea, decreased oral intake, and right flank pain. Temperature is 38.9°C, pulse is 112/min, and blood pressure is 104/68 mmHg. Mucous membranes are dry. Urinalysis shows leukocyte esterase and nitrites positive. Serum BUN is 42 mg/dL and creatinine is 1.8 mg/dL, increased from a baseline of 0.8 mg/dL. Which mechanism best explains the creatinine elevation?

options.text: Prerenal azotemia from fever-induced vasodilation and reduced oral intake
Intrinsic renal injury from direct bacterial tubular toxins
Obstructive uropathy from inflammatory ureteral compression
Immune complex glomerulonephritis triggered by the infection

explanation: In acute pyelonephritis, fever-induced systemic vasodilation, decreased oral intake, and the inflammatory response reduce effective circulating volume, impairing renal perfusion. This prerenal mechanism is responsible for most creatinine elevation. BUN:Cr ratio is typically >20. IV fluids and antibiotics normalize renal function in most cases.
pearl: Acute pyelonephritis raises creatinine mainly through prerenal mechanisms. Always give IV fluids alongside antibiotics. Intrinsic AKI can occur but is less common.

optionExplanations.A: Prerenal azotemia from fever-induced vasodilation and reduced oral intake is the dominant mechanism of creatinine elevation in acute pyelonephritis. Systemic infection triggers inflammatory cytokine release (TNF-α, IL-6) causing peripheral vasodilation and reducing mean arterial pressure; simultaneously, fever increases insensible losses, nausea reduces oral intake, and pain impairs hydration. The net effect is reduced effective circulating volume reaching the renal afferent arterioles. Despite structural renal infection, the glomeruli and tubules remain functionally intact — creatinine rises because less blood is filtered, not because tubular cells are destroyed. BUN:Cr ratio >20 and FENa <1% confirm the prerenal physiology. IV fluids rapidly restore renal perfusion and normalize creatinine.
optionExplanations.B: Direct bacterial tubular toxicity causing intrinsic acute tubular necrosis (ATN) can occur in severe pyelonephritis or ascending Gram-negative bacteremia, but it is not the dominant mechanism in uncomplicated pyelonephritis. ATN would produce muddy brown granular casts on urinalysis, FENa >2%, and isosthenuric urine — findings absent here. The clinical scenario (young woman, moderate creatinine elevation from a clear baseline, hemodynamic stability, lack of septic shock or aminoglycoside use) is much more consistent with prerenal azotemia than intrinsic tubular injury.
optionExplanations.C: Obstructive uropathy from inflammatory ureteral compression is a rare complication of severe pyelonephritis or perinephric abscess, where edematous periureteral tissue or infected periureteral lymphatics mechanically compress the ureter. This would typically cause unilateral obstruction with severe flank pain, hydronephrosis on imaging, and — in bilateral cases — anuria rather than the modest creatinine elevation seen here. The patient has bilateral function (urinating with dysuria and frequency), and the clinical picture does not suggest complete ureteral obstruction. Imaging (ultrasound or CT) would show hydronephrosis if obstruction were the cause.
optionExplanations.D: Immune complex glomerulonephritis can complicate bacterial infections but typically occurs with specific organisms: post-streptococcal GN (group A Streptococcus pharyngitis or impetigo), endocarditis-associated GN (staph, strep), or shunt nephritis. Gram-negative urinary pathogens (E. coli, Klebsiella) causing pyelonephritis are not typical triggers for immune complex-mediated glomerular injury. GN would produce nephritic sediment (RBC casts, dysmorphic RBCs, proteinuria) and activated complement (low C3), neither of which is described. The rapid resolution with antibiotics and IV fluids is further inconsistent with glomerulonephritis, which has a protracted course.

subject: Immunology
testedConcept: X-linked agammaglobulinemia — BTK mutation and B cell maturation arrest
physicianTask: Medical Knowledge: Applying Foundational Science Concepts
memoryAnchor: XLA: BruTon BTK. X-linked, Boys only, B cells absent, all Ig absent, normal T cells. Presents ~6 months. Treat: IVIG.
commonTrap: Students pick ADA deficiency because both cause recurrent bacterial infections, but ADA deficiency is SCID (both B and T cells absent) — normal T cells in this patient immediately excludes any SCID diagnosis.
stem: A 6-year-old boy has recurrent infections with encapsulated organisms (S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, N. meningitidis). Flow cytometry shows absent B cells with very low serum immunoglobulins; T cell counts are normal. His maternal uncles had similar histories. Which pathophysiology best explains this presentation?

options.text: Defective BTK tyrosine kinase causing failure of B cell maturation beyond the pro-B stage
ADA enzyme deficiency causing toxic deoxyadenosine accumulation in B and T cells
Absent MHC class II expression causing failure of CD4+ T cell activation
Defective CD40L on T cells preventing B cell class-switch recombination

explanation: X-linked agammaglobulinemia (Bruton disease) is caused by BTK mutations. BTK is required for B cell maturation beyond the pro-B cell stage; without it, mature B cells are absent and immunoglobulins are absent. T cells are normal. X-linked inheritance explains the affected males with affected maternal uncles. ADA deficiency causes SCID (both B and T cell failure). CD40L deficiency causes hyper-IgM syndrome.
pearl: XLA (Bruton): BTK mutation, X-linked, absent B cells + absent Ig, normal T cells. Presents ~6 months (after maternal Ab wane). Recurrent encapsulated bacterial infections. Treat: IVIG.

optionExplanations.A: BTK (Bruton tyrosine kinase) is essential for pre-BCR signaling at the pro-B to pre-B cell transition. Without BTK, B cells cannot mature past the pro-B stage — circulating mature B cells and all immunoglobulin classes are absent. X-linked inheritance (BTK gene on chromosome Xq21.3) means males are affected; females are carriers. The maternal uncle pattern is classic for X-linked recessive transmission.
optionExplanations.B: ADA (adenosine deaminase) deficiency causes SCID by allowing toxic deoxyadenosine accumulation, which is especially damaging to developing lymphocytes — both T and B cells are eliminated. The critical distinguishing feature is that ADA deficiency eliminates BOTH B and T cells (SCID), whereas this patient has NORMAL T cell counts. Any SCID diagnosis is excluded by the normal T cell count.
optionExplanations.C: Bare lymphocyte syndrome (MHC class II deficiency) impairs CD4+ T cell development and activation by preventing antigen presentation. T cell function is severely compromised, and B cells cannot receive T cell help for class-switching. However, B cells are PRESENT (unlike XLA where they are absent), and the clinical phenotype includes T cell dysfunction — distinct from the isolated B cell/Ig deficiency seen here.
optionExplanations.D: CD40L deficiency (X-linked hyper-IgM syndrome, HIGM1) prevents T cells from sending the CD40/CD40L co-stimulatory signal to B cells, blocking class-switch recombination. The result is very high IgM with absent or very low IgG, IgA, and IgE — but B cells ARE present and CAN produce IgM. This is a completely different phenotype from XLA where B cells are absent and all Ig classes (including IgM) are depleted.

subject: Cardiology
testedConcept: Inferior STEMI — RCA territory and right ventricular infarction management
physicianTask: Patient Care: Diagnosis
memoryAnchor: Inferior STEMI: II, III, aVF elevation → RCA. Check right-sided leads (V4R) for RV infarction. RV infarct: AVOID nitrates + diuretics (preload-dependent). Give IV fluids.
commonTrap: Giving nitrates for chest pain in inferior STEMI without recognizing RV infarction — nitrates cause vasodilation and preload reduction, which is catastrophic when RV output is preload-dependent.
stem: A 62-year-old man presents with crushing chest pain for 1 hour. EKG shows 3-mm ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF. BP is 86/58 mmHg and JVP is markedly elevated. Right-sided leads show 2-mm ST elevation in V4R. Which management approach is most appropriate?

options.text: Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation and urgent primary PCI — avoid nitrates and diuretics
Sublingual nitroglycerin, aspirin, and urgent primary PCI
Furosemide IV for pulmonary edema and urgent cath lab activation
Thrombolytics alone — cath lab not indicated for inferior STEMI

explanation: ST elevation in II, III, aVF indicates inferior STEMI from RCA occlusion. V4R elevation confirms concurrent right ventricular infarction. In RV infarction, the right ventricle is preload-dependent — its function requires adequate filling pressure. Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation maintains preload and RV output. Nitrates and diuretics reduce preload catastrophically in RV infarction, worsening hypotension and cardiogenic shock. Urgent primary PCI to restore RCA flow is definitive treatment.
pearl: RV infarction: inferior STEMI + JVP elevation + hypotension + clear lungs + V4R elevation. AVOID nitrates and diuretics. IV fluids + primary PCI. Distinguish from LV failure (pulmonary edema).

optionExplanations.A: Right ventricular infarction requires IV fluid resuscitation as the immediate hemodynamic intervention because the ischemic RV becomes preload-dependent. The infarcted RV loses contractility and cannot generate sufficient pressure to fill the pulmonary vasculature and deliver adequate preload to the LV. The only way to maintain cardiac output is to maximize RV filling through intravenous fluids — increasing right heart preload overcomes the impaired contractility by operating on a higher portion of the Frank-Starling curve. Simultaneously, urgent primary PCI of the culprit RCA lesion restores coronary flow, limiting RV myocardial damage. A pulmonary artery catheter (Swan-Ganz) during RV infarction would show: elevated RA pressure, low pulmonary artery pressure, low wedge pressure (preload depleted LV), and low cardiac output — confirming preload-dependent hemodynamics.
optionExplanations.B: Sublingual nitroglycerin is contraindicated in RV infarction. Nitrates are venodilators — they reduce venous return and right heart preload. In a normal heart, this venodilatory effect reduces LV filling pressure, relieving pulmonary congestion (therapeutic in LV failure/ACS with preserved RV). However, in RV infarction, the ischemic RV already cannot pump adequate blood forward — reducing preload further by venodilation eliminates the compensatory mechanism (augmented preload) that the RV is using to maintain output. Nitroglycerin in RV infarction can precipitate profound hypotension, bradycardia, and cardiogenic shock. This is one of the most dangerous drug errors in emergency cardiology: treating "ACS chest pain" with sublingual nitrates without first checking for RV involvement in inferior STEMI.
optionExplanations.C: Furosemide IV is a loop diuretic that reduces preload by promoting natriuresis and venodilation — the same preload-reducing mechanism that makes it first-line for LEFT ventricular failure with pulmonary edema. In RV infarction, the clinical picture (JVP elevation, hypotension, clear lungs) mimics the preload distribution of tamponade — the right heart is congested (elevated JVP), but the left heart is underfilled (hypotension without pulmonary edema). Administering furosemide reduces the already-inadequate preload that the failing RV is using to maintain forward output, precipitating severe hemodynamic collapse. The visual similarity to LV failure (hypotension + JVP elevation) is a known clinical trap — clear lungs exclude significant LV failure; V4R elevation confirms RV involvement.
optionExplanations.D: Thrombolytics are an alternative reperfusion strategy for STEMI when primary PCI is unavailable within 120 minutes. The statement that "cath lab is not indicated for inferior STEMI" is factually incorrect — inferior STEMI from RCA occlusion has the same indication for urgent mechanical reperfusion as anterior STEMI. Moreover, inferior STEMI complicated by RV infarction has particularly high mortality and represents a case where rapid coronary flow restoration is most critical. Right ventricular myocardium has inherent reperfusion potential because of its thin wall and lower oxygen demand; early PCI of the RCA can dramatically improve RV function and hemodynamics. Thrombolytics alone (without PCI) have inferior outcomes to primary PCI in any STEMI, and especially in hemodynamically unstable patients.

subject: Physiology
testedConcept: Cardiac tamponade — pulsus paradoxus via ventricular interdependence
physicianTask: Medical Knowledge: Applying Foundational Science Concepts
memoryAnchor: Beck's triad: Hypotension + Muffled heart sounds + Elevated JVP. Pulsus paradoxus >10 mmHg. Echo: RA then RV diastolic collapse. Treat: pericardiocentesis.
commonTrap: Students pick Kussmaul sign (JVP rises on inspiration) — Kussmaul sign is constrictive pericarditis, not tamponade. In tamponade, JVP is elevated but falls (normal) with inspiration; pulsus paradoxus is the key physical finding.
stem: A 45-year-old woman with metastatic breast cancer presents with dyspnea, hypotension (BP 78/52), elevated JVP, and muffled heart sounds. Bedside echo shows a large circumferential pericardial effusion with right atrial collapse in systole. Pulsus paradoxus is 22 mmHg. Which hemodynamic mechanism explains pulsus paradoxus in this condition?

options.text: Inspiratory increase in RV filling shifts the interventricular septum leftward, reducing LV filling and stroke volume
Inspiratory increase in pulmonary vascular resistance reduces left-sided venous return
Pericardial fluid directly compresses coronary arteries, reducing myocardial contractility during inspiration
Inspiratory lung expansion mechanically compresses the aorta, reducing systolic pressure

explanation: In cardiac tamponade, the rigid pericardial effusion creates a fixed pericardial volume. During inspiration, venous return to the right ventricle increases normally. However, the pericardial sac prevents outward RV expansion, so the RV can only expand by shifting the interventricular septum leftward into the LV cavity. This reduces LV filling and stroke volume — a >10 mmHg drop in SBP with inspiration defines pulsus paradoxus. This mechanism (exaggerated ventricular interdependence from external constraint) is unique to tamponade.
pearl: Pulsus paradoxus mechanism: inspiration → ↑RV filling → septum shifts left (fixed pericardial volume) → ↓LV filling → SBP drops >10 mmHg. Treat immediately with pericardiocentesis. Echo shows RA then RV diastolic collapse.

optionExplanations.A: During inspiration, intrathoracic pressure falls and right heart venous return augments. In tamponade, the pericardial effusion creates a fixed, non-distensible space. The filling RV cannot expand outward, so it expands inward by bowing the interventricular septum leftward. This compresses the LV cavity and reduces LV end-diastolic volume — dropping stroke volume and systolic blood pressure by >10 mmHg during inspiration. This exaggerated ventricular interdependence is the defining hemodynamic mechanism of pulsus paradoxus in tamponade.
optionExplanations.B: Pulmonary vascular resistance does increase slightly with lung expansion during deep inspiration due to alveolar vessel compression. However, this effect is quantitatively minor and does not explain a >10 mmHg systolic blood pressure drop. The dominant mechanism in tamponade is interventricular septal shift from increased RV filling in a fixed pericardial space, not changes in pulmonary vascular resistance. This distractor inverts cause and effect.
optionExplanations.C: The pericardial effusion does increase intrapericardial pressure, which is transmitted to all four chambers — this is why cardiac output falls in tamponade. However, coronary perfusion pressure (diastolic aortic pressure minus myocardial wall tension) is not significantly altered during the inspiratory cycle specifically. The beat-to-beat variation in pulsus paradoxus is explained by the real-time septal shift mechanism, not by coronary compression.
optionExplanations.D: Inspiratory aortic compression from lung expansion does not occur in any clinically relevant physiology. The aorta is not compressible by the lungs. This is a physiologically implausible distractor designed to test whether students understand that pulsus paradoxus is a right-heart/ventricular interdependence phenomenon, not an aortic compression phenomenon.

subject: Physiology
testedConcept: ARDS — Berlin criteria and lung-protective low tidal volume ventilation
physicianTask: Patient Care: Clinical Interventions
memoryAnchor: ARDS Berlin: bilateral infiltrates + PaO2/FiO2 <300 + not fully explained by cardiac failure + acute (<7 days). Lung-protective: 6 mL/kg IBW, plateau <30, PEEP ≥5. AVOID high tidal volumes.
commonTrap: Students use 10–12 mL/kg tidal volume because it is physiologic at rest — high tidal volumes cause ventilator-induced lung injury (VILI) through volutrauma in the heterogeneously injured ARDS lung. The ARMA trial proved 6 mL/kg IBW reduces mortality.
stem: A 38-year-old man with aspiration pneumonia is intubated for worsening hypoxia. CXR shows bilateral diffuse infiltrates. PaO2/FiO2 is 160 mmHg. PCWP is 10 mmHg and echocardiography confirms normal LV function. Which ventilator strategy is most appropriate?

options.text: Low tidal volume 6 mL/kg ideal body weight with plateau pressure limited to 30 cmH2O
High tidal volume 12 mL/kg with low PEEP to minimize barotrauma
High-frequency oscillatory ventilation as initial strategy
Pressure support ventilation with high pressure support to reduce patient work

explanation: This patient meets Berlin ARDS criteria (bilateral infiltrates, PaO2/FiO2 160 mmHg, acute onset, not cardiac). Lung-protective ventilation with low tidal volume 6 mL/kg ideal body weight and plateau pressure ≤30 cmH2O reduces ventilator-induced lung injury. The ARMA trial demonstrated a 22% relative reduction in in-hospital mortality compared to 12 mL/kg. The heterogeneously injured ARDS lung concentrates tidal volume into less-damaged ("baby lung") regions, causing overdistension at higher volumes. PEEP is titrated to ≥5 cmH2O to prevent atelectrauma.
pearl: ARDS: 6 mL/kg IBW + plateau ≤30 cmH2O + PEEP ≥5. Permissive hypercapnia is acceptable. Prone positioning for severe ARDS (PaO2/FiO2 <150). Berlin severity: mild 200–300, moderate 100–200, severe <100.

optionExplanations.A: The ARMA trial (ARDSNet, 2000) randomized 861 patients to 6 vs. 12 mL/kg ideal body weight tidal volume, demonstrating 22% relative mortality reduction with low tidal volume 6 mL/kg. The injured ARDS lung is heterogeneous — well-ventilated regions receive the full tidal volume and are overdistended at standard volumes ("volutrauma"). Limiting plateau pressure to ≤30 cmH2O prevents barotrauma. PEEP recruitment prevents atelectrauma from cyclic alveolar collapse and reopening.
optionExplanations.B: High tidal volumes (10–12 mL/kg) cause ventilator-induced lung injury through volutrauma (overdistension of less-injured lung units), barotrauma (alveolar rupture from excessive pressure), and biotrauma (systemic inflammatory mediator release). The ARMA trial definitively proved that standard physiologic tidal volumes are harmful in ARDS. "Low PEEP to minimize barotrauma" is counterproductive — PEEP prevents atelectrauma; removing it worsens outcomes.
optionExplanations.C: High-frequency oscillatory ventilation (HFOV) was proposed as an extreme lung-protective strategy using very small tidal volumes at high frequencies. However, the OSCAR and OSCILLATE trials (both 2013) showed no mortality benefit, and OSCILLATE demonstrated increased mortality with HFOV compared to conventional lung-protective ventilation. HFOV is not recommended as a first-line strategy and may be considered only as rescue therapy in refractory severe ARDS.
optionExplanations.D: Pressure support ventilation delivers a clinician-set pressure during patient-initiated breaths. In the acute phase of severe ARDS, patients have high respiratory drive, and spontaneous efforts against high airway pressures can generate large tidal volumes despite set pressure support — known as patient self-inflicted lung injury (P-SILI). Controlled mechanical ventilation with strict tidal volume limits is preferred in moderate-severe ARDS. PSV is used later during weaning when respiratory drive normalizes.

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: Pulmonary embolism — Wells score and CT pulmonary angiography for high-probability PE
physicianTask: Patient Care: Laboratory and Diagnostic Studies
memoryAnchor: Wells PE: DVT signs (3), alternative dx unlikely (3), HR>100 (1.5), immobilization (1.5), prior VTE (1.5), hemoptysis (1), malignancy (1). Score ≥5 = high → CT-PA directly. Low score → D-dimer first.
commonTrap: Sending D-dimer in a high-probability patient — D-dimer is a sensitive but nonspecific test only useful for RULING OUT PE in low-probability patients. A positive D-dimer in a high-probability patient does not confirm PE and does not change management.
stem: A 32-year-old woman has sudden dyspnea and pleuritic chest pain after a 14-hour flight. HR 118 bpm, SpO2 93%. Left leg Doppler confirms proximal DVT. CXR: no infiltrates. Which is the most appropriate immediate diagnostic step?

options.text: CT pulmonary angiography (CT-PA)
D-dimer level
V/Q lung scan
Echocardiography alone

explanation: CT pulmonary angiography (CT-PA) is the gold-standard imaging for PE. This patient has a HIGH clinical probability by Wells criteria: DVT signs and confirmed DVT (+3), HR >100 (+1.5), and alternative diagnosis less likely (+3) — Wells score ≥7. In high-probability patients, CT pulmonary angiography is performed directly without D-dimer testing. D-dimer is only useful in low/intermediate probability to rule out PE. CT-PA also provides risk stratification (clot burden, RV/LV ratio).
pearl: High-probability Wells PE (≥5): go directly to CT pulmonary angiography — no D-dimer. D-dimer is for low-probability patients (high sensitivity, low specificity). CT-PA also shows RV/LV ratio for hemodynamic risk stratification.

optionExplanations.A: CT pulmonary angiography is the gold standard for PE diagnosis with sensitivity and specificity exceeding 95%. This patient has a Wells score ≥7 (DVT confirmed = 3, alternative diagnosis unlikely = 3, HR >100 = 1.5), placing her in the high-probability category. Guidelines from ACC/AHA and ACCP recommend proceeding directly to CT-PA in high-probability patients without obtaining D-dimer. CT-PA also provides simultaneous assessment of clot location, right heart strain (RV/LV diameter ratio >1.0 indicates hemodynamic compromise), and can guide risk stratification for thrombolysis vs. anticoagulation alone.
optionExplanations.B: D-dimer is a fibrin degradation product with >95% sensitivity for PE but poor specificity. It is only clinically useful in low to intermediate pre-test probability patients — a negative D-dimer rules out PE, avoiding CT radiation. In high-probability patients (Wells ≥5), the pre-test probability is too high for D-dimer to meaningfully change management: a negative result would be unexpected and potentially misleading, while a positive result (near-universal in this scenario) does not confirm PE and adds no diagnostic information. Sending D-dimer in high-probability PE delays definitive imaging.
optionExplanations.C: V/Q lung scan is an alternative PE imaging modality when CT-PA is contraindicated — contrast allergy, severe renal insufficiency, or pregnancy (relative preference to reduce radiation to breast/uterus). V/Q results are categorized as high/intermediate/low probability and can be non-diagnostic. In this hemodynamically stable young woman without contraindications to CT contrast, CT-PA provides superior specificity and diagnostic certainty compared to V/Q scan. V/Q is most valuable when CT is not available or contraindicated.
optionExplanations.D: Echocardiography can identify RV dilation (RV/LV ratio >1.0), tricuspid regurgitation, and paradoxical septal motion — signs of hemodynamically significant PE. It is invaluable in unstable patients (shock, pulseless electrical activity) who cannot be transported to CT. Echo may also detect right heart thrombus. However, echocardiography CANNOT directly visualize pulmonary emboli and cannot confirm PE as the cause of findings. In this hemodynamically stable patient, CT-PA provides definitive anatomical confirmation that echocardiography cannot.

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: Celiac disease — anti-tTG IgA antibody, HLA-DQ2/DQ8, and villous atrophy
physicianTask: Patient Care: Diagnosis
memoryAnchor: Celiac: anti-tTG IgA most sensitive/specific (check total IgA first). HLA-DQ2 (95%) or DQ8 (5%). Biopsy: villous atrophy + crypt hyperplasia + intraepithelial lymphocytes. Treat: gluten-free diet.
commonTrap: Students order IgG anti-gliadin antibodies — anti-gliadin (AGA) has poor specificity and is outdated. Anti-tTG IgA and anti-endomysial IgA (EMA) are the current gold-standard serologic tests. Always check total IgA because selective IgA deficiency causes false-negative tTG IgA.
stem: A 27-year-old woman has chronic diarrhea, weight loss, fatigue, and a pruritic papulovesicular rash on the elbows. Labs show microcytic anemia and low 25-OH vitamin D. Small bowel biopsy shows villous atrophy with crypt hyperplasia and intraepithelial lymphocytosis. Which antibody finding would best confirm celiac disease?

options.text: Elevated anti-tissue transglutaminase (anti-tTG) IgA antibodies with normal total IgA
Positive IgG anti-gliadin antibodies (AGA-IgG)
Elevated IgE to wheat protein on radioallergosorbent testing
Positive perinuclear anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic antibody (p-ANCA)

explanation: Celiac disease is confirmed by elevated anti-tissue transglutaminase (anti-tTG) IgA antibodies (sensitivity 95–99%, specificity 97–99%) combined with the classic small bowel biopsy findings of villous atrophy, crypt hyperplasia, and intraepithelial lymphocytosis. Anti-tTG IgA is the preferred first-line serologic test. Normal total IgA is confirmed because selective IgA deficiency (prevalence ~2% in celiac) causes false-negative anti-tTG IgA. Anti-gliadin IgG has poor specificity. p-ANCA is associated with ulcerative colitis. Wheat IgE is a wheat allergy marker, not celiac.
pearl: Celiac: anti-tTG IgA (sensitivity/specificity >95%) — always check total IgA. HLA-DQ2/DQ8. Biopsy confirms. Complications: osteoporosis, enteropathy-associated T-cell lymphoma (EATL), refractory disease. Treatment: gluten-free diet (lifelong).

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: DKA vs hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state (HHS) — distinguishing laboratory features
physicianTask: Patient Care: Diagnosis
memoryAnchor: DKA: T1DM, glucose 250–600, pH<7.3, anion gap ↑, ketones ↑. HHS: T2DM elderly, glucose >600, pH>7.3, minimal ketones, serum osm>320. HHS mortality higher than DKA.
commonTrap: Students diagnose DKA for any hyperglycemic coma — HHS features glucose often >1000 mg/dL without acidosis or significant ketosis. The distinguishing features are pH >7.3 (no acidosis) and osmolality >320 mOsm/kg in HHS.
stem: A 74-year-old man with T2DM is found confused with 5 days of polyuria and decreased oral intake. Glucose is 1050 mg/dL. Serum osmolality is 392 mOsm/kg. pH is 7.36, HCO3 is 23 mEq/L, anion gap is 11. Urine ketones are trace. Creatinine is 2.4 mg/dL. Which of the following best describes this presentation and its primary treatment?

options.text: Hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state — treat with aggressive IV fluid resuscitation before insulin
Diabetic ketoacidosis — treat with insulin drip and bicarbonate replacement
Diabetic ketoacidosis — check anion gap more carefully before diagnosing HHS
Lactic acidosis from metformin — stop all diabetes medications immediately

explanation: Hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state (HHS) is defined by glucose >600 mg/dL, serum osmolality >320 mOsm/kg, pH >7.30, minimal ketones, and absent anion gap acidosis — all met here. HHS occurs in older T2DM patients from dehydration without insulin deficiency sufficient to cause ketosis. Treatment: aggressive IV fluid resuscitation to correct the large free water deficit (often 8–10 L), followed by gradual insulin infusion. Rapid glucose correction risks cerebral edema. Bicarbonate is not indicated (no acidosis). HHS mortality (10–20%) exceeds DKA (~1%).
pearl: HHS vs DKA: HHS=glucose>600, pH>7.3, osm>320, no ketoacidosis, elderly T2DM. DKA=glucose250–600, pH<7.3, anion gap ↑, ketones, T1DM. Treat HHS: fluids first, then insulin. Rapid correction → cerebral edema.

subject: Physiology
testedConcept: Primary vs secondary adrenal insufficiency — ACTH levels, aldosterone, and hyperpigmentation
physicianTask: Patient Care: Diagnosis
memoryAnchor: Primary AI (Addison's): ↑ACTH → hyperpigmentation, ↓cortisol, ↓aldosterone → hyperkalemia + hyponatremia. Secondary AI: ↓ACTH → no pigmentation, ↓cortisol, normal aldosterone (RAAS intact). Cosyntropin test confirms.
commonTrap: Students forget hyperkalemia occurs ONLY in primary AI — secondary AI (pituitary failure) has intact RAAS because aldosterone is controlled by angiotensin II, not ACTH. Hyperkalemia with adrenal insufficiency = primary (Addison's).
stem: A 40-year-old woman presents with fatigue, weight loss, hypotension, hyponatremia (Na 127), and hyperkalemia (K 5.8). She has increased skin pigmentation in skin creases and gum lines. Morning cortisol is 3 µg/dL. ACTH stimulation test shows no cortisol rise. Plasma ACTH is markedly elevated. Which diagnosis and distinguishing feature are most accurate?

options.text: Primary adrenal insufficiency — aldosterone deficiency causes hyperkalemia and elevated ACTH drives hyperpigmentation
Secondary adrenal insufficiency — pituitary ACTH deficiency causes aldosterone deficiency
Steroid withdrawal — exogenous glucocorticoids suppressed ACTH chronically
Pheochromocytoma — catecholamine excess suppresses cortisol synthesis

explanation: Primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison's disease) destroys the adrenal cortex, eliminating both cortisol and aldosterone. Aldosterone deficiency causes hyperkalemia (impaired K+ excretion) and hyponatremia (volume depletion + ADH release). The ACTH level is markedly elevated because loss of cortisol negative feedback drives pituitary ACTH hypersecretion. Excess ACTH processing produces MSH, which stimulates melanocytes causing hyperpigmentation — a pathognomonic feature of primary adrenal insufficiency. In secondary AI, ACTH is low (pituitary failure) and aldosterone is preserved (RAAS-controlled), so hyperkalemia and hyperpigmentation do not occur.
pearl: Primary AI (Addison's): ↑ACTH, hyperpigmentation, ↓cortisol, ↓aldosterone → hyperkalemia. Secondary AI: ↓ACTH, no pigmentation, ↓cortisol, normal aldosterone, no hyperkalemia. Cosyntropin stimulation test: cortisol <18 µg/dL at 30–60 min = adrenal failure.

subject: Pharmacology
testedConcept: Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia — PF4-heparin antibody mechanism and non-heparin anticoagulant requirement
physicianTask: Patient Care: Diagnosis
memoryAnchor: HIT: platelet drop >50% on days 5–10 + thrombosis + positive PF4-heparin antibody. Stop ALL heparin. Start argatroban or bivalirudin. NO warfarin acutely (protein C depletion → thrombosis). NO platelet transfusion.
commonTrap: Students stop heparin and start warfarin — warfarin depletes protein C (short half-life) faster than clotting factors, causing a transient prothrombotic state. In active HIT with high thrombotic risk, this paradoxically worsens thrombosis. Non-heparin anticoagulant first.
stem: A 58-year-old man on unfractionated heparin for DVT has a platelet count drop from 240,000 to 40,000/µL on day 8. He develops right leg ischemia. Anti-PF4/heparin ELISA is positive. What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Discontinue heparin and begin argatroban (direct thrombin inhibitor)
Discontinue heparin and immediately start warfarin with a loading dose
Reduce heparin dose by 50% and switch to low-molecular-weight heparin
Transfuse platelets and continue heparin at the same dose

explanation: Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) is caused by IgG antibodies against platelet factor 4 (PF4)-heparin complexes that activate platelets via FcγRIIA, causing platelet consumption and paradoxical thrombosis. Management requires: (1) immediate cessation of all heparin products (including flushes, LMWH, heparin-coated lines), and (2) initiation of a non-heparin anticoagulant such as argatroban (a direct thrombin inhibitor). Argatroban is preferred when renal function is impaired (hepatically metabolized). Warfarin must not be started acutely — it depletes protein C (short half-life) before clotting factors, worsening thrombosis. Platelet transfusion paradoxically worsens thrombosis.
pearl: HIT: 4Ts (Thrombocytopenia, Timing 5–10 days, Thrombosis, alternative explanations absent). Stop heparin → start argatroban or fondaparinux. No warfarin until platelets >150K and therapeutic with non-heparin agent ≥5 days.

optionExplanations.A: In HIT, IgG antibodies against PF4-heparin complexes bind platelet FcγRIIA receptors, triggering platelet activation, aggregation, and release of additional PF4 — amplifying the thrombogenic state. The platelet count falls because activated platelets are consumed, while the coagulation cascade is simultaneously activated by platelet-derived procoagulants, producing a prothrombotic state despite thrombocytopenia. Argatroban directly inhibits thrombin (the final common pathway) without requiring heparin, breaking the cycle. All heparin products must be stopped immediately — including LMWH (>80% cross-reactivity with PF4 antibodies) and heparin flush.
optionExplanations.B: Warfarin initiation in acute HIT is contraindicated and dangerous. Warfarin inhibits synthesis of all vitamin K-dependent factors (II, VII, IX, X) and the anticoagulant proteins C and S. Protein C has the shortest half-life (~8 hours), so it is depleted first during warfarin loading. This creates a transient prothrombotic window — in a patient already in a hypercoagulable HIT state, this dramatically increases risk of warfarin-induced skin necrosis, venous limb gangrene, or extension of existing thrombosis. Warfarin may be started only after platelets recover to >150,000 and after therapeutic non-heparin anticoagulation for ≥5 days.
optionExplanations.C: Reducing the heparin dose or switching to LMWH are both inappropriate in HIT. PF4-heparin antibodies cross-react with LMWH-PF4 complexes in over 80% of cases, maintaining the antigen-antibody-platelet interaction at lower but still antigenic drug concentrations. Dose reduction prolongs heparin exposure and antigen presence without resolving the immune-mediated platelet activation. Any heparin product must be completely discontinued.
optionExplanations.D: Platelet transfusion in HIT is contraindicated because it provides additional substrates for FcγRIIA-mediated platelet activation, potentially fueling further thrombin generation and worsening thrombosis. Unlike production failure anemias (aplastic anemia, myelosuppression) where transfusion corrects a deficit, HIT thrombocytopenia results from immune-mediated platelet activation and consumption — adding platelets escalates the thrombotic risk. This "feeding fuel to the fire" concern is why the clinical aphorism "platelets for the clot, not the count" applies in HIT.

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: Disseminated intravascular coagulation — coagulation factor consumption and fibrinolysis distinguishing from TTP
physicianTask: Patient Care: Diagnosis
memoryAnchor: DIC: ↑PT, ↑PTT, ↓platelets, ↓fibrinogen, ↑D-dimer/FSPs, schistocytes. Everything abnormal. TTP: ↓platelets + MAHA + normal coagulation (PT/PTT/fibrinogen NORMAL) + ADAMTS13 deficiency.
commonTrap: Students confuse DIC with TTP because both cause thrombocytopenia and microangiopathic hemolytic anemia (MAHA). The critical distinguishing lab: PT and PTT are PROLONGED in DIC (clotting factor consumption), while they are NORMAL in TTP (only platelets and microvasculature affected — not coagulation cascade).
stem: A 35-year-old woman with gram-negative septic shock develops oozing from IV sites. Labs: PT 32s, PTT 72s, platelets 28,000/µL, fibrinogen 48 mg/dL (low), D-dimer >10 µg/mL, schistocytes on smear. Which diagnosis is most likely and which laboratory finding definitively distinguishes it from thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP)?

options.text: Disseminated intravascular coagulation — prolonged PT/PTT and low fibrinogen distinguish from TTP, which has normal coagulation studies
TTP — ADAMTS13 deficiency elevates D-dimer in TTP as well as DIC
DIC — an elevated ADAMTS13 level is the defining laboratory feature of DIC
HUS from E. coli — thrombocytopenia + MAHA in sepsis is always HUS-mediated

explanation: Disseminated intravascular coagulation results from systemic activation of coagulation (by endotoxin, inflammatory cytokines) that simultaneously generates fibrin microthrombi (causing MAHA and schistocytes) and consumes clotting factors and platelets. The laboratory hallmark of DIC is consumption of coagulation factors: elevated PT and PTT, thrombocytopenia, low fibrinogen, and high D-dimer. In TTP, platelet microthrombi form from deficient ADAMTS13 (von Willebrand factor cleaving protease), causing MAHA and thrombocytopenia — but the coagulation cascade is not activated, so PT, PTT, and fibrinogen remain normal. This laboratory dissociation is the key diagnostic distinction.
pearl: DIC vs TTP: DIC=↑PT,↑PTT,↓fibrinogen,↑D-dimer (coagulation cascade consumed). TTP=normal PT/PTT/fibrinogen,↓platelets, ADAMTS13 deficient. Treat DIC: underlying cause + FFP + cryoprecipitate. Treat TTP: plasma exchange + steroids.

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: Ectopic pregnancy — β-hCG discriminatory zone and methotrexate criteria
physicianTask: Patient Care: Diagnosis
memoryAnchor: Ectopic: β-hCG >1500–2000 + no IUP on transvaginal US = presumed ectopic. Stable + hCG<5000 + no fetal cardiac activity → methotrexate. Unstable → salpingostomy. Risk factors: PID, prior ectopic, tubal surgery, IUD.
commonTrap: Students wait for β-hCG levels to double over 48 hours before deciding — in ectopic pregnancy with adnexal mass and absent IUP, the diagnosis is presumed and management should not be delayed. Serial hCG monitoring is for uncertain early intrauterine pregnancies of unknown viability, not confirmed ectopic.
stem: A 28-year-old woman with a history of pelvic inflammatory disease has 7 weeks of amenorrhea, vaginal spotting, and right lower quadrant pain. Serum β-hCG is 2,300 mIU/mL. Transvaginal US shows no intrauterine pregnancy and a 2.4-cm right adnexal mass with ring-of-fire Doppler vascularity. She is hemodynamically stable. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Methotrexate 50 mg/m² IM — medical management if criteria are met
Immediate laparoscopic salpingectomy regardless of hemodynamic status
Serial β-hCG every 48 hours — wait for abnormal doubling before treating
Transvaginal aspiration of the adnexal mass under ultrasound guidance

explanation: Presumed ectopic pregnancy: β-hCG above the discriminatory zone (>1500–2000 mIU/mL), no intrauterine pregnancy on transvaginal ultrasound, and adnexal mass with ring-of-fire vascularity. This patient is hemodynamically stable, β-hCG is 2,300 mIU/mL (<5,000), and no fetal cardiac activity is mentioned — criteria for methotrexate (MTX) medical management. MTX inhibits dihydrofolate reductase, stopping trophoblast proliferation. Contraindications to MTX include hemodynamic instability, β-hCG >5,000, fetal cardiac activity, hepatic/renal disease, or inability to follow up. Success rate 75–95% when criteria are met.
pearl: Ectopic pregnancy: β-hCG >1500–2000 + no IUP on TVUS = presumed ectopic. Stable + hCG<5000 + no cardiac activity → MTX (inhibits DHFR, stops trophoblast growth). Unstable or failed MTX → surgical salpingostomy/salpingectomy. Risk factors: PID, prior ectopic.

subject: Immunology
testedConcept: SLE — anti-dsDNA antibodies, Type III hypersensitivity, and complement consumption in lupus nephritis
physicianTask: Medical Knowledge: Applying Foundational Science Concepts
memoryAnchor: SLE: ANA most sensitive (95%+), anti-Smith most specific (no activity correlation), anti-dsDNA most specific AND correlates with disease activity (especially nephritis). Type III hypersensitivity: immune complex deposition → complement activation → low C3/C4.
commonTrap: Students pick anti-Smith as the antibody correlating with disease activity — anti-Smith is highly specific for SLE but does NOT fluctuate with disease activity. Anti-dsDNA correlates with flare (especially lupus nephritis) and rising anti-dsDNA + falling complement predicts relapse.
stem: A 28-year-old woman with SLE has worsening facial rash, arthralgia, and tea-colored urine. Labs show anti-dsDNA markedly elevated, C3 48 mg/dL (low), C4 7 mg/dL (low), and UA with RBC casts and proteinuria. What is the most likely immunopathologic mechanism of her glomerular injury?

options.text: Type III hypersensitivity — immune complex deposition in glomerular capillaries activates complement and recruits neutrophils
Type II hypersensitivity — anti-GBM antibodies directly attack the glomerular basement membrane
Type IV hypersensitivity — cytotoxic T cell infiltration destroys glomerular podocytes
Type I hypersensitivity — IgE-mediated mast cell degranulation within the glomerular tuft

explanation: Lupus nephritis is the classic example of Type III hypersensitivity (immune complex-mediated). Anti-dsDNA antibodies form complexes with nuclear antigens released from apoptotic cells. These immune complexes deposit in glomerular capillary walls (subendothelial, mesangial, or subepithelial locations). Complement is activated via the classical pathway, consuming C3 and C4 (explaining the low complement levels) and generating C3a/C5a (chemotactic anaphylatoxins that recruit neutrophils) and MAC (C5b-9), directly damaging the glomerular capillary wall. The result is nephritic syndrome with RBC casts, hematuria, and proteinuria.
pearl: SLE nephritis: Type III hypersensitivity. Anti-dsDNA + ↓C3/C4 = active disease. Granular "full-house" immunofluorescence (IgG, IgA, IgM, C3, C1q). Class III/IV = proliferative GN (worst prognosis). Treat with mycophenolate + steroids (induction), then maintenance.

subject: Biochemistry
testedConcept: Phenylketonuria — phenylalanine hydroxylase deficiency and BH4 cofactor requirement
physicianTask: Medical Knowledge: Applying Foundational Science Concepts
memoryAnchor: PKU: phenylalanine hydroxylase deficient, BH4 cofactor. Phe accumulates → phenylpyruvate (musty urine), competition with large neutral AAs at BBB → ↓Tyr/Trp in brain → intellectual disability, hypopigmentation. Newborn screen. Treat: low-Phe diet ± sapropterin.
commonTrap: Students confuse PKU with alkaptonuria — alkaptonuria is homogentisate oxidase deficiency causing dark urine on air exposure (oxidized homogentisic acid) and ochronosis (darkening connective tissue). PKU causes intellectual disability and musty urine from phenylpyruvate.
stem: A 6-month-old infant has intellectual disability, fair skin and hair, eczema, and musty-smelling urine. Newborn screening (Guthrie test) was abnormal but not followed up. Plasma phenylalanine is 28 mg/dL. The deficient enzyme requires tetrahydrobiopterin (BH4) as a cofactor. Which enzyme deficiency explains this presentation?

options.text: Phenylalanine hydroxylase — inability to convert phenylalanine to tyrosine in the liver
Homogentisate oxidase — inability to metabolize homogentisic acid in tyrosine catabolism
Tyrosine hydroxylase — inability to convert tyrosine to DOPA in catecholamine synthesis
Fumarylacetoacetate hydrolase — inability to complete tyrosine catabolism (tyrosinemia type I)

explanation: Phenylketonuria (PKU) is caused by phenylalanine hydroxylase (PAH) deficiency. PAH converts phenylalanine (essential amino acid from dietary protein) to tyrosine in the liver, requiring tetrahydrobiopterin (BH4) as a cofactor and molecular oxygen. Without functional phenylalanine hydroxylase, phenylalanine accumulates and is transaminated to phenylpyruvate, phenylacetate, and phenyllactate (giving the musty urine odor). Excess phenylalanine competes with other large neutral amino acids for the blood-brain barrier transporter, reducing tyrosine/tryptophan availability and impairing neurotransmitter synthesis — causing intellectual disability. Hypopigmentation results from reduced tyrosine for melanin synthesis.
pearl: PKU: phenylalanine hydroxylase deficiency + BH4 cofactor. Phe → phenylpyruvate (musty). Competes with Tyr/Trp at BBB → ↓neurotransmitters → intellectual disability. Hypopigmentation (less tyrosine for melanin). Newborn screen: Guthrie test. Treat: low-Phe diet. Maternal PKU: teratogenic (microcephaly, CHD).

subject: Pharmacology
testedConcept: Familial hypercholesterolemia — LDL receptor deficiency and statin mechanism via SREBP upregulation
physicianTask: Patient Care: Pharmacotherapy
memoryAnchor: Statins: ↓HMG-CoA reductase → ↓intrahepatic cholesterol → SREBP activation → ↑LDL receptor expression → ↑LDL clearance. Het FH: 1 functional allele upregulated → statins moderately effective. Hom FH: no functional receptors → add PCSK9 inhibitor.
commonTrap: Students think statins primarily reduce LDL production — statins do reduce cholesterol synthesis, but the PRIMARY mechanism by which LDL-C falls in blood is upregulation of hepatic LDL receptors via SREBP, which dramatically increases LDL clearance from plasma. Without receptors to upregulate (homozygous FH), statins are much less effective.
stem: A 32-year-old man with familial hypercholesterolemia (heterozygous LDL receptor loss-of-function mutation) has LDL-C 380 mg/dL, arcus corneae, and Achilles tendon xanthomata. His father had a myocardial infarction at age 36. High-intensity statin therapy is started. Why does statin therapy reduce serum LDL-C despite the reduced number of functional LDL receptors?

options.text: Statins inhibit HMG-CoA reductase, reducing intracellular cholesterol and upregulating SREBP-mediated expression of the remaining functional LDL receptors
Statins directly correct the LDL receptor gene mutation, restoring full receptor expression
Statins reduce hepatic VLDL production, indirectly reducing LDL independently of LDL receptor upregulation
Statins block cholesterol absorption in the intestine, preventing dietary cholesterol from raising serum LDL-C

explanation: Statins competitively inhibit HMG-CoA reductase, the rate-limiting enzyme in hepatic cholesterol biosynthesis. This reduces intracellular hepatocyte cholesterol, which activates SREBP (sterol regulatory element-binding protein) cleavage and nuclear translocation. SREBP upregulates transcription of the LDL receptor gene. In heterozygous familial hypercholesterolemia (one functional LDL receptor allele remaining), the functioning receptor copy is dramatically upregulated, increasing hepatic LDL clearance from plasma. LDL-C falls by 40–60%. In homozygous FH (both alleles defective), there are virtually no functional LDL receptors to upregulate, making statins substantially less effective.
pearl: Statins: ↓HMG-CoA reductase → ↓hepatocyte cholesterol → SREBP → ↑LDL receptors → ↑LDL clearance. Het FH (1 functional allele): statins moderately effective. Hom FH: add PCSK9 inhibitor (prevents LDL receptor degradation) + ezetimibe ± LDL apheresis.

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: Minimal change disease — podocyte effacement and steroid responsiveness
physicianTask: Patient Care: Diagnosis
memoryAnchor: MCD: child + nephrotic syndrome + normal LM + effaced foot processes on EM = minimal change disease. Exquisitely steroid-sensitive. Associated with Hodgkin lymphoma in adults.
commonTrap: Students pick FSGS because both have foot process effacement — FSGS shows segmental sclerosis on LM and is not steroid-sensitive. MCD has normal light microscopy.
stem: A 4-year-old boy presents with periorbital edema, abdominal distension, and frothy urine. Labs show albumin 1.8 g/dL, total protein 3.2 g/dL, urine protein 8.4 g/day, and total cholesterol 360 mg/dL. Renal biopsy shows no abnormality on light microscopy; electron microscopy shows diffuse podocyte foot process effacement with no electron-dense deposits. Which diagnosis and initial treatment are most appropriate?

options.text: Minimal change disease — treat with corticosteroids (prednisone)
Focal segmental glomerulosclerosis — treat with cyclosporine
Membranous nephropathy — treat with rituximab
IgA nephropathy — treat with ACE inhibitor alone

explanation: Minimal change disease is the most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in children. The biopsy hallmark is normal light microscopy with diffuse podocyte foot process effacement on electron microscopy and no immune deposits. Corticosteroids (prednisone) produce complete remission in >90% of children — the exquisite steroid-sensitivity is pathognomonic for minimal change disease. FSGS shows segmental glomerulosclerosis on LM and is resistant to steroids. Membranous nephropathy has subepithelial deposits (spike-and-dome). IgA nephropathy has mesangial IgA deposits.
pearl: MCD: child + nephrotic + normal LM + foot process effacement on EM (no deposits) → steroids (>90% remission). Adult MCD = check for Hodgkin lymphoma. FSGS = same EM but segmental sclerosis on LM, steroid-resistant.

optionExplanations.A: Minimal change disease causes nephrotic syndrome through selective loss of the glomerular polyanion charge barrier (loss of heparan sulfate proteoglycans), allowing albumin to leak despite structurally intact glomerular membranes. The characteristic EM finding is diffuse effacement of podocyte foot processes — the only light-microscopic finding that distinguishes it from normal is occasional mild mesangial hypercellularity. Corticosteroids (prednisone 60 mg/m²/day) induce complete remission in over 90% of children within 4–8 weeks. Rapid steroid responsiveness is so characteristic that it is essentially diagnostic when present.
optionExplanations.B: Focal segmental glomerulosclerosis shares podocyte foot process effacement on EM with MCD but is distinguished by segmental areas of sclerosis and hyalinosis visible on light microscopy. FSGS is the most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in adults and is associated with obesity, HIV (collapsing variant), sickle cell disease, and prior glomerular injury. It has a poor prognosis with frequent progression to CKD. Unlike MCD, FSGS responds poorly to corticosteroids — calcineurin inhibitors (cyclosporine or tacrolimus) are used for steroid-resistant cases.
optionExplanations.C: Membranous nephropathy is the most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in non-diabetic adults. Biopsy shows thickened glomerular capillary walls on LM (PAS stain), subepithelial immune deposits creating a "spike and dome" pattern on silver stain, and granular IgG4 deposits on immunofluorescence. Anti-PLA2R antibodies are positive in 70–80% of primary cases. Rituximab (anti-CD20) is now first-line for rituximab-sensitive primary membranous nephropathy. The normal LM and absence of immune deposits in this child excludes membranous nephropathy.
optionExplanations.D: IgA nephropathy (Berger disease) is the most common primary glomerulonephritis worldwide and typically presents as recurrent gross hematuria or microscopic hematuria following mucosal infections — not as pure nephrotic syndrome. Biopsy shows mesangial IgA deposits on immunofluorescence. ACE inhibitors are used for proteinuria reduction. The clinical scenario here — a child with heavy proteinuria (nephrotic range), hypoalbuminemia, edema, and hyperlipidemia — without hematuria and with normal LM on biopsy — is entirely inconsistent with IgA nephropathy.

subject: Physiology
testedConcept: Autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease — PKD1/PKD2 and cyst pathogenesis
physicianTask: Medical Knowledge: Applying Foundational Science Concepts
memoryAnchor: ADPKD: PKD1 (85%, chr 16, polycystin-1) or PKD2 (15%, chr 4, polycystin-2). Berry aneurysms, mitral valve prolapse, liver cysts. CKD by 60s. HTN early. Screen: tolvaptan for rapid progressors.
commonTrap: Students choose ARPKD because it involves liver fibrosis — ADPKD also has liver cysts but NOT liver fibrosis. ARPKD causes periportal liver fibrosis (congenital hepatic fibrosis) and presents in infancy/childhood with oligohydramnios.
stem: A 42-year-old man with hypertension has bilateral flank pain and hematuria. Family history: father had renal failure in his 50s, paternal uncle died from a brain hemorrhage. Imaging shows massively enlarged bilateral kidneys with innumerable cysts. Creatinine is 2.1 mg/dL. Which gene mutation most likely explains this presentation?

options.text: PKD1 mutation encoding polycystin-1 on chromosome 16
PKHD1 mutation encoding fibrocystin on chromosome 6
TSC2 mutation causing tuberous sclerosis complex
VHL mutation causing von Hippel-Lindau syndrome

explanation: Autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD) is caused by PKD1 (chromosome 16, polycystin-1, 85% of cases) or PKD2 (chromosome 4, polycystin-2, 15%) mutations. PKD1 mutations cause earlier and more severe disease. The bilateral massively enlarged cystic kidneys, hypertension, hematuria, CKD, positive family history (dominant inheritance — father affected), and the paternal uncle with brain hemorrhage (berry aneurysm rupture) are classic. Associated findings: intracranial berry aneurysms (8–12%), liver cysts, mitral valve prolapse. PKHD1 causes autosomal recessive PKD (infancy, liver fibrosis). TSC2 causes tuberous sclerosis. VHL causes clear cell RCC and hemangioblastomas.
pearl: ADPKD: PKD1 (chr16, 85%) > PKD2 (chr4). Berry aneurysms, liver cysts, mitral valve prolapse. Screen relatives. Tolvaptan (V2 receptor antagonist) slows cyst growth in rapid progressors. HTN = earliest manifestation. CKD by 50–60s in PKD1.

subject: Physiology
testedConcept: Renal tubular acidosis type I — impaired distal H+ secretion and urine pH
physicianTask: Patient Care: Laboratory and Diagnostic Studies
memoryAnchor: RTA I (distal): urine pH >5.5 despite acidosis. Hypokalemia + nephrocalcinosis + osteomalacia. Caused by Sjogren, SLE, amphotericin B. RTA II (proximal): HCO3 wasting, urine pH variable. RTA IV: hyperkalemia (aldosterone deficiency/resistance).
commonTrap: Students pick RTA II because they remember proximal tubule is the "main" acid handler — in type I, the DISTAL tubule cannot secrete H+, so urine cannot be maximally acidified. The pathognomonic finding is urine pH >5.5 despite severe metabolic acidosis.
stem: A 38-year-old woman with Sjogren syndrome has fatigue and muscle weakness. Labs: pH 7.28, HCO3 12 mEq/L, K+ 2.9 mEq/L, chloride 112 mEq/L (non-anion gap acidosis). Urine pH is 6.8 despite severe systemic acidosis. Renal ultrasound shows bilateral medullary nephrocalcinosis. Which renal tubular acidosis subtype explains these findings?

options.text: Type I distal RTA — failure of distal H+ secretion keeps urine pH above 5.5
Type II proximal RTA — failure of bicarbonate reabsorption causing bicarbonaturia
Type IV RTA — aldosterone deficiency causing hyperkalemia and acidosis
Anion gap metabolic acidosis — lactic acidosis from tissue hypoperfusion

explanation: Type I distal RTA results from impaired H+ secretion by intercalated cells of the distal nephron (alpha-intercalated cells). Without distal H+ secretion, urine cannot be acidified below pH 5.5 even during severe systemic metabolic acidosis. Key features: non-anion gap hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis, hypokalemia (K+ wasted distally), urine pH >5.5, and nephrocalcinosis (alkaline urine promotes calcium phosphate precipitation). Sjogren syndrome, SLE, and amphotericin B are classic causes. Treatment: oral bicarbonate supplementation.
pearl: Type I distal RTA: urine pH >5.5 despite acidosis. Hypokalemia + nephrocalcinosis. Causes: Sjogren, SLE, amphotericin B. Type II: proximal HCO3 wasting (Fanconi syndrome). Type IV: hyperkalemia (diabetic nephropathy, hypoaldosteronism). Treat I/II with oral bicarbonate.

subject: Physiology
testedConcept: Hyperkalemia EKG progression — peaked T waves to sine wave pattern
physicianTask: Patient Care: Laboratory and Diagnostic Studies
memoryAnchor: Hyperkalemia EKG sequence: Peaked T → PR prolongation → P wave loss → Wide QRS → Sine wave → VFib. "PAST the sine wave = death." Treat K>6.5 or symptomatic: Ca gluconate (membrane stabilize), insulin+glucose, bicarb, kayexalate/patiromer, dialysis.
commonTrap: Students give insulin without calcium gluconate first — in severe hyperkalemia with EKG changes, calcium gluconate stabilizes the cardiac membrane immediately (within 5 min); insulin and glucose shift K+ intracellularly (20–30 min). Calcium must come first.
stem: A 68-year-old man with ESRD on hemodialysis missed his last two sessions. K+ is 7.4 mEq/L. EKG shows peaked narrow T waves in precordial leads, prolonged PR interval, and absent P waves with a widened QRS of 180 ms. Which immediate treatment should be administered first?

options.text: Intravenous calcium gluconate to stabilize the cardiac membrane
Regular insulin with dextrose to shift potassium intracellularly
Sodium bicarbonate to alkalinize plasma and shift potassium
Sodium polystyrene sulfonate orally to eliminate potassium via GI tract

explanation: Intravenous calcium gluconate is the first-line treatment for severe hyperkalemia with EKG changes. Calcium gluconate stabilizes the cardiac membrane potential by raising the threshold potential, protecting against fatal arrhythmias within 1–5 minutes. It does not lower serum potassium but antagonizes its membrane effects. After calcium, insulin plus dextrose shifts K+ intracellularly (onset 15–30 min) and is the most reliable potassium-lowering intervention. Sodium bicarbonate has modest benefit. Sodium polystyrene sulfonate (Kayexalate) causes GI potassium elimination over hours — not appropriate in acute EKG emergency.
pearl: Severe hyperkalemia EKG treatment sequence: (1) calcium gluconate IV (membrane stabilization, 5 min) → (2) insulin + dextrose (intracellular shift, 20 min) → (3) NaHCO3 if acidotic → (4) albuterol → (5) Kayexalate/patiromer (elimination) → (6) emergent dialysis (definitive).

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: Guillain-Barré syndrome — ascending weakness with albuminocytologic dissociation in CSF
physicianTask: Patient Care: Diagnosis
memoryAnchor: GBS: ascending weakness 2–4 weeks post-infection (Campylobacter, EBV, CMV, Zika). CSF: ↑protein + normal WBC = albuminocytologic dissociation. Demyelinating AIDP = slowed NCV. Treat: IVIG or plasmapheresis. Monitor respiratory — 30% need ventilation.
commonTrap: Students worry that elevated CSF protein means meningitis and add antibiotics — GBS CSF protein is elevated WITHOUT pleocytosis (albuminocytologic dissociation), which distinguishes it from meningitis (high protein AND high WBC).
stem: A 24-year-old woman had a GI illness 3 weeks ago. Now she has ascending bilateral leg weakness that started distally and has progressed to her arms. Deep tendon reflexes are absent. Sensory examination shows mild distal paresthesias. Lumbar puncture: opening pressure normal, WBC 3 cells/µL, protein 98 mg/dL (elevated), glucose normal. Nerve conduction studies show prolonged distal latencies and slowed conduction velocities. Which is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Guillain-Barré syndrome — acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy
Bacterial meningitis — needs emergent antibiotics and dexamethasone
Myasthenia gravis — repetitive nerve stimulation would show decremental response
Transverse myelitis — MRI spine would show T2 signal abnormality

explanation: Guillain-Barré syndrome (acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy, AIDP) is the most common cause of acute flaccid paralysis in previously healthy individuals. The ascending pattern of weakness, areflexia, mild sensory symptoms following a viral illness, albuminocytologic dissociation in CSF (elevated protein with normal cell count), and slowed nerve conduction velocities confirming demyelination are the classic diagnostic combination. GBS is an autoimmune attack on peripheral nerve myelin (molecular mimicry after infection). Treatment: intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) or plasmapheresis — both equivalent. Respiratory monitoring is critical as 30% require mechanical ventilation.
pearl: GBS: post-infectious ascending weakness + areflexia + albuminocytologic dissociation (↑protein, normal WBC) + slowed NCV. Most common trigger: Campylobacter jejuni. Treatment: IVIG or plasmapheresis (not both). Monitor FVC — intubate if <20 mL/kg or rapidly declining.

subject: Immunology
testedConcept: Terminal complement deficiency — C5-C9 MAC deficiency and recurrent Neisseria infections
physicianTask: Patient Care: Diagnosis
memoryAnchor: Terminal complement (C5-C9 MAC) deficiency → recurrent Neisseria infections (N. meningitidis, N. gonorrhoeae) specifically. C3 deficiency → encapsulated organisms broadly. MBL deficiency → early childhood infections. Lupus-like illness = C1q/C2/C4 deficiency (classical pathway).
commonTrap: Students pick C3 deficiency for any complement-related recurrent infection — C3 deficiency causes susceptibility to ALL encapsulated bacteria (pneumococcus, H. influenzae, meningococcus). Terminal complement (MAC) deficiency specifically and selectively causes recurrent Neisseria infections because lysis by MAC is particularly important for killing these gram-negative diplococci.
stem: A 22-year-old college student has had three separate episodes of meningococcal meningitis caused by different serogroups of N. meningitidis, with one episode of disseminated gonococcal infection. He has no other infections. Serum CH50 is undetectable. Which complement component deficiency most likely explains his susceptibility?

options.text: Terminal complement deficiency (C5-C9) impairing membrane attack complex formation
C3 deficiency impairing opsonization of encapsulated bacteria broadly
C1 inhibitor deficiency causing hereditary angioedema
Mannose-binding lectin deficiency impairing the lectin complement pathway

explanation: Terminal complement deficiency (C5-C9) impairs formation of the membrane attack complex (MAC). The MAC is uniquely critical for killing gram-negative diplococci — particularly Neisseria meningitidis and Neisseria gonorrhoeae — through direct bacterial lysis. Patients with terminal complement deficiency have a dramatically increased susceptibility specifically to Neisseria species infections while remaining resistant to other pathogens. The undetectable CH50 confirms total complement pathway deficiency (CH50 measures the functional lytic capacity of the entire pathway). Recurrent meningococcal infections from different serogroups (or disseminated gonococcal infection) in a young person should always prompt complement evaluation.
pearl: Terminal complement (C5-C9/MAC) deficiency → ONLY Neisseria infections (meningitidis, gonorrhoeae). C3 deficiency → all encapsulated organisms. CH50 screens functional complement. Treat: meningococcal vaccination (all serogroups). Complement deficiencies are autosomal recessive.

subject: Pharmacology
testedConcept: Anaphylaxis — epinephrine as first-line treatment and mechanism of IgE-mediated mast cell degranulation
physicianTask: Patient Care: Clinical Interventions
memoryAnchor: Anaphylaxis: IgE → mast cell degranulation → histamine + leukotrienes + PAF → vasodilation + bronchospasm + urticaria + angioedema. First-line: epinephrine IM (thigh). Antihistamines and steroids are adjuncts ONLY — never first-line.
commonTrap: Students give diphenhydramine first for anaphylaxis because it treats allergy — antihistamines only block H1 receptors (reducing urticaria and itch) but do NOT reverse airway compromise, hypotension, or life-threatening bronchospasm. Epinephrine IM into the lateral thigh is always first-line.
stem: A 24-year-old woman develops throat tightening, diffuse urticaria, and wheezing 10 minutes after eating peanuts at a restaurant. BP is 88/56 mmHg, HR 124 bpm, SpO2 91%. She has audible stridor. Which is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.text: Epinephrine 0.3 mg intramuscular injection into the lateral thigh
Diphenhydramine 50 mg intramuscular injection
Methylprednisolone 125 mg intravenous bolus
Albuterol nebulized treatment for bronchospasm

explanation: Epinephrine intramuscular injection into the lateral thigh is the only first-line treatment for anaphylaxis. In anaphylaxis, IgE-mediated mast cell degranulation releases histamine, leukotrienes, and platelet-activating factor, causing vasodilation (hypotension), increased vascular permeability (angioedema, urticaria), and bronchospasm. Epinephrine reverses all components: alpha-1 agonism causes vasoconstriction (raises BP), beta-1 increases cardiac output, and beta-2 causes bronchodilation. Intramuscular lateral thigh achieves peak plasma levels faster than subcutaneous or deltoid injection. Diphenhydramine (H1 blocker), steroids (anti-inflammatory, prevents biphasic reaction), and albuterol are adjuncts given after epinephrine — they cannot reverse hypotension or airway edema.
pearl: Anaphylaxis: epinephrine IM (lateral thigh) is ALWAYS first-line — no exception. Adult dose: 0.3 mg (0.3 mL of 1:1000). Then: IV fluids, albuterol, diphenhydramine, steroids. Observe 4–8 hours for biphasic reaction. Discharge with EpiPen prescription.

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: Dermatomyositis — perifascicular atrophy and anti-Mi-2 antibody with heliotrope rash
physicianTask: Patient Care: Diagnosis
memoryAnchor: Dermatomyositis: heliotrope rash (periorbital) + Gottron papules (MCP/PIP) + proximal muscle weakness + ↑CK. Anti-Mi-2 (specific), anti-Jo-1 (anti-synthetase syndrome with ILD). Perifascicular atrophy on biopsy. Paraneoplastic (ovarian CA in adults). Treat: steroids.
commonTrap: Students confuse dermatomyositis and polymyositis — dermatomyositis has characteristic skin findings (heliotrope rash, Gottron papules) and perifascicular atrophy on biopsy (due to complement-mediated microangiopathy). Polymyositis has endomysial infiltrates with no skin findings.
stem: A 52-year-old woman has 8 weeks of proximal muscle weakness (cannot rise from chair without arm support). She has a violet discoloration around the eyelids and flat erythematous papules over the MCP and PIP joints bilaterally. CK is 3,400 U/L. Anti-Mi-2 antibody is positive. Which diagnosis and expected complication require immediate investigation?

options.text: Dermatomyositis — investigate for underlying malignancy (especially ovarian or lung cancer)
Polymyositis — investigate for interstitial lung disease with high-resolution CT
Systemic sclerosis — investigate for pulmonary hypertension with echocardiography
Sjögren syndrome — investigate for lymphoma given inflammatory myositis

explanation: Dermatomyositis is confirmed by the heliotrope rash (violet periorbital discoloration), Gottron papules (erythematous papules over MCP/PIP joints), proximal muscle weakness, elevated CK, and positive anti-Mi-2 antibody (highly specific for dermatomyositis). In adults over 40, dermatomyositis carries a significant association with underlying malignancy — particularly ovarian, lung, gastric, and colon cancers. This association mandates age-appropriate cancer screening and pelvic imaging (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, ovarian ultrasound, colonoscopy). Anti-Mi-2 is specifically associated with classic dermatomyositis without ILD. Anti-Jo-1 is associated with anti-synthetase syndrome (myositis + ILD + mechanic hands).
pearl: Dermatomyositis: heliotrope rash + Gottron papules + proximal weakness + ↑CK + anti-Mi-2. Biopsy: perifascicular atrophy (complement-mediated microangiopathy). Adult onset → screen for malignancy. Treatment: high-dose corticosteroids + steroid-sparing agent (azathioprine/methotrexate). Anti-Jo-1 = anti-synthetase syndrome (ILD risk).

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: PCOS — theca-cell androgen excess amplified by hyperinsulinemia
usmleContentArea: Female and Transgender Reproductive System & Breast
physicianTask: Medical Knowledge: Applying Foundational Science Concepts
memoryAnchor: PCOS: ovulatory dysfunction plus hyperandrogenism, often with insulin resistance. LH and insulin stimulate ovarian theca-cell androgen production; insulin also lowers hepatic SHBG.
commonTrap: An elevated LH-to-FSH ratio can occur but is neither required nor sufficiently reliable to diagnose PCOS. Diagnosis uses ovulatory dysfunction, hyperandrogenism, and polycystic ovarian morphology after excluding mimics.
stem: A 23-year-old woman has oligomenorrhea, hirsutism, acne, and difficulty conceiving for 18 months. BMI is 31 kg/m². Testosterone and fasting insulin are elevated, and pelvic ultrasound shows increased ovarian volume with numerous small follicles. Thyroid function, prolactin, and 17-hydroxyprogesterone are normal. Which mechanism best explains the androgen excess?

options.text: LH and hyperinsulinemia stimulate ovarian theca cells to overproduce androgens
Elevated FSH drives ovarian granulosa cell hyperplasia with excess estrogen conversion to androgens
Adrenal 21-hydroxylase deficiency causes shunting of cortisol precursors to androgens
Elevated prolactin suppresses dopamine, increasing LH pulsatility and androgen production

explanation: In PCOS, altered GnRH pulsatility can favor LH secretion, and LH stimulates ovarian theca cells to synthesize androgens. Hyperinsulinemia amplifies theca-cell androgen production and lowers hepatic sex hormone-binding globulin, increasing free testosterone. Follicular maturation stalls, producing chronic oligo-anovulation. The LH-to-FSH ratio is not required for diagnosis and should not be treated as a diagnostic criterion.
pearl: PCOS diagnosis requires two of three features after excluding mimics: oligo-anovulation, clinical or biochemical hyperandrogenism, and polycystic ovarian morphology. Combined hormonal contraception treats cycle irregularity and hyperandrogenism; letrozole is preferred for ovulation induction.

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: ALL in children — lymphoblasts, TdT positivity, and vincristine-based induction chemotherapy
physicianTask: Patient Care: Diagnosis
memoryAnchor: ALL: children (peak age 2–5), B-ALL most common. TdT+ (terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase), CD10+ (CALLA), CD19/CD20+ (B lineage). Treat: vincristine + prednisone + asparaginase + methotrexate (intrathecal). 90% cure rate in children. t(12;21) best prognosis; Philadelphia-positive ALL worst.
commonTrap: Students confuse ALL with AML — ALL blasts are TdT positive and lack granules/Auer rods. AML blasts have Auer rods (cytoplasmic crystalline inclusions), are myeloperoxidase (MPO) positive, and are TdT negative. ALL = lymphoid blasts; AML = myeloid blasts.
stem: A 5-year-old girl has 2 weeks of fatigue, pallor, bone pain, and easy bruising. Exam shows cervical lymphadenopathy and hepatosplenomegaly. CBC: WBC 85,000/µL with 90% blasts, Hgb 7.2 g/dL, platelets 22,000/µL. Bone marrow biopsy shows >90% lymphoblasts that are TdT positive, CD10 positive, and CD19 positive. Which diagnosis and the most important initial management are correct?

options.text: Precursor B-cell ALL — chemotherapy induction with vincristine, corticosteroids, and asparaginase
Acute myeloid leukemia — chemotherapy with cytarabine and daunorubicin (7+3 regimen)
Burkitt lymphoma/leukemia — rituximab plus intensive chemotherapy
T-cell ALL — indicated by CD19 positivity in this age group

explanation: Precursor B-cell ALL is the most common cancer diagnosed in children. The diagnostic criteria are met: lymphoblasts occupy more than 25% of marrow and express TdT, CD10, and the B-lineage marker CD19. Treatment follows induction, consolidation, and maintenance phases, with CNS-directed prophylaxis. Modern pediatric protocols produce long-term survival near 90%; brain and other central nervous system cancers, not ALL, currently account for the highest childhood cancer mortality in the United States.
pearl: ALL: children, TdT+, CD10+(CALLA). B-ALL: CD19/CD20+. T-ALL: CD3/CD7+. AML: TdT-, MPO+, Auer rods. ALL treatment: induction (vincristine+pred+ASP) → consolidation → maintenance. CNS prophylaxis: intrathecal MTX. t(12;21)/ETV6-RUNX1 = best prognosis (children). Ph+ ALL (BCR-ABL) = add imatinib.

optionExplanations.A: The immunophenotype of TdT+/CD10+/CD19+ defines precursor B-cell ALL. Terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase (TdT) is a DNA polymerase expressed only in immature lymphoid precursors during early development — it is not expressed in mature lymphocytes, myeloid cells, or normal marrow cells. Its presence indicates lymphoblastic origin. CD10 (CALLA) is expressed in the common precursor B-ALL stage. CD19 marks B-lineage commitment. Induction chemotherapy targets the rapidly proliferating blasts: vincristine (spindle poison), corticosteroids (lymphotoxic), asparaginase (depletes asparagine, required for lymphoblast growth), with or without an anthracycline.
optionExplanations.B: AML diagnosis requires myeloid blast morphology (Auer rods — crystalline cytoplasmic inclusions pathognomonic for AML), myeloperoxidase (MPO) positivity, and expression of myeloid antigens (CD13, CD33, CD117). The immunophenotype in this patient (TdT+, CD10+, CD19+) is exclusively lymphoid — AML blasts are TdT negative and would not express CD19. The 7+3 regimen (cytarabine 7 days + daunorubicin 3 days) is the standard AML induction regimen and would be inappropriate for ALL.
optionExplanations.C: Burkitt lymphoma/leukemia (mature B-cell ALL, L3 morphology) is a distinct entity from precursor B-cell ALL. Burkitt cells express surface immunoglobulin (sIg+) and CD20+ but are TdT NEGATIVE — the absence of TdT distinguishes it from lymphoblastic ALL. Burkitt is associated with t(8;14) and c-MYC overexpression. It responds to dose-intensive short-duration chemotherapy rather than the prolonged ALL protocol. The TdT positivity in this patient firmly establishes precursor B-cell ALL rather than Burkitt.
optionExplanations.D: T-cell ALL expresses T-lineage markers: CD3 (cytoplasmic), CD7, CD5, CD2, and variably CD4/CD8. Importantly, T-ALL does NOT express CD19 or CD20 (B-lineage markers). T-ALL also expresses TdT (as do all precursor lymphoblastic leukemias/lymphomas). The CD19 positivity in this patient establishes B lineage — T-ALL must express T lineage markers to carry that designation. T-ALL more commonly presents in adolescent males with a mediastinal mass and is associated with NOTCH1 mutations; it is treated with modified ALL protocols but has historically slightly worse prognosis than B-ALL.

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: Multiple myeloma — monoclonal protein, CRAB criteria, and plasma cell pathology
physicianTask: Patient Care: Diagnosis
memoryAnchor: Multiple myeloma CRAB: Calcium ↑, Renal failure, Anemia, Bone lesions (lytic, NO osteoblastic activity → no bone scan uptake). M-spike on SPEP, Bence-Jones protein in urine. ≥10% plasma cells on marrow biopsy. Rouleaux formation on smear.
commonTrap: Students say bone scan detects myeloma bone disease — lytic myeloma bone lesions have NO osteoblastic activity, so bone scans are NEGATIVE (bone scans detect osteoblastic activity). Skeletal survey X-rays or whole-body CT/PET detect lytic lesions in myeloma.
stem: A 68-year-old man has 4 months of back pain, fatigue, and confusion. Labs: Ca 12.4 mg/dL, creatinine 2.8 mg/dL, Hgb 8.9 g/dL. Serum protein electrophoresis shows a large M-spike. Urine shows Bence-Jones proteinuria. Bone marrow biopsy shows 45% plasma cells. Spine X-ray shows multiple vertebral lytic lesions. Which best characterizes the pathologic mechanism of hypercalcemia in this condition?

options.text: Myeloma cells secrete cytokines (RANKL, IL-6, MIP-1α) activating osteoclasts without corresponding osteoblast activity
Myeloma cells produce PTH-related peptide (PTHrP) causing humoral hypercalcemia of malignancy
Plasma cells secrete 1-alpha-hydroxylase, producing calcitriol and increasing intestinal calcium absorption
Monoclonal immunoglobulin deposits in bone matrix, causing pathologic stress fractures and calcium release
