explanation: In multiple myeloma, malignant plasma cells activate osteoclasts through paracrine secretion of RANKL (receptor activator of NF-κB ligand), IL-6, MIP-1α (macrophage inflammatory protein), and DKK-1 (which also suppresses osteoblasts). This creates a vicious cycle of bone resorption without corresponding bone formation — resulting in purely lytic lesions (no sclerosis) and releasing calcium from bone into the bloodstream. PTHrP causes hypercalcemia in solid tumor malignancies (squamous cell carcinoma, breast, kidney) — not typically in myeloma. Calcitriol production is the mechanism in granulomatous diseases (sarcoidosis, lymphoma). Ig deposits cause amyloid, not lytic lesions.
pearl: Myeloma hypercalcemia: osteoclast activation via RANKL/IL-6/MIP-1α without osteoblast coupling → lytic bone destruction → Ca²⁺ release. Lytic lesions: "punched out" on X-ray, NEGATIVE bone scan. Treat CRAB: bortezomib, lenalidomide + dexamethasone + autologous SCT if eligible.

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: Polycythemia vera — JAK2 V617F mutation and erythrocytosis pathogenesis
physicianTask: Patient Care: Diagnosis
memoryAnchor: PV: JAK2 V617F (>95%). ↑RBC mass + splenomegaly + low EPO (vs secondary polycythemia: ↑EPO). Aquagenic pruritus (basophil histamine after bathing). Plethora. Treat: phlebotomy ± hydroxyurea. Risk: thrombosis (hyperviscosity) + transformation to myelofibrosis or AML.
commonTrap: Students confuse PV with secondary polycythemia from high altitude or pulmonary disease — in secondary polycythemia, EPO is HIGH (appropriate response to hypoxia). In PV, EPO is LOW (JAK2 mutation makes erythroid precursors EPO-independent; feedback suppresses EPO production). Key: ↑RBC mass + ↓EPO = PV.
stem: A 58-year-old man has persistent facial redness, headaches, dizziness, and itching after bathing. Hematocrit is 62%. WBC 14,200/µL, platelets 520,000/µL, serum EPO is undetectable. Bone marrow biopsy shows panmyelosis (hypercellularity of all cell lines). JAK2 V617F mutation is detected in >90% of cells. Which mechanism explains the autonomous erythrocytosis?

options.text: Constitutively active JAK2 signaling mimics EPO receptor activation, driving EPO-independent erythroid proliferation
EPO receptor mutation causing hypersensitivity to normal EPO levels
Inappropriate EPO production from a renal cell carcinoma paraneoplastic syndrome
Hypoxia-inducible factor (HIF) stabilization from VHL mutation causing autonomous EPO transcription

explanation: The JAK2 V617F point mutation in the JH2 pseudokinase domain constitutively activates the JAK2 tyrosine kinase. JAK2 is a key downstream effector of the erythropoietin receptor (EPOR). The V617F mutation causes JAK2 to signal continuously without EPO receptor binding — mimicking continuous EPO stimulation and driving EPO-independent erythroid proliferation. Because endogenous EPO production is feedback-inhibited by the resulting erythrocytosis, serum EPO is undetectable. JAK2 V617F also activates megakaryocyte and myeloid lineages, explaining the panmyelosis (all cell line expansion). EPO receptor mutations (Chuvash polycythemia) are rare. VHL mutation (not JAK2) causes secondary erythrocytosis via HIF stabilization.
pearl: PV diagnostic criteria: ↑Hgb/Hct + JAK2 V617F + low EPO + BM panmyelosis. Aquagenic pruritus (basophil histamine after hot bath). Complications: DVT, stroke, Budd-Chiari, transformation to myelofibrosis/AML. Treat: phlebotomy (target Hct <45%), aspirin, hydroxyurea for high-risk.

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: Hepatocellular carcinoma — cirrhosis, AFP, and Barcelona staging
physicianTask: Patient Care: Diagnosis
memoryAnchor: HCC: cirrhosis (HBV, HCV, alcoholic, NASH) + ↑AFP + characteristic imaging (arterial enhancement + portal venous washout on CT/MRI). Surveillance: US every 6 months in cirrhosis. No biopsy needed if imaging characteristic + AFP >400. Risk of rupture and venous invasion.
commonTrap: Students always biopsy to confirm HCC — in cirrhotic patients with a liver mass >1 cm showing characteristic imaging features (arterial enhancement + washout) AND AFP >400 ng/mL, biopsy is NOT required. Biopsy risks seeding. The diagnosis is made radiologically per AASLD/EASL criteria.
stem: A 58-year-old man with cirrhosis from HCV has a 3-cm liver mass detected on surveillance ultrasound. AFP is 680 ng/mL. Triphasic CT shows the mass has arterial-phase enhancement and portal venous washout. No extrahepatic disease. Which diagnosis and management are most appropriate?

options.text: Hepatocellular carcinoma — diagnose without biopsy based on imaging criteria; refer for curative resection or ablation if criteria met
Hepatocellular carcinoma — must perform liver biopsy to confirm before any treatment
Metastatic colon cancer — AFP is often elevated in metastatic colon cancer to the liver
Hepatic hemangioma — characteristically enhances arterially and washes out in portal phase

explanation: Hepatocellular carcinoma can be diagnosed without tissue biopsy in cirrhotic patients when a mass >1 cm shows the characteristic imaging pattern of arterial-phase hypervascular enhancement followed by portal venous or delayed-phase washout on CT or MRI — because this pattern has >95% specificity for HCC in cirrhosis. AFP >400 ng/mL in this context strongly supports the diagnosis. Biopsy risks tumor seeding along the needle tract and is unnecessary when imaging criteria are met. Management is stage-dependent (Barcelona Clinic Liver Cancer staging): single tumor with preserved liver function → surgical resection or radiofrequency ablation. Multiple tumors → transarterial chemoembolization (TACE) or systemic therapy (sorafenib). Hemangiomas enhance peripherally with centripetal fill-in, not washout.
pearl: HCC diagnosis in cirrhosis: >1 cm mass + arterial enhancement + portal washout → HCC without biopsy (AASLD). AFP >400 supportive. BCLC staging guides treatment: resection/ablation (early), TACE (intermediate), sorafenib (advanced). Surveillance: abdominal US every 6 months in all cirrhotics.

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: Upper GI bleeding — peptic ulcer Forrest classification and endoscopic hemostasis
physicianTask: Patient Care: Clinical Interventions
memoryAnchor: UGIB: hematemesis, coffee-ground emesis, melena. Most common cause: peptic ulcer disease. Resuscitate → endoscopy within 24h. Forrest Ia (spurting) = endoscopic therapy. Forrest IIa (visible vessel) = endoscopic therapy. Forrest III (clean base) = discharge if stable. H. pylori eradication and PPI healing.
commonTrap: Students always give blood transfusion for any GI bleed — restrictive transfusion strategy (Hgb <7 g/dL threshold) is superior to liberal transfusion (Hgb <9 g/dL threshold) in non-cardiac UGIB, reducing mortality by avoiding volume overload and portal hypertension exacerbation in cirrhotics.
stem: A 52-year-old man with chronic NSAID use presents with hematemesis. Hemoglobin is 8.4 g/dL after resuscitation with 2L NS. He is hemodynamically stable. Endoscopy shows a 1.4-cm posterior duodenal ulcer with a visible vessel at the ulcer base (Forrest IIa). Which management approach is most appropriate?

options.text: Endoscopic hemostasis (bipolar electrocoagulation or hemoclip application) plus high-dose IV PPI infusion
Emergent surgical duodenostomy — endoscopic therapy is contraindicated for posterior duodenal ulcers
Discharge with oral PPI alone — Forrest IIa ulcers rarely rebleed after initial hemostasis
Transfuse 4 units of packed red blood cells immediately regardless of hemodynamic status

explanation: Forrest IIa (non-bleeding visible vessel) carries a 43–55% rebleeding risk without intervention. Endoscopic hemostasis (contact thermal therapy, hemoclip, or injection) combined with high-dose IV PPI (pantoprazole 80 mg bolus then 8 mg/hr for 72 hours) significantly reduces rebleeding risk, need for surgery, and mortality. PPI infusion raises gastric pH >6, preventing fibrinolysis at the ulcer base. Emergency surgery is reserved for endoscopic failure (persistent bleeding despite two endoscopic attempts). Posterior duodenal ulcers can erode into the gastroduodenal artery (major vessel), making endoscopic hemostasis especially important. A clean base ulcer (Forrest III) is at very low rebleed risk and can be managed without endoscopic therapy.
pearl: Forrest classification: Ia (spurting) + IIa (visible vessel) + IIb (adherent clot) = high rebleed risk → endoscopic treatment + IV PPI. Forrest IIc (flat pigment) + III (clean base) = low risk → oral PPI, consider early discharge. H. pylori testing and eradication after healing. NSAID cessation.

subject: Pharmacology
testedConcept: Myasthenia gravis — neuromuscular junction AChR antibody and pyridostigmine mechanism
physicianTask: Patient Care: Pharmacotherapy
memoryAnchor: MG: anti-AChR antibodies (85%) → ↓AChR at NMJ → fatigable weakness. Ptosis + diplopia earliest, bulbar (dysphagia, dysarthria), proximal limb weakness. EMG: decremental response on repetitive stimulation. Treat: pyridostigmine (AChE inhibitor). Thymectomy. Immunosuppression.
commonTrap: Students confuse MG with Lambert-Eaton syndrome — Lambert-Eaton (LES) is paraneoplastic (SCLC), anti-VGCC antibodies, IMPROVES with repetitive stimulation (incremental response, opposite of MG), and predominantly affects proximal legs. MG: decremental response on repetitive nerve stimulation, anti-AChR antibodies, worse with use.
stem: A 32-year-old woman has 4 months of progressive ptosis (worse in the evening), diplopia, and difficulty swallowing. She notices her voice becomes nasal after prolonged speaking. Repetitive nerve stimulation at 3 Hz shows a 40% decremental response in the compound muscle action potential. Anti-AChR antibody is positive. Which pharmacologic intervention addresses the underlying neuromuscular transmission defect?

options.text: Pyridostigmine — inhibits acetylcholinesterase, increasing ACh in the neuromuscular junction and compensating for reduced AChR density
3,4-Diaminopyridine — blocks potassium channels on the presynaptic terminal, increasing ACh release (Lambert-Eaton treatment)
Atropine — blocks muscarinic receptors to prevent excess ACh side effects at ganglia
Neostigmine intravenous — used for Tensilon test diagnosis and not for chronic management

explanation: In myasthenia gravis, anti-AChR antibodies reduce functional acetylcholine receptor density at the neuromuscular junction (NMJ) postsynaptic membrane. With fewer AChRs available, normal amounts of ACh released by the motor neuron produce insufficient end-plate potentials, causing muscle weakness. Pyridostigmine inhibits acetylcholinesterase (AChE) — the enzyme that degrades ACh in the synaptic cleft. By inhibiting AChE, more ACh molecules remain available to bind the reduced number of functional AChRs, partially compensating for the receptor loss. Pyridostigmine is longer-acting than neostigmine and suitable for oral chronic use. It does not treat the underlying autoimmune cause — immunosuppression (prednisone, azathioprine) and thymectomy are used for disease modification.
pearl: MG: AChE inhibitor (pyridostigmine) = symptomatic treatment. Disease modification: thymectomy + immunosuppression (prednisone, azathioprine, mycophenolate). Myasthenic crisis (respiratory failure): IVIG or plasmapheresis + ICU. Avoid: aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, magnesium, beta-blockers (all worsen NMJ transmission).

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: Normal pressure hydrocephalus — wet/wacky/wobbly triad and CSF diversion
physicianTask: Patient Care: Diagnosis
memoryAnchor: NPH: Wet (urinary incontinence) + Wacky (cognitive impairment) + Wobbly (magnetic gait, wide-based). CSF pressure is NORMAL. MRI: enlarged ventricles out of proportion to sulci. Treatable dementia → CSF shunting improves gait first, then incontinence, then cognition. Contrast with Alzheimer's (memory first, normal gait).
commonTrap: Students diagnose Alzheimer's for any elderly patient with cognitive impairment — NPH is a treatable cause of dementia. The classic triad of magnetic gait (as if feet are stuck to the floor, wide-based small steps) PLUS urinary incontinence PLUS cognitive impairment in an elderly patient warrants MRI and lumbar puncture. Gait improvement after large-volume LP (30 mL CSF drainage) predicts shunt response.
stem: A 78-year-old man has 18 months of progressive memory impairment, urinary incontinence, and a wide-based shuffling gait described as 'magnetic' (difficulty initiating steps, as if feet are stuck). MRI shows enlarged lateral and third ventricles out of proportion to cortical sulcal enlargement. Opening pressure on LP is 145 mmHg (normal). After removing 30 mL of CSF, gait immediately improves. Which treatable condition is most likely?

options.text: Normal pressure hydrocephalus — CSF shunting improves the triad, especially gait
Alzheimer disease — ventricular enlargement results from cortical atrophy (ex vacuo hydrocephalus)
Communicating hydrocephalus from meningitis — CSF pressure would be elevated
Frontotemporal dementia — behavioral symptoms and language impairment predominate

explanation: Normal pressure hydrocephalus (NPH) presents with the classic triad of gait apraxia (magnetic gait), urinary incontinence, and dementia. Despite enlarged ventricles, CSF opening pressure is normal on LP. The enlarged ventricles compress the periventricular white matter tracts responsible for leg movement (the fibers serving the legs run closest to the ventricles), explaining why gait is affected most prominently and early. The large-volume LP tap test (removing 30 mL CSF) with subsequent gait improvement is a strong predictor of response to CSF diversion (ventriculoperitoneal shunt). NPH is a potentially reversible cause of dementia. Gait improves first after shunting, then incontinence, then cognition (least reliably). Ex vacuo hydrocephalus from cortical atrophy in Alzheimer shows gyral atrophy with proportionally enlarged sulci — not disproportionate ventricular enlargement.
pearl: NPH triad: magnetic gait + urinary incontinence + dementia. Normal opening pressure. Ventricles enlarged out of proportion to sulci on MRI. Large-volume LP tap test: gait improvement predicts shunt response. Treat: VP shunt. Gait recovers best (white matter compression). Earliest improvement = gait, latest = cognition.

subject: Physiology
testedConcept: Syncope — differential diagnosis and neurocardiogenic vs cardiac syncope features
physicianTask: Patient Care: Diagnosis
memoryAnchor: Syncope types: Neurocardiogenic (vasovagal) = triggered (prolonged standing/heat/emotion) + prodrome (nausea/diaphoresis/pallor) + recovery without confusion. Cardiac = sudden, no prodrome, after exertion → red flag for HCM/ARVD/channelopathy. Orthostatic = postural change, ≥20 mmHg SBP drop. Carotid sinus hypersensitivity = pressure on neck.
commonTrap: Students diagnose vasovagal syncope for all syncope in young patients — exertional syncope (during exercise, not after) in a young person is cardiac syncope until proven otherwise. It suggests hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, ARVD, long QT syndrome, or Wolff-Parkinson-White. These require urgent cardiac evaluation and can cause sudden cardiac death.
stem: A 19-year-old male basketball player collapsed suddenly during a game without prodrome, regaining consciousness within 30 seconds. His father died suddenly at age 42 during exercise. ECG shows prominent septal Q waves in V5–V6 and increased voltage in lateral leads. He has a harsh late-peaking systolic murmur that increases with Valsalva. Which is the most likely cause of syncope?

options.text: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy — dynamic LVOT obstruction causing exercise-induced cardiac syncope
Vasovagal syncope — emotional response to competitive athletic stress
Aortic stenosis — fixed outflow obstruction causing effort syncope in a teenager
Orthostatic hypotension — volume depletion from perspiration during exercise

explanation: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy is strongly suggested by exertional syncope without prodrome, a family history of sudden death, septal hypertrophy on ECG, and a systolic murmur that increases when preload falls during Valsalva. Exercise can worsen dynamic left ventricular outflow obstruction and can also provoke ventricular arrhythmias. Vasovagal syncope usually has a prodrome, and fixed aortic stenosis would require a structural valve lesion rather than the dynamic findings described here.
pearl: HCM red flags: exertional syncope + family history sudden death + septal hypertrophy + LVOT murmur worsening with Valsalva. Sudden death risk: ventricular arrhythmias from disorganized myofibers. Workup: echo + genetic testing + Holter. Treat: beta-blocker or verapamil for symptoms; ICD for high-risk; alcohol septal ablation or myectomy for refractory obstruction. Activity restriction.

subject: Pharmacology
testedConcept: Acute decompensated heart failure — IV diuresis, vasodilators, and afterload reduction
physicianTask: Patient Care: Clinical Interventions
memoryAnchor: ADHF: congested (wet) → furosemide IV (diuresis). Low CO (cold) → vasopressors or inotropes (dobutamine). Warm wet → IV diuretics + vasodilators (nitroglycerin → reduces preload/afterload). Cold wet = worst (cardiogenic shock) → inotropes + cautious diuresis. BiPAP reduces work of breathing.
commonTrap: Do not newly initiate or rapidly increase a beta blocker during active pulmonary congestion. However, a chronic evidence-based beta blocker is usually continued when perfusion is adequate and there is no shock, severe bradycardia, or other contraindication.
stem: A 72-year-old woman with known HFrEF (EF 25%) presents with acute dyspnea at rest, orthopnea, and bilateral crackles to the mid-lungs. BP 158/94, HR 108, SpO2 88% on room air. CXR shows pulmonary vascular congestion and bilateral pleural effusions. She is warm peripherally. Which immediate treatment targets her most critical pathophysiologic problem?

options.text: Intravenous furosemide 40–80 mg to relieve pulmonary congestion via diuresis and early venodilation
Carvedilol 12.5 mg oral — initiate beta-blocker to reduce adrenergic stress on the failing heart
Digoxin 0.5 mg intravenous — positive inotrope to acutely improve ejection fraction
Metoprolol tartrate IV — faster-acting beta-blocker to reduce heart rate and improve diastolic filling

explanation: This patient is warm and wet: perfusion is preserved, but pulmonary congestion and elevated filling pressures are causing hypoxemia and dyspnea. Intravenous loop diuresis directly treats the congestion; intravenous vasodilator therapy can also be considered because she is hypertensive. A beta blocker should not be newly initiated or up-titrated during active decompensation. Chronic beta-blocker therapy is generally continued when the patient is perfusing adequately and has no shock, severe bradycardia, or other contraindication.
pearl: ADHF warm-wet: IV loop diuretic (furosemide) first-line. Add IV nitroglycerin if hypertensive. BiPAP reduces work of breathing. Do NOT start beta-blockers in acute decompensation (may cause cardiogenic shock). Cold-wet (cardiogenic shock): inotropes (dobutamine) + diuresis. Target: symptom relief, normovolemia, optimize chronic HF medications after stabilization.

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: Type 4 renal tubular acidosis - hypoaldosteronism causing hyperkalemic non-anion gap metabolic acidosis
physicianTask: Medical Knowledge: Applying Foundational Science Concepts
memoryAnchor: Type 4 RTA = hyperkalemia. Low aldosterone effect reduces distal sodium reabsorption, reducing potassium and hydrogen secretion.
commonTrap: Students confuse type 4 RTA with distal type 1 RTA. Type 1 has hypokalemia and urine pH above 5.5; type 4 has hyperkalemia from impaired aldosterone effect.
stem: A 63-year-old man with long-standing type 2 diabetes and CKD stage 3 is evaluated for weakness. BP is 138/82 mmHg. Labs show Na+ 137 mEq/L, K+ 5.9 mEq/L, Cl- 112 mEq/L, HCO3- 16 mEq/L, BUN 32 mg/dL, creatinine 1.8 mg/dL, glucose 146 mg/dL, and anion gap 9. Urine pH is 5.2. Plasma renin activity and aldosterone are both low. Which mechanism best explains this acid-base disorder?

options.text: Reduced aldosterone effect decreases distal sodium reabsorption and impairs potassium and hydrogen secretion
Defective proximal bicarbonate reabsorption causes bicarbonaturia with severe potassium wasting
Inability of alpha-intercalated cells to secrete hydrogen ions keeps urine pH inappropriately alkaline
Excess lactate production from tissue hypoperfusion increases the serum anion gap

explanation: This patient has type 4 renal tubular acidosis: hyperkalemia plus non-anion gap metabolic acidosis in a diabetic patient with low renin and low aldosterone. Reduced aldosterone effect lowers ENaC-mediated sodium reabsorption in principal cells, which normally creates a lumen-negative electrical gradient. Without that gradient, potassium secretion by principal cells and hydrogen secretion by alpha-intercalated cells fall, causing hyperkalemia and metabolic acidosis. Urine pH can be less than 5.5 because the collecting duct can still acidify urine when distal sodium delivery and aldosterone signaling are impaired. Proximal type 2 RTA causes bicarbonate wasting and usually hypokalemia. Distal type 1 RTA causes impaired H+ secretion with urine pH above 5.5 and hypokalemia. Lactic acidosis would increase the anion gap.
pearl: Type 4 RTA: diabetic nephropathy, ACE inhibitors, ARBs, spironolactone, trimethoprim, or NSAIDs. Pattern: hyperkalemia plus non-anion gap metabolic acidosis plus low aldosterone effect.

subject: Immunology
testedConcept: Chronic granulomatous disease - NADPH oxidase defect and catalase-positive infections
physicianTask: Patient Care: Diagnosis
memoryAnchor: CGD = cannot generate reactive oxygen species. Catalase-positive organisms destroy their own hydrogen peroxide.
commonTrap: Students choose myeloperoxidase deficiency whenever oxidative burst is mentioned. MPO deficiency has a normal DHR oxidative burst and is often mild.
stem: A 4-year-old boy has recurrent pneumonia, suppurative lymphadenitis, and a liver abscess. Temperature is 39.1 C and WBC count is 15,800/uL with 70% neutrophils. Culture grows Serratia marcescens. His maternal uncle died in childhood from recurrent bacterial infections. CBC shows normal neutrophil count. Dihydrorhodamine flow cytometry shows markedly decreased fluorescence after stimulation. Which defect most likely explains these findings?

options.text: NADPH oxidase deficiency causing impaired respiratory burst in phagocytes
Myeloperoxidase deficiency preventing conversion of hydrogen peroxide into hypochlorous acid
C5 through C9 complement deficiency causing impaired membrane attack complex formation
Leukocyte adhesion molecule defect causing failure of neutrophil extravasation into infected tissue

explanation: Chronic granulomatous disease is caused by defective NADPH oxidase, most commonly X-linked, leading to impaired respiratory burst in neutrophils and macrophages. Dihydrorhodamine testing shows decreased fluorescence because reactive oxygen species are not generated. Patients develop recurrent infections with catalase-positive organisms such as Staphylococcus aureus, Serratia, Burkholderia, Nocardia, and Aspergillus because those organisms degrade their own hydrogen peroxide, preventing phagocytes from using microbial hydrogen peroxide for killing. Myeloperoxidase deficiency affects conversion of hydrogen peroxide to hypochlorous acid but has a normal oxidative burst test. C5-C9 deficiency causes recurrent Neisseria infections. Leukocyte adhesion deficiency causes delayed umbilical cord separation, neutrophilia, and absent pus formation.
pearl: CGD: NADPH oxidase defect, abnormal DHR test, catalase-positive infections. Catalase-positive organisms include Staph aureus, Serratia, Burkholderia, Nocardia, and Aspergillus.

subject: Pharmacology
testedConcept: Diabetic ketoacidosis - potassium shift and insulin therapy before potassium correction
physicianTask: Patient Care: Clinical Interventions
memoryAnchor: DKA total body potassium is low even when serum potassium is normal or high. Insulin drives K into cells.
commonTrap: Students start insulin immediately whenever DKA is recognized. If potassium is low, insulin can trigger fatal hypokalemia and arrhythmia.
stem: A 24-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes has vomiting, abdominal pain, and confusion. Temperature is 37.2 C, BP 94/58 mmHg, HR 126/min, and respirations are deep and rapid. Labs show glucose 514 mg/dL, Na+ 130 mEq/L, K+ 3.1 mEq/L, HCO3- 8 mEq/L, pH 7.12, anion gap 28, and positive serum beta-hydroxybutyrate. After isotonic saline is started, what is the next best step?

options.text: Give intravenous potassium before starting insulin to prevent worsening hypokalemia
Start regular insulin infusion immediately because hyperglycemia is the primary life-threatening problem
Administer sodium bicarbonate immediately because the arterial pH is below physiologic range
Give subcutaneous long-acting insulin only and avoid intravenous fluids because sodium is low

explanation: This patient has diabetic ketoacidosis with low serum potassium. In DKA, osmotic diuresis and vomiting cause total body potassium depletion, while acidosis and insulin deficiency shift potassium out of cells, making serum potassium appear less depleted than the body stores really are. If serum K+ is below 3.3 mEq/L, potassium must be replaced before insulin is started because insulin activates Na+/K+ ATPase and drives potassium into cells, potentially causing severe hypokalemia, weakness, and fatal ventricular arrhythmias. Insulin is essential once potassium is safe. Bicarbonate is reserved for extreme acidosis, typically pH below 6.9. Hyponatremia is dilutional and corrects with fluids and insulin.
pearl: DKA order: isotonic fluids first, check potassium, then insulin if K+ is at least 3.3. If K+ below 3.3, replace potassium before insulin. Total body potassium is depleted even if serum potassium is normal or high.

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: Subarachnoid hemorrhage - berry aneurysm rupture and CT-negative lumbar puncture xanthochromia
physicianTask: Patient Care: Laboratory and Diagnostic Studies
memoryAnchor: Thunderclap headache plus neck stiffness equals SAH until proven otherwise. CT can miss delayed SAH; LP shows xanthochromia.
commonTrap: Students stop after a negative noncontrast CT. Sensitivity falls with time, so persistent high suspicion requires lumbar puncture or vascular imaging.
stem: A 41-year-old woman develops the sudden worst headache of her life during exercise, followed by vomiting and neck stiffness. BP is 172/98 mmHg and temperature is 37.0 C. Noncontrast head CT performed 18 hours after symptom onset shows no acute blood. Neurologic exam is normal except photophobia. Lumbar puncture shows opening pressure 280 mm H2O, RBCs in tubes 1 and 4, and xanthochromia. Which diagnosis is most likely?

options.text: Aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage from rupture of a saccular berry aneurysm
Migraine with aura causing sterile meningeal irritation and traumatic lumbar puncture
Bacterial meningitis causing neutrophilic CSF inflammation with low glucose concentration
Epidural hematoma from middle meningeal artery rupture after occult head trauma

explanation: A thunderclap headache with vomiting, neck stiffness, elevated opening pressure, persistent RBCs across CSF tubes, and xanthochromia indicates subarachnoid hemorrhage. Berry aneurysm rupture is the classic cause of spontaneous subarachnoid hemorrhage in adults. Noncontrast CT is highly sensitive early but sensitivity decreases as time passes, so lumbar puncture is appropriate when suspicion remains high after a negative CT. Xanthochromia reflects hemoglobin breakdown in CSF and distinguishes true subarachnoid hemorrhage from a traumatic tap. Migraine does not cause xanthochromia. Bacterial meningitis would show fever, neutrophilic pleocytosis, high protein, and low glucose. Epidural hematoma follows trauma and causes lens-shaped extra-axial bleeding.
pearl: SAH diagnosis: sudden thunderclap headache. CT first; if negative and suspicion remains, LP for xanthochromia. Complications include rebleeding, vasospasm, hydrocephalus, and SIADH. Treat aneurysm with clipping or coiling and give nimodipine for vasospasm prevention.

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: Post-streptococcal GN — immune complex mechanism and alternative complement pathway
physicianTask: Medical Knowledge: Applying Foundational Science Concepts
memoryAnchor: PSGN = subepithelial humps on EM + low C3, normal C4 = alternative complement pathway. 1–3 weeks post-pharyngitis.
commonTrap: Students pick IgA nephropathy because it also follows infection, but IgA deposition is mesangial and occurs hours to days after mucosal infection — not 3 weeks post-pharyngitis with subepithelial humps.
stem: A 19-year-old man presents with cola-colored urine, periorbital edema, and hypertension 3 weeks after a sore throat. UA shows RBC casts and proteinuria. C3 is low, C4 is normal. ASO titer is elevated. Biopsy shows subepithelial electron-dense "humps." Which immune mechanism best explains this finding?

options.text: Deposition of streptococcal antigen-antibody complexes in the subepithelial space
Anti-GBM antibodies cross-reacting with type IV collagen
T cell-mediated cytotoxic injury to podocytes
IgA complex deposition in the mesangium following mucosal infection

explanation: Post-streptococcal GN: streptococcal cationic antigens migrate to the subepithelial space and are bound by circulating antibodies forming immune complexes (the "humps"). This activates complement via the alternative pathway, consuming C3 while C4 stays normal. Presents 1–3 weeks post-pharyngitis, nephritic syndrome, excellent prognosis in children.
pearl: PSGN: subepithelial humps on EM, low C3 normal C4 (alternative pathway). 1–3 weeks post-pharyngitis, 3–6 weeks post-impetigo. Excellent prognosis in children.

optionExplanations.A: Nephritogenic streptococcal antigens (nephritis-associated plasmin receptor, streptokinase) migrate to the subepithelial space — between podocytes and the outer GBM surface — and are bound by circulating antibodies in situ. This forms the characteristic "humps" visible on electron microscopy. The resulting immune complexes activate complement via the alternative pathway, consuming C3 while sparing C4.
optionExplanations.B: Anti-GBM disease (Goodpasture syndrome) involves IgG antibodies targeting the alpha-3 chain of type IV collagen in the GBM, producing LINEAR IgG deposits on immunofluorescence — not granular subepithelial humps. It causes rapidly progressive GN with pulmonary hemorrhage and has no temporal relationship to streptococcal infection.
optionExplanations.C: T cell-mediated podocyte injury is the mechanism of minimal change disease, which presents as nephrotic syndrome (massive proteinuria, edema, hypoalbuminemia) — not nephritic syndrome with RBC casts and hematuria. Electron microscopy in MCD shows diffuse podocyte foot process effacement without immune deposits.
optionExplanations.D: IgA nephropathy (Berger disease) is triggered by mucosal infections and typically presents within hours to days after an upper respiratory illness — a much shorter latency than PSGN's 1–3 weeks. IgA deposits in the MESANGIUM, not subepithelially, and complement is typically normal. It is the most common glomerulopathy worldwide but does not show subepithelial humps.

subject: Physiology
testedConcept: Severe aortic stenosis — hemodynamic consequences (increased afterload, concentric LVH, diastolic dysfunction)
physicianTask: Medical Knowledge: Applying Foundational Science Concepts
memoryAnchor: AS = afterload ↑ → concentric LVH → diastolic dysfunction. Classic triad: Syncope + Angina + Dyspnea (SAD). Mean gradient >40 = severe.
commonTrap: Students pick eccentric dilation (C) because they confuse the LV response to AFTERLOAD (concentric hypertrophy in AS) with VOLUME overload (eccentric dilation in AR) — the key is matching the type of overload to the compensatory mechanism.
stem: A 70-year-old woman has severe aortic stenosis (valve area 0.7 cm², mean gradient 52 mmHg). She is in cardiogenic shock. Which of the following hemodynamic changes best characterizes severe aortic stenosis?

options.text: Increased left ventricular afterload with concentric hypertrophy and diastolic dysfunction
Decreased left ventricular preload from reduced venous return
Increased left ventricular preload with eccentric dilation and systolic dysfunction
Reduced coronary perfusion pressure from low aortic diastolic pressure alone

explanation: Severe aortic stenosis creates a fixed outflow obstruction, dramatically increasing LV afterload. In response, the LV undergoes concentric hypertrophy (increased wall thickness, normal or reduced chamber size). The hypertrophied, stiff LV develops diastolic dysfunction. Reduced coronary perfusion occurs because hypertrophied myocardium has increased oxygen demand with reduced subendocardial perfusion — a dual mechanism for ischemia in AS without obstructive CAD.
pearl: Severe AS: increased afterload → concentric LVH → diastolic dysfunction. Subendocardial ischemia from: (1) increased O2 demand by hypertrophied LV, (2) reduced perfusion time (long ejection phase). Classic: syncope + angina + dyspnea = severe AS triad.

optionExplanations.A: Severe aortic stenosis creates fixed outflow obstruction at the aortic valve level, requiring the left ventricle to generate markedly elevated pressures to eject blood. This pressure overload (increased afterload) triggers the Law of Laplace compensation: to maintain wall stress at manageable levels, the LV wall thickens concentrically (increased wall thickness, preserved or reduced chamber volume). Concentric LVH normalizes wall stress but increases myocardial oxygen demand. The hypertrophied myocardium becomes stiff (reduced compliance) — filling is impaired, and diastolic dysfunction follows. AS requires simultaneous high LV systolic pressure and dependence on atrial kick for filling; loss of atrial contraction (as in new AF) can precipitate acute decompensation.
optionExplanations.B: Decreased LV preload from reduced venous return is a feature of distributive states (sepsis, hemorrhage, Addison crisis) or obstruction of venous return (cardiac tamponade, tension pneumothorax). In aortic stenosis, the LV is actually volume-loaded (high end-diastolic volume due to impaired emptying) and depends on maintained preload to compensate for the outflow obstruction. Reducing preload in severe AS (e.g., with aggressive diuresis or nitrates) can precipitate a catastrophic drop in cardiac output because the failing, hypertrophied LV depends on adequate filling to generate sufficient pressure gradient across the stenotic valve. This is why vasodilators are generally avoided in severe AS.
optionExplanations.C: Eccentric hypertrophy (increased wall thickness WITH increased chamber volume, sometimes called dilation) is the LV response to VOLUME overload — as in aortic regurgitation or mitral regurgitation — not pressure overload. In chronic AR, the LV must accommodate the regurgitant volume in addition to the forward stroke volume, causing progressive LV dilation. The LV generates high wall stress through chamber enlargement; by Laplace, it compensates by adding sarcomeres in series (eccentric hypertrophy — increased wall thickness proportional to increased radius). Aortic stenosis causes pressure overload, not volume overload, and the appropriate response is concentric hypertrophy (sarcomeres added in parallel), not eccentric dilation.
optionExplanations.D: Subendocardial ischemia in severe AS occurs through two mechanisms simultaneously — and neither is simply "low aortic diastolic pressure alone." First, the massively hypertrophied LV has dramatically increased oxygen demand (wall stress × heart rate × contractility). Second, coronary perfusion is compromised: the prolonged LV systolic ejection time (AS requires more time to open and eject through the narrow valve) compresses the subendocardial coronary vessels during systole, shortening the diastolic filling time available for coronary perfusion. Additionally, when LV end-diastolic pressure rises (diastolic dysfunction), the diastolic pressure gradient driving subendocardial perfusion falls. This dual mechanism explains why AS patients develop angina even without obstructive CAD. Aortic diastolic pressure may actually be preserved in AS because the high-resistance valve maintains central aortic diastolic pressure.

subject: Physiology
testedConcept: Hepatic encephalopathy — ammonia-glutamine mechanism and astrocyte swelling
physicianTask: Medical Knowledge: Applying Foundational Science Concepts
memoryAnchor: HE: portal shunting → ammonia bypasses liver → astrocytes convert NH3→glutamine → osmotic astrocyte swelling → cerebral edema. Lactulose: acidifies colon, traps NH4+. Rifaximin: reduces gut bacteria.
commonTrap: Students pick direct cortical neuron toxicity from ammonia — ammonia is metabolized in astrocytes (the only brain cell type capable of ammonia detoxification), causing glutamine accumulation and astrocyte swelling. Neurons are secondarily affected.
stem: A 52-year-old man with cirrhosis and spontaneous bacterial peritonitis develops progressive confusion and asterixis over 48 hours. Serum ammonia is 162 µmol/L. Which mechanism best explains how ammonia causes neurological dysfunction in hepatic encephalopathy?

options.text: Astrocyte metabolism of ammonia to glutamine causes osmotic astrocyte swelling and cerebral edema
Ammonia directly destroys cortical neurons through oxidative mitochondrial damage
Ammonia crosses the blood-brain barrier and directly binds GABA-A receptors
Ammonia inhibits choline acetyltransferase, reducing acetylcholine synthesis

explanation: In hepatic encephalopathy, portosystemic shunting allows intestinal ammonia to bypass hepatic urea cycle detoxification. In the brain, astrocytes (the only cells capable of ammonia detoxification) convert ammonia to glutamine via glutamine synthetase. Excess glutamine is osmotically active and accumulates intracellularly, causing osmotic astrocyte swelling and cerebral edema. This impairs neuronal signaling and elevates intracranial pressure. Treatment: lactulose (acidifies colon → traps ammonia as NH4+, non-absorbable) + rifaximin (reduces urease-producing gut bacteria).
pearl: Hepatic encephalopathy: NH3 → astrocyte glutamine → osmotic swelling → cerebral edema. Asterixis = flapping tremor. Lactulose + rifaximin. Triggers: GI bleed, infection, constipation, hypokalemia, dehydration.

optionExplanations.A: Astrocyte metabolism of ammonia to glutamine is the central mechanism of hepatic encephalopathy neuropathology. Astrocytes express glutamine synthetase — the only brain enzyme capable of ammonia detoxification — which converts ammonia (NH3) + glutamate → glutamine using ATP. In cirrhosis with portosystemic shunting, ammonia bypasses the hepatic urea cycle and reaches the brain in toxic concentrations. Astrocytes convert this excess ammonia to glutamine, but glutamine is osmotically active and trapped intracellularly (astrocytes lack sufficient glutaminase to catabolize it rapidly). The resulting intracellular osmotic load draws water into astrocytes, causing swelling (cytotoxic edema). This astrocyte swelling compresses neurons, disrupts gap junction communication, impairs release of neurotransmitter precursors, and raises intracranial pressure in severe cases (grade IV HE). The characteristic electroencephalographic change (diffuse slowing, triphasic waves) and asterixis (flapping tremor from metabolic encephalopathy) result from disrupted neurotransmission in this environment.
optionExplanations.B: Direct cortical neuron toxicity is a secondary rather than primary mechanism in HE. Neurons themselves have minimal capacity to metabolize ammonia — they lack glutamine synthetase at significant levels. Neurons are therefore exposed to elevated ammonia concentrations in the extracellular space, but they are protected to some degree by astrocytes metabolizing ammonia first. Ammonia does impair the tricarboxylic acid cycle in neurons (inhibiting alpha-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase, impairing GABA neurotransmitter synthesis, and disrupting mitochondrial function) as a secondary effect, but the direct neurotoxicity is downstream from the astrocyte swelling and edema that disrupts the neuronal microenvironment. This distractor tests whether students know that the primary cellular target is the astrocyte, not the neuron.
optionExplanations.C: Ammonia crossing the blood-brain barrier and binding GABA-A receptors directly is not the mechanism. Ammonia is a small uncharged molecule (NH3 form) that does cross the BBB readily, and ammonia does upregulate central GABA-A receptor sensitivity — partly explaining why benzodiazepines precipitate or worsen HE. However, ammonia itself does not bind GABA-A receptors; it modulates GABA-ergic tone through indirect mechanisms (increasing GABA synthesis, altering neurosteroid production). The GABA hypothesis of HE is a partial explanation for the sedation, but the primary mechanism of neurotoxicity is astrocyte glutamine-mediated osmotic swelling, not direct GABA-A receptor binding by ammonia.
optionExplanations.D: Choline acetyltransferase (ChAT) catalyzes acetylcholine synthesis from choline and acetyl-CoA. Its inhibition would reduce cholinergic neurotransmission, causing a cholinergic deficit syndrome — associated with Alzheimer disease, not hepatic encephalopathy. Hepatic encephalopathy does not selectively impair cholinergic neurotransmission; it causes global neurotransmitter dysfunction as a consequence of astrocyte swelling and disrupted brain metabolism. Ammonia impairs the TCA cycle and can reduce glutamate (excitatory) and GABA (inhibitory) neurotransmitter synthesis, but inhibition of ChAT is not a recognized mechanism in HE pathophysiology.

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: Preeclampsia — defective placentation and sFlt-1 causing systemic endothelial dysfunction
physicianTask: Medical Knowledge: Applying Foundational Science Concepts
memoryAnchor: Preeclampsia: defective spiral artery remodeling → ischemic placenta → ↑sFlt-1 → sequesters VEGF → endothelial dysfunction → HTN + proteinuria. Only cure: DELIVERY. Severe features: BP>160/110, thrombocytopenia, elevated LFTs, renal insufficiency, pulmonary edema.
commonTrap: Students pick elevated aldosterone as the mechanism of hypertension in preeclampsia — aldosterone is actually LOW in preeclampsia because elevated BP suppresses RAAS. The hypertension is driven by anti-angiogenic sFlt-1 causing endothelial dysfunction, not by volume overload.
stem: A 26-year-old woman at 32 weeks gestation develops BP 158/104 mmHg, proteinuria, severe headache, and epigastric pain. Earlier uterine artery Doppler showed persistent high-resistance flow, and placental pathology now shows shallow trophoblast invasion of maternal spiral arteries. Which downstream molecular mechanism links this placental abnormality to both maternal hypertension and glomerular endotheliosis?

options.text: Defective trophoblast invasion causes ischemic placenta to release sFlt-1, sequestering VEGF and causing systemic endothelial dysfunction
Placental progesterone deficiency reduces nitric oxide production and increases peripheral vascular resistance
Glomerulonephritis from immune complex deposition causes proteinuria independently of endothelial dysfunction
Elevated aldosterone from placental renin causes hypertension and nephrotic-range proteinuria

explanation: Preeclampsia: defective cytotrophoblast invasion during placentation prevents normal spiral artery remodeling. The resulting ischemic, hypoxic placenta upregulates sFlt-1 (soluble fms-like tyrosine kinase 1), a truncated VEGF receptor that acts as a decoy, sequestering free VEGF and PlGF in maternal circulation. This creates relative VEGF deficiency throughout the maternal vasculature, causing endothelial dysfunction: loss of vasodilatory nitric oxide → hypertension; glomerular endothelial swelling (endotheliosis) → proteinuria; disrupted blood-brain barrier → headache, seizures (eclampsia). Delivery resolves the source of sFlt-1.
pearl: Preeclampsia mechanism: defective placentation → ischemic placenta → ↑sFlt-1 → VEGF sequestration → endothelial dysfunction (HTN + proteinuria). sFlt-1/PlGF ratio is a validated biomarker. Only cure = delivery. Prevention: low-dose aspirin from 12 weeks in high-risk patients.

optionExplanations.A: Defective cytotrophoblast invasion fails to transform spiral arteries from high-resistance muscular vessels into low-resistance channels. The resulting ischemic-hypoxic placenta upregulates sFlt-1, which circulates in maternal blood and binds free VEGF and PlGF (placental growth factor). Without VEGF signaling, endothelial cells cannot produce adequate nitric oxide, prostacyclin, and other vasodilatory mediators — leading to systemic vasoconstriction (hypertension) and glomerular endotheliosis (proteinuria). The dramatically elevated sFlt-1 and depressed PlGF levels confirmed in this patient are both a consequence of the placental ischemia and the molecular mechanism driving the clinical syndrome.
optionExplanations.B: Progesterone does contribute to vasodilation during normal pregnancy, but progesterone deficiency is not specifically identified as the mechanism of preeclampsia. The primary upstream driver is sFlt-1-mediated VEGF sequestration from the ischemic placenta. Nitric oxide production is indeed reduced in preeclampsia, but this occurs downstream of VEGF deficiency — not from progesterone deficiency. Confusing downstream mediators (reduced NO) with upstream mechanisms (sFlt-1 → VEGF deficiency) is a common conceptual error.
optionExplanations.C: Glomerular pathology in preeclampsia is specifically "glomerular endotheliosis" — swelling and vacuolization of glomerular endothelial cells that narrows capillary lumina, disrupts the endothelial fenestrations (normally filtering pores), and increases glomerular permeability to albumin. This is an endothelial dysfunction lesion driven by VEGF deficiency, not an immune complex-mediated glomerulonephritis. There are no immune deposits in preeclampsia — immunofluorescence of the biopsy in preeclampsia shows no granular IgG, IgA, IgM, or complement deposits.
optionExplanations.D: In normal pregnancy, the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS) is physiologically activated to accommodate the 40–50% expansion of intravascular volume. In preeclampsia, the elevated maternal blood pressure paradoxically suppresses renin secretion from the juxtaglomerular apparatus — aldosterone levels are actually lower in preeclampsia than in normal pregnancy. The hypertension is driven by anti-angiogenic endothelial dysfunction, not by aldosterone-mediated volume expansion. Hyperaldosteronism (Conn syndrome) causes hypertension with hypokalemia — not the hyponatremia and hyperkalemia of preeclampsia.

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: Acute tubular necrosis — muddy brown granular casts and ischemic vs nephrotoxic causes
physicianTask: Patient Care: Diagnosis
memoryAnchor: ATN casts: muddy brown granular casts from sloughed tubular cells. Causes: ischemia (shock, prolonged hypotension) or nephrotoxins (aminoglycosides, contrast, myoglobin/hemoglobin). FENa >2% (vs prerenal <1%). Supportive care; most recover.
commonTrap: Students confuse ATN with prerenal azotemia — prerenal has concentrated urine (FeNa <1%, UNa <20), no casts. ATN has isosthenuric dilute urine (FeNa >2%, UNa >40) with muddy brown casts from tubular cell death and sloughing.
stem: A 55-year-old man underwent emergency repair of a ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm. Intraoperatively, cross-clamp time was 85 minutes with periods of hypotension. On postoperative day 2, urine output is 180 mL/day. Creatinine has risen from 0.9 to 4.8 mg/dL. UA shows muddy brown granular casts. FENa is 3.2%. Which diagnosis and expected course are most accurate?

options.text: Acute tubular necrosis from ischemia — most cases recover with supportive care
Prerenal azotemia — volume resuscitation will rapidly restore creatinine to baseline
Acute interstitial nephritis — requires immediate high-dose corticosteroids
Renal artery thrombosis — emergent revascularization required within 6 hours

explanation: Acute tubular necrosis from ischemia is the diagnosis. Prolonged renal ischemia from cross-clamping and hypotension during aortic surgery causes tubular epithelial cell death. The pathognomonic finding is muddy brown granular casts — composed of sloughed tubular epithelial cells and their debris. FENa >2% confirms intrinsic renal failure (tubular dysfunction prevents sodium conservation). Most ischemic ATN recovers with supportive care (fluid management, dialysis if needed for severe uremia or volume overload) over 1–3 weeks as tubular cells regenerate. Prerenal azotemia has FENa <1% and no casts.
pearl: ATN: muddy brown granular casts (sloughed tubular cells) + FENa >2% + UNa >40 + isosthenuric urine. Ischemic causes: prolonged hypotension, surgery, sepsis. Nephrotoxic: aminoglycosides (proximal tubule), contrast (medullary ischemia), myoglobinuria. Supportive care; renal recovery expected in most.

optionExplanations.A: The combination of prolonged renal ischemia from aortic cross-clamping, intraoperative hypotension, marked creatinine rise, muddy brown granular casts, and FENa >2% confirms ischemic acute tubular necrosis. Tubular cell necrosis begins within minutes of severe ischemia and is histologically characterized by loss of brush border, nuclear pyknosis, cell sloughing into the tubular lumen (forming granular casts), and basement membrane denudation. Tubular cells regenerate from surviving stem cells over 1–3 weeks; dialysis may be needed temporarily. Outcome is generally favorable when the precipitating ischemic event is reversed.
optionExplanations.B: Prerenal azotemia reflects reduced renal perfusion with structurally intact tubules that avidly retain sodium and water. FeNa <1%, urine sodium <20 mEq/L, concentrated urine (osmolality >500 mOsm/kg), and BUN:Cr ratio >20 characterize prerenal. Critically, there are NO casts in prerenal — the tubular epithelium is intact and functional. This patient has FENa 3.2% and muddy brown casts, ruling out prerenal. Volume resuscitation alone will not reverse ATN.
optionExplanations.C: Acute interstitial nephritis (AIN) presents with the triad of fever, rash, and eosinophilia following drug exposure (beta-lactams, NSAIDs, PPIs) or infection. Urinalysis shows WBC casts and eosinophiluria (though eosinophiluria is insensitive). Granular or muddy brown casts are not typical. AIN can be treated with corticosteroids if drug discontinuation does not lead to recovery. This patient has no drug exposure history consistent with AIN, and muddy brown casts firmly point to ATN.
optionExplanations.D: Renal artery thrombosis or embolism typically causes sudden onset flank pain, hematuria, elevated LDH (from infarction), and imaging showing absence of renal cortical enhancement (wedge-shaped infarcts). Urine sediment would show few or no casts because tubular function is not the primary pathology. Emergent revascularization is only beneficial within the first 6 hours of acute occlusion. The muddy brown casts and gradual rise in creatinine starting on postoperative day 2 (not day 0) make acute renal artery thrombosis highly unlikely.

subject: Immunology
testedConcept: DiGeorge syndrome — 22q11.2 deletion and T cell deficiency from thymic aplasia
physicianTask: Patient Care: Diagnosis
memoryAnchor: DiGeorge: 22q11 deletion (CATCH-22: Cardiac defects, Abnormal facies, Thymic aplasia → T cell deficiency, Cleft palate, Hypocalcemia-22). Absent thymus → no T cells → viral/fungal infections. Also B cell dysfunction (T cell help lost). Absent thymic shadow on CXR.
commonTrap: Students pick XLA (Bruton) because both have recurrent infections — DiGeorge has T cell deficiency (fungal, viral, PCP) due to thymic aplasia. XLA has absent B cells and all Ig classes (encapsulated bacteria only, normal T cells). DiGeorge has abnormal facies and cardiac defects.
stem: A 3-week-old boy has tetany from hypocalcemia. Cardiac workup reveals a conotruncal heart defect (truncus arteriosus). The infant has a fish-shaped mouth and low-set ears. CXR shows absence of the thymic shadow. Flow cytometry shows markedly reduced CD3+ T cells with intact B cell numbers. Which genetic mutation explains this presentation?

options.text: 22q11.2 microdeletion causing failure of third and fourth pharyngeal pouch development
BTK mutation causing failure of B cell maturation beyond the pro-B cell stage
ADA deficiency causing toxic deoxyadenosine accumulation depleting T and B cells
RAG1/RAG2 mutation preventing VDJ recombination in both T and B cells

explanation: 22q11.2 microdeletion (DiGeorge syndrome, velocardiofacial syndrome) impairs development of the third and fourth pharyngeal pouches, leading to thymic aplasia, parathyroid aplasia (hypocalcemia/tetany), and conotruncal cardiac defects. Absent thymus → no T cell maturation → CD3+ lymphopenia with intact B cell numbers. The combination of hypocalcemia at birth, conotruncal cardiac defect, abnormal facies, and absent thymic shadow on CXR is pathognomonic. BTK mutation causes XLA (absent B cells, normal T cells). ADA deficiency causes SCID (both T and B cells absent). RAG mutations cause T-B-SCID.
pearl: DiGeorge (22q11 deletion): CATCH-22. T cell deficiency from thymic aplasia → opportunistic infections (Candida, PCP, viral). Hypocalcemia from absent parathyroids. Conotruncal heart defects. Absent thymic shadow on CXR. Treat with thymic transplant or T cell replete HSCT for severe cases.

optionExplanations.A: The 22q11.2 microdeletion encompasses the TBX1 gene, which is critical for pharyngeal arch and pouch development. The third pharyngeal pouch gives rise to the inferior parathyroid glands and the thymic anlage; the fourth gives rise to the superior parathyroid glands and contributes to great vessel formation. Failure of these structures to migrate properly causes the complete CATCH-22 phenotype. Absent T cells with normal B cell numbers is the key immunologic finding — B cells develop in the bone marrow (independent of thymus), while T cell maturation requires thymic stromal interaction. Patients are susceptible to opportunistic infections from intracellular pathogens.
optionExplanations.B: BTK (Bruton tyrosine kinase) mutation causes X-linked agammaglobulinemia (XLA), characterized by absent B cells and very low immunoglobulins of all classes. T cells are completely normal in XLA. Patients present at approximately 6 months (after maternal antibody wanes) with recurrent infections from encapsulated bacteria — not opportunistic fungal or viral infections. There are no cardiac defects, facial abnormalities, or hypocalcemia. The flow cytometry pattern of absent B cells with normal T cells is the opposite of DiGeorge syndrome.
optionExplanations.C: ADA (adenosine deaminase) deficiency causes severe combined immunodeficiency (SCID) by allowing toxic accumulation of deoxyadenosine, which is particularly damaging to developing lymphocytes. Both T and B cells are depleted in ADA-SCID. Patients typically present in infancy with failure to thrive, recurrent severe infections from all pathogen categories (bacteria, viruses, fungi), and markedly reduced lymphocyte counts. There are no associated cardiac defects or hypocalcemia, and the gene is on chromosome 20q13 — not 22q11.
optionExplanations.D: RAG1/RAG2 mutations prevent V(D)J recombination during lymphocyte development, eliminating both T and B cells (T-B-SCID). Both lymphocyte lineages require functional RAG activity for antigen receptor gene assembly. The immunologic phenotype is complete absence of both T and B cells (with NK cells preserved in some variants). DiGeorge syndrome specifically affects T cells while sparing B cell development — RAG mutation would abolish B cells as well as T cells. RAG-SCID also has no associated cardiac defects or parathyroid abnormalities.

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: Hemophilia A — factor VIII deficiency and PTT correction with mixing study
physicianTask: Patient Care: Laboratory and Diagnostic Studies
memoryAnchor: Hemophilia A: Factor VIII deficiency (X-linked recessive, males). ↑PTT, normal PT, normal bleeding time/platelets. Mixing study corrects PTT (no inhibitor) vs does not correct (inhibitor present). Hemophilia B: Factor IX. Von Willebrand: ↑PTT + ↑bleeding time (affects platelet adhesion AND factor VIII carrier).
commonTrap: Students say the bleeding time is prolonged in hemophilia — bleeding time reflects platelet plug formation (primary hemostasis), which requires von Willebrand factor and platelet function. Hemophilia A/B are secondary hemostasis defects (coagulation cascade); bleeding time is normal. PTT is prolonged because factors VIII/IX are intrinsic pathway components.
stem: A 4-year-old boy has repeated episodes of joint bleeding and prolonged bleeding after dental extraction. His mother says her brother had similar problems. Labs: PT 12s (normal), PTT 68s (elevated), bleeding time 4 min (normal), platelets 220,000/µL. A mixing study (patient plasma 1:1 with normal plasma) corrects the PTT to 13s. Which diagnosis is confirmed by these findings?

options.text: Hemophilia A — factor VIII deficiency; mixing study correction indicates factor deficiency, not inhibitor
Von Willebrand disease — elevated PTT and bleeding time confirm both platelet and coagulation defects
Lupus anticoagulant — antiphospholipid antibody that prolongs PTT but does not correct on mixing
Vitamin K deficiency — both PT and PTT are prolonged in vitamin K deficiency

explanation: Hemophilia A (factor VIII deficiency) is X-linked recessive, explaining why males are affected and the maternal uncle is the likely affected relative. The laboratory profile — isolated prolonged PTT with normal PT, normal bleeding time, and normal platelet count — is characteristic of an intrinsic pathway factor deficiency. The mixing study corrects the PTT to normal: adding normal plasma (which contains all clotting factors) restores factor VIII, confirming the deficiency rather than an inhibitor. An inhibitor (anti-factor VIII antibody) would NOT correct on mixing. Factor assay would confirm factor VIII deficiency. Treatment: recombinant factor VIII concentrate for acute bleeds; prophylactic factor VIII for severe disease.
pearl: Hemophilia A: ↑PTT, normal PT, normal bleeding time. Mixing corrects = factor deficiency. Mixing does NOT correct = inhibitor present. Hemophilia A: factor VIII (X-linked). Hemophilia B: factor IX (Christmas disease). VWD: ↑PTT + ↑bleeding time (affects platelet adhesion + factor VIII stabilization).

optionExplanations.A: Factor VIII is an essential cofactor in the intrinsic coagulation pathway — it is part of the tenase complex (factor VIIIa + IXa) that activates factor X. Without factor VIII, the intrinsic pathway is blocked and PTT is prolonged. Because PT tests the extrinsic pathway (tissue factor → VII → X → thrombin) which does not include factors VIII or IX, PT is unaffected. The mixing study principle: if the prolonged PTT corrects to normal when mixed 1:1 with normal plasma, the patient's plasma lacked a factor that normal plasma provides (factor deficiency). If PTT remains prolonged, the patient's plasma contains an inhibitory antibody that also inactivates normal plasma factors.
optionExplanations.B: Von Willebrand disease (VWD) causes both an elevated bleeding time (VWF is required for platelet adhesion to subendothelial collagen) and an elevated PTT (VWF is the carrier protein that stabilizes factor VIII from proteolysis — VWF deficiency leads to factor VIII depletion). The NORMAL bleeding time in this patient excludes VWD as the primary diagnosis. The question specifically states bleeding time is 4 minutes (normal, typically <7–10 min), which is inconsistent with VWD.
optionExplanations.C: Lupus anticoagulant (antiphospholipid antibody) causes a prolonged PTT in vitro by binding phospholipid in the reagent, but paradoxically causes THROMBOSIS in vivo. The defining characteristic of lupus anticoagulant is that the PTT does NOT correct on mixing with normal plasma — the inhibitory antibody from the patient's plasma also inactivates the phospholipid reagent in the normal plasma. The CORRECTION of the PTT on mixing study in this patient definitively excludes lupus anticoagulant and confirms factor deficiency.
optionExplanations.D: Vitamin K is required for gamma-carboxylation of factors II, VII, IX, X, and proteins C and S. Deficiency of all these factors causes prolongation of BOTH PT and PTT — not isolated PTT prolongation as seen here. In this patient, the PT is normal (12 seconds), which requires intact factor VII (vitamin K-dependent). A normal PT conclusively rules out vitamin K deficiency as the cause of the isolated PTT elevation.

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia - PF4 antibodies causing platelet activation and thrombosis
physicianTask: Patient Care: Clinical Interventions
memoryAnchor: HIT is thrombosis despite thrombocytopenia. Stop heparin and start a non-heparin anticoagulant.
commonTrap: Students see platelets below 100,000 and stop anticoagulation entirely. In HIT, the patient is hypercoagulable, so anticoagulation must continue with a non-heparin agent.
stem: A 59-year-old man is hospitalized for an anterior STEMI and receives unfractionated heparin. On hospital day 6, his platelet count falls from 242,000/uL to 84,000/uL and Doppler ultrasound shows a new femoral vein thrombosis. Creatinine is 4.2 mg/dL after contrast-associated acute kidney injury; AST and ALT are normal. PT and PTT were normal before admission, and the smear shows no schistocytes. Which immediate management is most appropriate?

options.text: Stop heparin and begin argatroban because platelet-activating PF4 antibodies create a hypercoagulable state
Stop heparin and begin fondaparinux despite the severe acute kidney injury
Stop heparin and transfuse platelets without starting another anticoagulant
Start warfarin immediately because vitamin K antagonism is safest during acute thrombocytopenia

explanation: This patient has heparin-induced thrombocytopenia: a platelet fall greater than 50% beginning 5–10 days after heparin exposure plus new thrombosis. All heparin must be stopped and a non-heparin anticoagulant started immediately because platelet-activating PF4 antibodies generate intense thrombin activity. Argatroban is hepatically cleared and is therefore appropriate with severe acute kidney injury and normal hepatic function. Fondaparinux is substantially renally cleared and is inappropriate in severe renal impairment. Platelet transfusion is avoided unless bleeding is life-threatening, and warfarin is delayed until platelet recovery because early protein C depletion can worsen thrombosis.

optionExplanations.A: Argatroban is a direct thrombin inhibitor cleared by the liver. It provides immediate non-heparin anticoagulation for acute HIT and is particularly useful here because severe kidney injury makes renally cleared alternatives less attractive.
optionExplanations.B: Fondaparinux can be used for HIT in selected patients, but it is renally eliminated and should be avoided in severe renal impairment. The creatinine elevation is included to distinguish it from hepatically cleared argatroban.
optionExplanations.C: Stopping heparin alone is insufficient because HIT is intensely prothrombotic. Platelet transfusion can provide additional substrate for thrombosis and is reserved for life-threatening bleeding or selected urgent procedures.
optionExplanations.D: Wrong. Warfarin is dangerous during acute HIT because rapid protein C depletion can worsen hypercoagulability and cause skin necrosis.

pearl: HIT timing: 5-10 days after heparin, platelet drop over 50%, thrombosis. Treat by stopping heparin and starting argatroban, bivalirudin, or fondaparinux. Avoid warfarin until platelets recover.

subject: Physiology
testedConcept: ARDS ventilator strategy - low tidal volume reduces ventilator-induced lung injury
physicianTask: Patient Care: Clinical Interventions
memoryAnchor: ARDS lungs are small baby lungs. Use low tidal volume to avoid overdistending the remaining aerated alveoli.
commonTrap: Students try to normalize PaCO2 with large tidal volumes. In ARDS, permissive hypercapnia is accepted to prevent volutrauma and barotrauma.
stem: A 46-year-old woman is intubated for severe pancreatitis complicated by acute respiratory distress syndrome. Temperature is 38.6 C, BP 104/62 mmHg, HR 118/min. Chest x-ray shows bilateral diffuse alveolar opacities. PaO2/FIO2 ratio is 92. Plateau pressure is 34 cm H2O while receiving volume-control ventilation with tidal volume 10 mL/kg predicted body weight. Which ventilator change most directly improves survival by targeting the underlying mechanical problem?

options.text: Reduce tidal volume to 6 mL/kg predicted body weight to limit alveolar overdistension and volutrauma
Increase tidal volume to 12 mL/kg predicted body weight to normalize PaCO2 and prevent respiratory acidosis
Lower positive end-expiratory pressure to zero to reduce intrathoracic pressure and improve venous return
Switch to pressure support ventilation to allow spontaneous large tidal volumes and reduce sedation needs

explanation: ARDS produces heterogeneous lung injury with many flooded or collapsed alveoli and a smaller remaining aerated lung volume. Delivering normal or high tidal volumes overdistends the remaining open alveoli, causing ventilator-induced lung injury through volutrauma, barotrauma, and inflammatory biotrauma. Low tidal volume ventilation at about 6 mL/kg predicted body weight improves survival by limiting plateau pressure and alveolar stretch; permissive hypercapnia is accepted if pH remains tolerable. Increasing tidal volume to normalize CO2 worsens mortality. PEEP is usually required to prevent end-expiratory alveolar collapse and improve oxygenation, although excessive PEEP can impair venous return. Pressure support is not appropriate in severe early ARDS because uncontrolled spontaneous effort can generate injurious tidal volumes and transpulmonary pressures.

optionExplanations.A: Correct. Low tidal volume targets the small functional lung volume in ARDS and reduces volutrauma and mortality.
optionExplanations.B: Wrong. Normalizing PaCO2 with high tidal volumes increases alveolar stretch and ventilator-induced lung injury.
optionExplanations.C: Wrong. Removing PEEP promotes derecruitment and worsens shunt physiology in ARDS.
optionExplanations.D: Wrong. Pressure support can permit large spontaneous tidal volumes and patient self-inflicted lung injury in severe early ARDS.

pearl: ARDS ventilation: low tidal volume 6 mL/kg predicted body weight, plateau pressure goal no more than 30 cm H2O, adequate PEEP, prone positioning for severe ARDS, and permissive hypercapnia if pH is acceptable.

subject: Pharmacology
testedConcept: Nitrate toxicity in right ventricular infarction - preload dependence and hypotension
physicianTask: Patient Care: Pharmacotherapy
memoryAnchor: RV infarct needs preload. Nitrates steal preload.
commonTrap: Students reflexively give nitroglycerin for all ischemic chest pain. Inferior STEMI with RV involvement is preload dependent, so nitrates can cause shock.
stem: A 64-year-old man has crushing chest pain and diaphoresis. ECG shows ST elevations in II, III, and aVF with ST elevation in right-sided lead V4R. BP is 86/54 mmHg, HR is 58/min, lungs are clear, and jugular venous pressure is elevated. After receiving sublingual nitroglycerin, he becomes more hypotensive. Which mechanism best explains this deterioration?

options.text: Venodilation reduces right ventricular preload and worsens left ventricular filling in a preload-dependent infarct
Coronary steal redirects blood away from the infarct-related artery through excessive arterial vasodilation
Increased pulmonary capillary wedge pressure causes acute alveolar edema and hypoxemic respiratory failure
Reflex sympathetic activation increases afterload and acutely worsens left ventricular systolic function

explanation: Inferior STEMI with ST elevation in right-sided lead V4R, hypotension, elevated JVP, and clear lungs indicates right ventricular infarction. The ischemic right ventricle cannot generate normal forward flow and becomes highly preload dependent. Nitrates produce venodilation, reducing venous return to the right heart. This lowers RV filling, reduces pulmonary blood flow, decreases LV preload, and worsens systemic hypotension. Treatment emphasizes cautious IV fluids and urgent reperfusion. Coronary steal is not the key nitrate toxicity here. Pulmonary edema is less likely because the lungs are clear and the problem is RV forward failure. Reflex sympathetic activation would tend to support blood pressure rather than explain the immediate collapse after venodilation.

optionExplanations.A: Correct. RV infarction is preload dependent; nitrate venodilation decreases venous return and collapses cardiac output.
optionExplanations.B: Wrong. Coronary steal is not the primary mechanism of nitrate-induced shock in RV infarction.
optionExplanations.C: Wrong. Clear lungs and elevated JVP point to RV infarction, not LV failure with pulmonary edema.
optionExplanations.D: Wrong. Reflex sympathetic activation is compensatory; the direct harmful effect is reduced preload.

pearl: RV infarct triad: hypotension, clear lungs, elevated JVP. Confirm with right-sided ECG lead V4R. Avoid nitrates and diuretics. Give IV fluids carefully and perform urgent reperfusion.

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: Hepatorenal syndrome - renal vasoconstriction from advanced cirrhosis without structural kidney injury
physicianTask: Patient Care: Diagnosis
memoryAnchor: HRS kidney looks normal but clamps down. Cirrhosis causes splanchnic dilation, low effective volume, and renal vasoconstriction.
commonTrap: Students call every creatinine rise in cirrhosis acute tubular necrosis. HRS has bland urine, low sodium, and no structural renal injury.
stem: A 56-year-old man with alcohol-related cirrhosis and tense ascites is admitted for worsening fatigue. BP is 92/58 mmHg. Labs show creatinine rising from 0.9 to 2.4 mg/dL over 10 days, BUN 46 mg/dL, Na+ 128 mEq/L, and total bilirubin 5.8 mg/dL. Urinalysis is bland with no casts or proteinuria. Urine sodium is 6 mEq/L. Renal ultrasound is normal. Which mechanism best explains his kidney injury?

options.text: Severe renal vasoconstriction triggered by splanchnic arterial dilation and reduced effective circulating volume
Immune complex deposition in glomeruli causing nephritic urine sediment and complement consumption
Direct tubular epithelial necrosis from prolonged ischemia producing muddy brown granular casts
Postrenal obstruction from ascites compressing both ureters and increasing tubular hydrostatic pressure

explanation: This patient has hepatorenal syndrome, a functional renal failure in advanced cirrhosis. Portal hypertension and cirrhosis cause marked splanchnic arterial vasodilation, reducing effective arterial blood volume despite total body fluid overload. Neurohormonal activation then causes intense renal vasoconstriction, lowering GFR without intrinsic structural kidney damage. The bland urinalysis, very low urine sodium, normal renal ultrasound, ascites, hypotension, and advanced liver disease support hepatorenal syndrome. Immune complex glomerulonephritis would cause hematuria, proteinuria, casts, and complement abnormalities. Acute tubular necrosis would produce muddy brown casts and higher urine sodium. Postrenal obstruction would show hydronephrosis on ultrasound.

optionExplanations.A: Correct. HRS is functional renal vasoconstriction caused by cirrhosis-driven splanchnic vasodilation and low effective arterial volume.
optionExplanations.B: Wrong. Immune complex nephritis causes active urine sediment, not bland urine with very low sodium.
optionExplanations.C: Wrong. ATN causes tubular injury with muddy brown casts and impaired sodium reabsorption.
optionExplanations.D: Wrong. Bilateral obstruction would cause hydronephrosis, which is absent on ultrasound.

pearl: Hepatorenal syndrome: advanced cirrhosis, ascites, rising creatinine, bland urine, urine Na usually very low, normal renal ultrasound. Treat with albumin plus vasoconstrictor therapy and liver transplant evaluation.

subject: Biochemistry
testedConcept: MELAS - mitochondrial tRNA mutation causing maternal inheritance, lactic acidosis, and stroke-like episodes
physicianTask: Medical Knowledge: Applying Foundational Science Concepts
memoryAnchor: Mitochondria come from mom. MELAS has mitochondrial tRNA mutation, lactic acidosis, and stroke-like episodes.
commonTrap: Students choose autosomal dominant inheritance because several generations are affected. Mitochondrial disease affects both sexes but is transmitted only by mothers.
stem: A 17-year-old girl has recurrent migraine-like headaches, vomiting, short stature, and sensorineural hearing loss. She is brought to the ED after a focal seizure and transient right arm weakness. MRI shows cortical signal abnormalities that do not respect a single vascular territory. Serum lactate is 5.6 mmol/L. Her mother and maternal aunt have hearing loss and diabetes; her father is healthy. Which genetic mechanism is most likely?

options.text: Point mutation in mitochondrial tRNA causing impaired oxidative phosphorylation with maternal inheritance
Autosomal dominant trinucleotide repeat expansion causing anticipation through paternal transmission
Defective lysosomal enzyme trafficking due to absent mannose-6-phosphate tagging in the Golgi apparatus
X-linked mutation causing impaired dystrophin production in skeletal and cardiac muscle membranes

explanation: This presentation is consistent with MELAS: mitochondrial encephalomyopathy, lactic acidosis, and stroke-like episodes. Mitochondrial tRNA mutations impair oxidative phosphorylation, forcing increased anaerobic metabolism and lactic acid production. Stroke-like neurologic deficits in MELAS do not respect a vascular territory because they reflect mitochondrial energy failure rather than thromboembolism. Maternal inheritance is key: affected mothers can transmit mitochondrial DNA mutations to all children, while affected fathers transmit them to none. Trinucleotide repeat expansion with anticipation suggests disorders such as Huntington disease or myotonic dystrophy, not lactic acidosis with maternal inheritance. I-cell disease involves lysosomal enzyme trafficking. Dystrophin mutations cause Duchenne or Becker muscular dystrophy.

optionExplanations.A: Correct. MELAS is a mitochondrial DNA disorder, often involving tRNA mutations, with maternal inheritance and lactic acidosis.
optionExplanations.B: Wrong. Trinucleotide repeat disorders do not explain maternal-only transmission or elevated lactate from oxidative phosphorylation failure.
optionExplanations.C: Wrong. Mannose-6-phosphate tagging failure causes I-cell disease with coarse facies and lysosomal storage findings.
optionExplanations.D: Wrong. Dystrophin disease causes progressive proximal muscle weakness, not stroke-like episodes with lactic acidosis.

pearl: MELAS: mitochondrial inheritance, encephalomyopathy, lactic acidosis, stroke-like episodes, hearing loss, diabetes. MRI lesions do not follow vascular territories.

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: MEN 2A - RET mutation with medullary thyroid carcinoma, pheochromocytoma, and hyperparathyroidism
physicianTask: Patient Care: Diagnosis
memoryAnchor: MEN 2A: Medullary thyroid, pheochromocytoma, parathyroid. RET proto-oncogene.
commonTrap: Students biopsy a thyroid nodule before recognizing pheochromocytoma. Catecholamine excess must be treated first to prevent hypertensive crisis.
stem: A 32-year-old woman has episodic headaches, palpitations, and sweating. BP is 184/106 mmHg during an episode. Labs show elevated plasma free metanephrines and calcium 11.4 mg/dL. Neck ultrasound shows a 1.8-cm thyroid nodule. Fine-needle aspiration reveals nests of polygonal cells with amyloid stroma; serum calcitonin is elevated. Her father had thyroid cancer in his 30s. Which inherited mutation best explains this syndrome?

options.text: Activating RET proto-oncogene mutation causing MEN 2A with medullary thyroid carcinoma
Inactivating MEN1 tumor suppressor mutation causing pituitary adenoma and pancreatic endocrine tumors
Germline VHL mutation causing hemangioblastomas, clear cell renal carcinoma, and pheochromocytoma
APC tumor suppressor mutation causing familial adenomatous polyposis with colorectal adenocarcinoma

explanation: This patient has MEN 2A: medullary thyroid carcinoma, pheochromocytoma, and primary hyperparathyroidism. Medullary thyroid carcinoma arises from parafollicular C cells, secretes calcitonin, and can show amyloid stroma derived from calcitonin. MEN 2A is caused by activating germline RET proto-oncogene mutations and is inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern. Pheochromocytoma must be recognized and treated before thyroid surgery because anesthesia or manipulation can precipitate hypertensive crisis. MEN1 causes parathyroid, pituitary, and pancreatic endocrine tumors, not medullary thyroid carcinoma. VHL can include pheochromocytoma but is classically associated with hemangioblastomas and clear cell renal carcinoma. APC causes familial adenomatous polyposis.

optionExplanations.A: Correct. MEN 2A is caused by activating RET mutations and includes medullary thyroid carcinoma, pheochromocytoma, and hyperparathyroidism.
optionExplanations.B: Wrong. MEN1 explains parathyroid, pituitary, and pancreatic tumors but not calcitonin-producing medullary thyroid carcinoma.
optionExplanations.C: Wrong. VHL can cause pheochromocytoma, but the medullary thyroid carcinoma plus hyperparathyroidism pattern points to MEN 2A.
optionExplanations.D: Wrong. APC mutations cause familial adenomatous polyposis and colorectal cancer risk, not this endocrine tumor triad.

pearl: MEN 2A: RET mutation, medullary thyroid carcinoma, pheochromocytoma, hyperparathyroidism. Treat pheochromocytoma before thyroidectomy. Medullary carcinoma has calcitonin and amyloid stroma.

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: Anti-GBM disease - linear IgG deposition causing pulmonary hemorrhage and rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis
physicianTask: Patient Care: Diagnosis
memoryAnchor: Goodpasture = good linear lines on immunofluorescence plus lungs and kidneys.
commonTrap: Students choose granulomatosis with polyangiitis when hemoptysis and nephritis occur together. Anti-GBM disease has linear IgG staining, while GPA is pauci-immune and often c-ANCA positive.
stem: A 23-year-old man presents with hemoptysis, fatigue, and dark urine. BP is 156/94 mmHg. Urinalysis shows dysmorphic RBCs and RBC casts. Creatinine is 2.9 mg/dL and BUN is 42 mg/dL. Chest x-ray shows bilateral patchy alveolar opacities. Kidney biopsy shows crescentic glomerulonephritis with smooth linear IgG staining along the glomerular basement membrane. Which diagnosis is most likely?

options.text: Anti-glomerular basement membrane disease caused by antibodies against type IV collagen
Granulomatosis with polyangiitis causing pauci-immune necrotizing granulomatous vasculitis
