Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis causing subepithelial immune complex humps after pharyngitis
IgA nephropathy causing mesangial immune complex deposition after mucosal infection

explanation: Anti-GBM disease causes rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis with pulmonary hemorrhage because antibodies target the alpha-3 chain of type IV collagen in glomerular and alveolar basement membranes. The key biopsy clue is smooth linear IgG deposition along the glomerular basement membrane. Hematuria with RBC casts indicates nephritic glomerular inflammation, while hemoptysis and alveolar opacities indicate pulmonary hemorrhage. Granulomatosis with polyangiitis can cause pulmonary-renal syndrome, but renal biopsy is typically pauci-immune rather than linear IgG. Post-streptococcal GN shows granular immune complex deposition and subepithelial humps. IgA nephropathy causes mesangial IgA deposition and usually follows mucosal infection closely.

optionExplanations.A: Correct. Anti-GBM disease targets type IV collagen and produces pulmonary hemorrhage plus crescentic GN with linear IgG staining.
optionExplanations.B: Wrong. GPA can cause lung and kidney disease, but the kidney biopsy is usually pauci-immune, not linear IgG positive.
optionExplanations.C: Wrong. PSGN produces granular immune complex staining and subepithelial humps rather than pulmonary hemorrhage with linear IgG.
optionExplanations.D: Wrong. IgA nephropathy causes mesangial IgA deposition and recurrent hematuria after mucosal infections.

pearl: Pulmonary-renal syndrome differential: anti-GBM has linear IgG; GPA and microscopic polyangiitis are pauci-immune ANCA vasculitides; lupus has full-house immune complex staining.

subject: Pharmacology
testedConcept: Vancomycin mechanism - D-Ala-D-Ala binding inhibiting peptidoglycan cross-linking
physicianTask: Patient Care: Pharmacotherapy
memoryAnchor: Vancomycin grabs D-Ala-D-Ala; beta-lactams bind PBPs. VRE changes D-Ala-D-Ala to D-Ala-D-Lac.
commonTrap: Students confuse vancomycin with beta-lactams because both affect the cell wall. Vancomycin binds the substrate; beta-lactams bind transpeptidase enzymes.
stem: A 67-year-old man in the ICU develops fever and hypotension after central venous catheter placement. Temperature is 39.2 C, BP is 84/52 mmHg, HR is 122/min, WBC count is 18,400/uL, and creatinine is 1.1 mg/dL. Blood cultures grow methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus. He is started on an antibiotic that must be infused slowly to reduce flushing and hypotension. Which molecular action best describes this drug?

options.text: Binding D-Ala-D-Ala termini to block peptidoglycan polymerization and cross-linking
Binding penicillin-binding proteins to irreversibly inhibit bacterial transpeptidase enzymes
Inhibiting DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV to prevent bacterial DNA supercoiling
Binding the 50S ribosomal subunit to block translocation during protein elongation

explanation: Vancomycin is used for serious MRSA infections and can cause infusion-related flushing and hypotension, historically called red man syndrome, if infused too rapidly. Its mechanism is binding D-Ala-D-Ala termini on peptidoglycan precursors, preventing cell wall polymerization and cross-linking. This is distinct from beta-lactams, which bind penicillin-binding proteins and inhibit transpeptidase enzymes. Vancomycin resistance in enterococci often changes the target from D-Ala-D-Ala to D-Ala-D-Lac, decreasing drug binding. Fluoroquinolones inhibit DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV. Macrolides bind the 50S ribosomal subunit and inhibit translocation.

optionExplanations.A: Correct. Vancomycin binds D-Ala-D-Ala and prevents peptidoglycan assembly and cross-linking.
optionExplanations.B: Wrong. Beta-lactams bind PBPs/transpeptidases; vancomycin binds the peptidoglycan substrate instead.
optionExplanations.C: Wrong. DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV inhibition describes fluoroquinolones.
optionExplanations.D: Wrong. Blocking 50S translocation describes macrolides such as azithromycin or erythromycin.

pearl: Vancomycin: MRSA coverage, D-Ala-D-Ala binding, nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity, infusion reaction. VRE changes D-Ala-D-Ala to D-Ala-D-Lac.

subject: Physiology
testedConcept: Cardiac tamponade - pulsus paradoxus from ventricular interdependence and fixed pericardial volume
physicianTask: Medical Knowledge: Applying Foundational Science Concepts
memoryAnchor: Tamponade traps the heart in a fixed box. Inspiration expands RV, septum shifts left, LV filling falls.
commonTrap: Students think pulsus paradoxus is caused by lung compression of the aorta. It is caused by exaggerated ventricular interdependence within a fixed pericardial space.
stem: A 48-year-old woman with metastatic breast cancer develops acute dyspnea and lightheadedness. BP is 82/54 mmHg, HR is 124/min, and jugular venous pressure is elevated. Heart sounds are muffled. Systolic blood pressure falls by 18 mmHg during inspiration. Echocardiography shows a large pericardial effusion with right atrial collapse. Which mechanism explains the inspiratory blood pressure drop?

options.text: Increased right ventricular filling shifts the interventricular septum leftward and reduces left ventricular preload
Inspiratory compression of the thoracic aorta directly decreases systemic arterial outflow
Decreased pulmonary vascular resistance causes excessive left ventricular filling and reflex hypotension
Acute mitral regurgitation causes increased left atrial pressure and pulmonary venous congestion

explanation: Cardiac tamponade creates a fixed pericardial volume that limits total cardiac chamber expansion. During inspiration, venous return to the right ventricle normally increases. In tamponade, the right ventricle cannot expand outward, so it bows the interventricular septum into the left ventricle. This reduces LV filling, lowers stroke volume, and causes an exaggerated inspiratory fall in systolic blood pressure called pulsus paradoxus. The mechanism is ventricular interdependence, not direct aortic compression. Pulmonary vascular resistance changes do not explain the fixed pericardial constraint. Acute mitral regurgitation causes pulmonary edema and a systolic murmur, not Beck triad with right atrial collapse.

optionExplanations.A: Correct. Fixed pericardial volume makes inspiratory RV expansion reduce LV filling through septal shift.
optionExplanations.B: Wrong. The aorta is not compressed by inspiration; pulsus paradoxus is an intracardiac filling problem.
optionExplanations.C: Wrong. The problem is decreased LV preload, not excessive LV filling.
optionExplanations.D: Wrong. Acute MR causes pulmonary congestion, not elevated JVP with muffled heart sounds and right atrial collapse.

pearl: Tamponade: hypotension, elevated JVP, muffled heart sounds, pulsus paradoxus, electrical alternans. Treat unstable tamponade with urgent pericardiocentesis.

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome - antibodies against presynaptic P/Q-type calcium channels
physicianTask: Patient Care: Diagnosis
memoryAnchor: LEMS gets stronger with use. Presynaptic calcium channels fail; repeated activity builds calcium.
commonTrap: Students choose myasthenia gravis for any fatigable weakness. LEMS has autonomic symptoms, proximal weakness, malignancy association, and strength improves with repeated use.
stem: A 62-year-old man with a 45-pack-year smoking history has progressive proximal leg weakness and dry mouth. He has difficulty rising from a chair, but strength improves after repeated testing. Deep tendon reflexes are decreased. Chest CT shows a hilar lung mass. Repetitive nerve stimulation shows an incremental increase in compound muscle action potential amplitude at high frequency. Which antibody is most likely present?

options.text: Antibodies against presynaptic P/Q-type voltage-gated calcium channels at the neuromuscular junction
Antibodies against postsynaptic acetylcholine receptors on the skeletal muscle motor end plate
Antibodies against acetylcholinesterase causing excessive acetylcholine accumulation in the synaptic cleft
Antibodies against peripheral nerve myelin causing ascending demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy

explanation: Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome is caused by antibodies against presynaptic P/Q-type voltage-gated calcium channels, impairing calcium influx and reducing acetylcholine release. Repeated activity allows calcium to accumulate in the presynaptic terminal, improving acetylcholine release and temporarily increasing strength, which explains facilitation and incremental response on repetitive nerve stimulation. It is strongly associated with small cell lung carcinoma, especially in older smokers, and often has autonomic symptoms such as dry mouth. Myasthenia gravis targets postsynaptic acetylcholine receptors and worsens with use. Acetylcholinesterase antibodies are not the mechanism. Guillain-Barre syndrome is an acute demyelinating neuropathy with ascending weakness.

optionExplanations.A: Correct. LEMS is a presynaptic calcium-channel antibody disorder with improved strength after repeated use.
optionExplanations.B: Wrong. Postsynaptic acetylcholine receptor antibodies cause myasthenia gravis, which worsens with use.
optionExplanations.C: Wrong. Acetylcholinesterase inhibition would increase acetylcholine signaling rather than reduce release.
optionExplanations.D: Wrong. Peripheral nerve myelin antibodies fit demyelinating neuropathy, not neuromuscular junction facilitation.

pearl: LEMS: proximal weakness, autonomic symptoms, decreased reflexes, improves with use, incremental response on stimulation, associated with small cell lung carcinoma.

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: Primary sclerosing cholangitis - inflammatory bile duct fibrosis associated with ulcerative colitis
physicianTask: Patient Care: Diagnosis
memoryAnchor: PSC = pipe scarring in UC. Beads on cholangiography, p-ANCA, cholangiocarcinoma risk.
commonTrap: Students choose primary biliary cholangitis for any cholestatic labs. PBC is autoimmune destruction of small intrahepatic ducts in middle-aged women and has anti-mitochondrial antibodies.
stem: A 36-year-old man with ulcerative colitis has progressive fatigue and pruritus. Labs show alkaline phosphatase 610 U/L, total bilirubin 3.1 mg/dL, AST 86 U/L, ALT 94 U/L, and positive p-ANCA. MRCP shows multifocal strictures and dilation of intrahepatic and extrahepatic bile ducts, creating a beaded appearance. Which complication is he at increased risk for?

options.text: Cholangiocarcinoma arising from chronic inflammatory fibrosis of large bile ducts
Hepatocellular adenoma caused by anabolic steroid exposure and beta-catenin activation
Pancreatic pseudocyst caused by enzyme-rich fluid collection after acute pancreatitis
Cavernous hemangioma caused by benign proliferation of hepatic vascular channels

explanation: Primary sclerosing cholangitis causes chronic inflammation and fibrosis of intrahepatic and extrahepatic bile ducts, producing alternating strictures and dilation with a beaded cholangiographic appearance. It is strongly associated with ulcerative colitis and p-ANCA positivity. Chronic biliary inflammation increases risk for cholangiocarcinoma, and patients can also progress to secondary biliary cirrhosis. Primary biliary cholangitis differs by affecting small intrahepatic ducts, occurring more often in middle-aged women, and having anti-mitochondrial antibodies. Hepatocellular adenoma is associated with oral contraceptives or anabolic steroids. Pancreatic pseudocysts follow pancreatitis. Hemangiomas are benign vascular lesions.

optionExplanations.A: Correct. PSC causes chronic large-duct inflammation and fibrosis, increasing cholangiocarcinoma risk.
optionExplanations.B: Wrong. Hepatocellular adenoma is linked to steroid or estrogen exposure, not beaded bile ducts with UC.
optionExplanations.C: Wrong. Pseudocysts are pancreatitis complications, not cholestatic liver disease complications.
optionExplanations.D: Wrong. Hemangioma is a benign vascular lesion unrelated to PSC or ulcerative colitis.

pearl: PSC: men, ulcerative colitis, p-ANCA, beaded bile ducts, cholangiocarcinoma risk. PBC: women, anti-mitochondrial antibodies, small intrahepatic ducts.

subject: Physiology
testedConcept: Syndrome of inappropriate ADH - euvolemic hyponatremia with concentrated urine
physicianTask: Patient Care: Laboratory and Diagnostic Studies
memoryAnchor: SIADH: serum dilute, urine concentrated, body looks euvolemic.
commonTrap: Students see hyponatremia and assume volume depletion. SIADH has inappropriately concentrated urine and urine sodium is not low because effective circulating volume is not depleted.
stem: A 70-year-old man with newly diagnosed small cell lung carcinoma becomes confused after chemotherapy. He is euvolemic on exam with no edema, orthostasis, vomiting, or diarrhea. Labs show Na+ 118 mEq/L, serum osmolality 252 mOsm/kg, urine osmolality 540 mOsm/kg, urine sodium 64 mEq/L, creatinine 0.8 mg/dL, glucose 96 mg/dL, and TSH normal. Which mechanism best explains the hyponatremia?

options.text: Ectopic ADH increases collecting duct water reabsorption through V2 receptor aquaporin insertion
Aldosterone deficiency causes renal sodium wasting with hyperkalemic metabolic acidosis
Primary polydipsia suppresses ADH and produces maximally dilute urine with low osmolality
Decreased effective arterial volume activates RAAS and causes avid renal sodium retention

explanation: This patient has SIADH from small cell lung carcinoma. The pattern is hypotonic hyponatremia with inappropriately concentrated urine and elevated urine sodium in a clinically euvolemic patient. Ectopic ADH activates V2 receptors in the collecting duct, increasing aquaporin-2 insertion and free water reabsorption. The added water lowers serum sodium and serum osmolality, while mild volume expansion promotes natriuresis, explaining high urine sodium. Aldosterone deficiency would cause hyperkalemia and metabolic acidosis. Primary polydipsia causes very dilute urine because ADH is suppressed. Low effective arterial volume causes edema or hypovolemia with avid sodium retention and low urine sodium.

optionExplanations.A: Correct. SIADH causes V2-mediated free water retention with concentrated urine despite low serum osmolality.
optionExplanations.B: Wrong. Aldosterone deficiency causes hyperkalemia and non-anion gap metabolic acidosis, not isolated euvolemic hyponatremia.
optionExplanations.C: Wrong. Primary polydipsia suppresses ADH, so urine osmolality should be very low.
optionExplanations.D: Wrong. Low effective arterial volume produces low urine sodium due to RAAS activation.

pearl: SIADH labs: low serum osmolality, high urine osmolality, urine sodium often above 40, euvolemic exam. Causes include small cell lung carcinoma, CNS disease, pulmonary disease, SSRIs, carbamazepine, and cyclophosphamide.

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: TTP - ADAMTS13 autoantibody causing platelet microthrombi
physicianTask: Patient Care: Diagnosis
memoryAnchor: TTP: thrombocytopenia + MAHA + neurologic symptoms + renal injury + fever. ADAMTS13 deficiency leaves large vWF multimers, causing platelet-rich microthrombi. PT/PTT normal.
commonTrap: Students choose DIC because both have schistocytes and thrombocytopenia, but DIC consumes clotting factors, so PT and PTT are prolonged. TTP has normal PT/PTT because the coagulation cascade is not the main problem.
stem: A 31-year-old woman has confusion, fever, petechiae, and dark urine. Platelets are 18,000/uL, hemoglobin is 7.6 g/dL, creatinine is 2.1 mg/dL, PT and PTT are normal, and peripheral smear shows schistocytes. LDH is elevated and haptoglobin is low. Which mechanism best explains this syndrome?

options.text: Autoantibody-mediated ADAMTS13 deficiency causing large von Willebrand factor multimers
Widespread tissue factor activation causing consumption of clotting factors and platelets
IgA immune complex deposition in mesangium after mucosal infection
Complement-mediated red cell lysis from loss of GPI-anchored surface proteins

explanation: This is thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura. ADAMTS13 normally cleaves ultra-large von Willebrand factor multimers into smaller less adhesive forms. Acquired autoantibody-mediated ADAMTS13 deficiency leaves large vWF multimers on endothelial surfaces, causing platelet adhesion, platelet-rich microthrombi, thrombocytopenia, and mechanical red cell fragmentation with schistocytes. Neurologic symptoms and renal dysfunction occur from microvascular ischemia. PT and PTT remain normal because clotting factors are not consumed, distinguishing TTP from DIC. Treatment is urgent plasma exchange plus immunosuppression.
pearl: TTP = ADAMTS13 deficiency. Normal PT/PTT separates it from DIC. Treat urgently with plasma exchange; untreated mortality is high.

optionExplanations.A: ADAMTS13 deficiency is the key mechanism. Without this metalloprotease, ultra-large vWF multimers persist and bind platelets excessively, forming platelet-rich microthrombi. This explains thrombocytopenia, schistocytes, neurologic dysfunction, renal injury, fever, high LDH, and normal PT/PTT.
optionExplanations.B: Widespread tissue factor activation describes DIC, not TTP. DIC consumes platelets and clotting factors, so PT and PTT are usually prolonged. This patient has normal PT/PTT, making coagulation factor consumption less likely than platelet microthrombi from ADAMTS13 deficiency.
optionExplanations.C: IgA immune complex deposition causes IgA nephropathy, which presents with hematuria after mucosal infection. It does not cause severe thrombocytopenia, schistocytes, neurologic symptoms, or normal-coagulation microangiopathic hemolysis.
optionExplanations.D: Loss of GPI-anchored proteins causes paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria through complement-mediated intravascular hemolysis. PNH can cause thrombosis, but it does not explain severe thrombocytopenia with platelet-rich microthrombi and the classic TTP pentad.

subject: Biochemistry
testedConcept: Acute intermittent porphyria - porphobilinogen deaminase deficiency
physicianTask: Patient Care: Diagnosis
memoryAnchor: AIP: abdominal pain + psychiatric symptoms + neuropathy + hyponatremia; no photosensitivity. Defect is porphobilinogen deaminase. Triggers: drugs, fasting, alcohol.
commonTrap: Photosensitivity points away from AIP and toward cutaneous porphyrias. AIP is neurovisceral because ALA and PBG accumulate before porphyrin ring formation.
stem: A 24-year-old woman develops severe abdominal pain, anxiety, insomnia, and progressive wrist extensor weakness after starting a new antiseizure medication. BP is 162/96 mmHg, HR is 118/min, sodium is 124 mEq/L, and urine porphobilinogen is markedly elevated. She has no blistering or photosensitive rash. Which enzyme is deficient?

options.text: Porphobilinogen deaminase in the cytosolic heme synthesis pathway
Uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase causing cutaneous photosensitivity and blistering
Ferrochelatase causing impaired iron insertion into protoporphyrin IX
ALA dehydratase inhibited by lead with basophilic stippling and neuropathy

explanation: Acute intermittent porphyria is caused by porphobilinogen deaminase deficiency. Attacks are triggered by drugs that induce hepatic cytochrome P450 enzymes, fasting, alcohol, or hormonal changes. Accumulation of ALA and porphobilinogen causes neurovisceral symptoms: severe abdominal pain, autonomic instability with hypertension and tachycardia, psychiatric symptoms, motor neuropathy, and SIADH-like hyponatremia. Because the block is before formation of photosensitive porphyrins, photosensitivity is absent. Treatment is hemin and glucose, which suppress hepatic ALA synthase.
pearl: AIP has abdominal pain and neuropathy without photosensitivity. Urine PBG is elevated. Treat with hemin and glucose; avoid CYP-inducing drugs.

optionExplanations.A: Porphobilinogen deaminase deficiency fits the neurovisceral presentation with elevated urine porphobilinogen and no photosensitivity. The accumulated precursors injure nerves and autonomic pathways, causing abdominal pain, psychiatric symptoms, hypertension, tachycardia, and motor neuropathy.
optionExplanations.B: Uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase deficiency causes porphyria cutanea tarda, not AIP. PCT presents with blistering photosensitivity on sun-exposed skin, tea-colored urine, and liver disease associations, but it does not usually cause acute abdominal pain with neuropathy.
optionExplanations.C: Ferrochelatase deficiency causes erythropoietic protoporphyria with photosensitivity due to protoporphyrin accumulation. It is not the best answer because this patient lacks photosensitivity and has elevated urine porphobilinogen with neurovisceral symptoms.
optionExplanations.D: ALA dehydratase inhibition from lead can cause abdominal pain and neuropathy, but the classic findings include basophilic stippling, microcytic anemia, and lead exposure. Markedly elevated porphobilinogen and no exposure history favor AIP instead.

subject: Physiology
testedConcept: WPW with atrial fibrillation - avoid AV nodal blockers
physicianTask: Patient Care: Pharmacotherapy
memoryAnchor: AF + WPW = wide irregular tachycardia. Avoid AV nodal blockers because they push conduction down the accessory pathway. Use procainamide or cardioversion if unstable.
commonTrap: Adenosine and beta-blockers are tempting for SVT/AF, but in pre-excited AF they can accelerate accessory pathway conduction and trigger ventricular fibrillation.
stem: A 27-year-old man with intermittent palpitations presents with lightheadedness. ECG shows an irregular wide-complex tachycardia at 210/min. Prior ECG showed a short PR interval and delta wave. BP is 118/72 mmHg and oxygen saturation is 98%. Which medication is most appropriate?

options.text: Intravenous procainamide to slow conduction through the accessory pathway
Intravenous adenosine to transiently block the atrioventricular node
Intravenous diltiazem to slow atrioventricular nodal conduction
Intravenous digoxin to increase vagal tone at the atrioventricular node

explanation: This is atrial fibrillation in Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome. The irregular wide-complex tachycardia plus history of delta wave indicates pre-excited AF conducting through an accessory pathway. The mechanism is abnormal atrial impulse transport directly to ventricular myocardium, bypassing the AV node. AV nodal blockers such as adenosine, diltiazem, beta-blockers, and digoxin are contraindicated because blocking the AV node can force more atrial impulses down the accessory pathway, causing very rapid ventricular rates, cardiac dysfunction, shock, ischemia, arrhythmia degeneration, or ventricular fibrillation. Stable patients are treated with procainamide or ibutilide; unstable patients require synchronized cardioversion.
pearl: Pre-excited AF: irregular wide-complex tachycardia in WPW. Avoid AV nodal blockers. Use procainamide if stable, synchronized cardioversion if unstable.

optionExplanations.A: Procainamide is correct because it slows conduction through the accessory pathway and prolongs refractoriness. This directly targets the dangerous route of conduction in WPW with AF rather than selectively blocking the AV node.
optionExplanations.B: Adenosine blocks the AV node and is useful for regular narrow-complex AV nodal reentrant tachycardia. It is not appropriate here because the rhythm is irregular wide-complex pre-excited AF, where AV nodal blockade can worsen accessory pathway conduction.
optionExplanations.C: Diltiazem slows AV nodal conduction and is commonly used for ordinary atrial fibrillation rate control. It is not safe in WPW with AF because it can preferentially shift conduction down the accessory pathway.
optionExplanations.D: Digoxin increases vagal tone at the AV node, but this is exactly the wrong target in pre-excited AF. It can accelerate accessory pathway conduction relative to AV nodal conduction and increase risk of ventricular fibrillation.

subject: Physiology
testedConcept: Refeeding syndrome - insulin-driven intracellular phosphate shift
physicianTask: Medical Knowledge: Applying Foundational Science Concepts
memoryAnchor: Refeeding: malnourished patient gets carbohydrates -> insulin surge -> phosphate, potassium, magnesium shift into cells. Hypophosphatemia causes weakness, respiratory failure, rhabdo, arrhythmias.
commonTrap: The problem is not phosphate loss from diarrhea; it is sudden intracellular uptake when carbohydrate refeeding restarts ATP production.
stem: A 46-year-old man with alcohol use disorder and BMI 15 is hospitalized for pancreatitis. After 4 days with minimal intake, tube feeds are started. Within 24 hours he develops weakness and respiratory distress. Labs show phosphate 0.8 mg/dL, potassium 2.9 mEq/L, magnesium 1.2 mg/dL, and glucose 168 mg/dL. Which mechanism best explains these abnormalities?

options.text: Insulin-mediated intracellular shift of phosphate, potassium, and magnesium after carbohydrate refeeding
Renal phosphate wasting from parathyroid hormone excess triggered by hypocalcemia
Osmotic diarrhea causing direct gastrointestinal loss of phosphate and magnesium
Respiratory alkalosis causing phosphate binding to albumin in the plasma compartment

explanation: Refeeding syndrome occurs when carbohydrates are introduced after starvation. Starvation keeps insulin low and cells depleted of phosphate, potassium, and magnesium. Refeeding raises insulin, which drives glucose into cells and restarts glycolysis and ATP production. Phosphate, potassium, and magnesium shift intracellularly, causing severe hypophosphatemia, hypokalemia, and hypomagnesemia. Hypophosphatemia impairs ATP generation in diaphragm and cardiac muscle, causing weakness, respiratory failure, rhabdomyolysis, hemolysis, and arrhythmias. Prevention requires slow caloric advancement and electrolyte repletion.
pearl: Refeeding syndrome is an insulin-driven intracellular shift. Watch phosphate first; severe hypophosphatemia causes respiratory failure and cardiac dysfunction.

optionExplanations.A: Insulin-mediated intracellular electrolyte shift is the central mechanism. Carbohydrate refeeding abruptly increases insulin, causing cellular uptake of glucose with phosphate, potassium, and magnesium. The low serum phosphate then impairs ATP generation in muscle.
optionExplanations.B: Parathyroid hormone excess can cause renal phosphate wasting, but it does not explain the immediate timing after tube feeds or the simultaneous hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia. The clinical context is starvation followed by carbohydrate refeeding.
optionExplanations.C: Gastrointestinal loss can reduce magnesium and potassium, but no diarrhea is described. It also would not explain the classic rapid phosphate collapse after nutrition is restarted in a severely malnourished patient.
optionExplanations.D: Respiratory alkalosis can shift phosphate intracellularly, not bind it to albumin as the primary mechanism. This patient develops respiratory distress after feeding; the low phosphate is the cause of weakness, not the result of hyperventilation.

subject: Biochemistry
testedConcept: G6PD deficiency - impaired NADPH generation causing oxidative hemolysis
physicianTask: Medical Knowledge: Applying Foundational Science Concepts
memoryAnchor: G6PD makes NADPH in the pentose phosphate pathway. NADPH keeps glutathione reduced. Oxidative drugs cause Heinz bodies and bite cells.
commonTrap: Pyruvate kinase deficiency causes chronic hemolysis from low ATP, not episodic oxidative hemolysis with Heinz bodies after TMP-SMX or dapsone.
stem: A 22-year-old man develops fatigue, jaundice, and dark urine 2 days after taking trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole. Hemoglobin is 8.2 g/dL, indirect bilirubin is elevated, haptoglobin is low, and smear shows bite cells with supravital staining positive for Heinz bodies. Which biochemical defect explains this presentation?

options.text: Decreased NADPH production impairing regeneration of reduced glutathione in red cells
Decreased ATP production from impaired conversion of phosphoenolpyruvate to pyruvate
Defective spectrin cytoskeleton causing membrane loss and osmotic fragility
Defective heme synthesis from impaired porphobilinogen deaminase activity

explanation: G6PD deficiency impairs the pentose phosphate pathway, reducing NADPH generation. RBCs need NADPH metabolism to keep glutathione in its reduced form. Reduced glutathione detoxifies oxidants and protects hemoglobin from denaturation. Oxidative stress from sulfonamides, dapsone, primaquine, infections, or fava beans causes hemoglobin precipitation as Heinz bodies; splenic macrophages remove these inclusions, creating bite cells. The mechanism is an enzyme deficiency causing increased oxidative injury, membrane dysfunction, and episodic intravascular and extravascular hemolysis with jaundice, dark urine, low haptoglobin, and elevated indirect bilirubin.
pearl: G6PD deficiency: oxidative stress -> Heinz bodies -> bite cells. Mechanism is low NADPH and low reduced glutathione.

optionExplanations.A: Decreased NADPH production is correct. G6PD is the rate-limiting enzyme of the pentose phosphate pathway, and NADPH is required to regenerate reduced glutathione. Without reduced glutathione, oxidants denature hemoglobin into Heinz bodies.
optionExplanations.B: Decreased ATP from pyruvate kinase deficiency causes chronic hemolytic anemia with echinocytes. It is not the best answer because pyruvate kinase defects do not classically cause Heinz bodies and bite cells after oxidant drug exposure.
optionExplanations.C: Spectrin defects cause hereditary spherocytosis, with spherocytes, increased osmotic fragility, splenomegaly, and often gallstones. This does not explain Heinz bodies or oxidant-triggered hemolysis after TMP-SMX.
optionExplanations.D: Porphobilinogen deaminase deficiency causes acute intermittent porphyria with abdominal pain and neuropsychiatric symptoms. It does not cause bite cells, Heinz bodies, or hemolysis triggered by oxidant medications.

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: Membranous nephropathy - anti-PLA2R antibodies and renal vein thrombosis risk
physicianTask: Patient Care: Diagnosis
memoryAnchor: Membranous nephropathy: adult nephrotic syndrome, anti-PLA2R, granular IgG/C3, spike and dome, risk of renal vein thrombosis.
commonTrap: Minimal change disease is common in children and has foot process effacement only. Adults with anti-PLA2R and spike-and-dome pattern have membranous nephropathy.
stem: A 52-year-old man has progressive leg edema and frothy urine. BP is 142/88 mmHg, creatinine is 1.3 mg/dL, serum albumin is 2.1 g/dL, and urine protein is 9.4 g/day. Anti-PLA2R antibody is positive. Kidney biopsy shows diffuse capillary wall thickening with granular IgG and C3. Which complication is he at increased risk for?

options.text: Renal vein thrombosis due to urinary loss of antithrombin III
Pulmonary hemorrhage due to linear anti-glomerular basement membrane antibodies
Rapidly progressive renal failure from pauci-immune crescentic vasculitis
Recurrent gross hematuria after upper respiratory tract infections

explanation: This patient has membranous nephropathy, a common cause of nephrotic syndrome in adults. Anti-PLA2R antibodies support primary membranous nephropathy. Immune complexes form along the subepithelial side of the glomerular basement membrane, causing diffuse capillary wall thickening, granular IgG/C3, and spike-and-dome appearance on electron microscopy. Nephrotic syndrome causes hypercoagulability because antithrombin III and other anticoagulant proteins are lost in urine while hepatic clotting factor production increases. Membranous nephropathy is especially associated with renal vein thrombosis.
pearl: Adult nephrotic syndrome + anti-PLA2R = membranous nephropathy. Nephrotic syndrome increases thrombosis risk through urinary antithrombin III loss.

optionExplanations.A: Renal vein thrombosis is the classic complication. Heavy proteinuria causes urinary loss of antithrombin III and other anticoagulant proteins, while the liver increases synthesis of clotting factors. This creates a hypercoagulable state, especially in membranous nephropathy.
optionExplanations.B: Pulmonary hemorrhage with linear anti-GBM antibodies describes Goodpasture disease. That is a nephritic syndrome with hematuria and crescents, not anti-PLA2R-positive nephrotic syndrome with granular IgG/C3 deposits.
optionExplanations.C: Pauci-immune crescentic vasculitis causes rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis with little immune deposition on immunofluorescence. This biopsy shows granular immune deposits and nephrotic-range proteinuria, so vasculitis is not the best answer.
optionExplanations.D: Recurrent gross hematuria after upper respiratory infections points to IgA nephropathy. IgA nephropathy causes mesangial IgA deposition and nephritic findings, not anti-PLA2R-positive membranous nephropathy.

subject: Pharmacology
testedConcept: Linezolid - 50S initiation complex inhibition and serotonin syndrome risk
physicianTask: Patient Care: Pharmacotherapy
memoryAnchor: Linezolid binds 23S rRNA of 50S and blocks initiation complex formation. Covers MRSA and VRE. Toxicity: thrombocytopenia, neuropathy, serotonin syndrome.
commonTrap: Vancomycin blocks cell wall D-Ala-D-Ala, not protein synthesis. Daptomycin depolarizes membranes but cannot treat pneumonia because surfactant inactivates it.
stem: A 63-year-old man with chronic kidney disease develops catheter-associated bacteremia. Blood cultures grow vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus faecium. Creatinine is 2.4 mg/dL, WBC is 16,200/uL, and temperature is 39.1 C. He takes sertraline for depression. A drug is started that binds bacterial 23S rRNA and blocks formation of the initiation complex. Which adverse effect is most important to monitor?

options.text: Serotonin syndrome due to monoamine oxidase inhibition by the antibiotic
Red man syndrome due to direct histamine release during rapid infusion
Tendon rupture due to impaired collagen synthesis in connective tissue
Nephrotoxicity due to aminoglycoside accumulation in proximal tubular cells

explanation: Linezolid is an oxazolidinone antibiotic that binds the 23S rRNA of the 50S ribosomal subunit and prevents formation of the bacterial initiation complex, inhibiting protein synthesis. It is active against resistant gram-positive organisms including MRSA and VRE. Linezolid also has weak monoamine oxidase inhibitor activity, so combining it with SSRIs such as sertraline can precipitate serotonin syndrome, with hyperthermia, agitation, tremor, clonus, hyperreflexia, autonomic dysfunction, and arrhythmia risk. Other toxicities include thrombocytopenia, optic neuropathy, and peripheral neuropathy with prolonged treatment.
pearl: Linezolid: 50S initiation complex blocker; covers MRSA and VRE; watch for thrombocytopenia and serotonin syndrome with SSRIs.

optionExplanations.A: Serotonin syndrome is the key risk because linezolid has monoamine oxidase inhibitor activity. In a patient taking sertraline, serotonin can accumulate, causing autonomic instability, neuromuscular hyperactivity, and altered mental status.
optionExplanations.B: Red man syndrome is associated with vancomycin infusion and histamine release. This is not the best answer because the organism is vancomycin-resistant and the drug described blocks the 50S initiation complex.
optionExplanations.C: Tendon rupture is classically associated with fluoroquinolones, especially in older adults or steroid users. Fluoroquinolones inhibit DNA gyrase/topoisomerase, not 23S rRNA initiation complex formation.
optionExplanations.D: Aminoglycosides can cause nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity through proximal tubular and inner ear accumulation. They bind the 30S subunit, not the 50S 23S rRNA site described in this question.

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency - panacinar emphysema and PAS-positive liver globules
physicianTask: Patient Care: Diagnosis
memoryAnchor: A1AT deficiency: panacinar emphysema lower lobes + liver disease. Unopposed neutrophil elastase destroys alveolar walls; mutant protein accumulates in hepatocytes.
commonTrap: Smoking causes centriacinar upper-lobe emphysema. A young patient with basilar emphysema and liver enzyme elevation points to A1AT deficiency.
stem: A 38-year-old man who never smoked has progressive dyspnea. Pulmonary function tests show FEV1/FVC 54% and increased total lung capacity. CT chest shows lower-lobe predominant emphysema. AST is 92 U/L and ALT is 104 U/L. Liver biopsy shows PAS-positive globules in hepatocytes. Which mechanism caused his lung disease?

options.text: Unopposed neutrophil elastase activity causing panacinar alveolar wall destruction
Carbon particle deposition activating macrophages around respiratory bronchioles
Type I hypersensitivity causing reversible bronchoconstriction and mucus plugging
Pulmonary vascular remodeling causing increased resistance in small arteries

explanation: Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency causes panacinar emphysema, especially in the lower lobes, and liver disease from accumulation of misfolded A1AT in hepatocytes. Alpha-1 antitrypsin normally inhibits neutrophil elastase. Deficiency allows elastase to destroy alveolar walls throughout the acinus, increasing compliance and total lung capacity while reducing elastic recoil and FEV1/FVC. PAS-positive globules in hepatocytes represent retained abnormal protein. Smoking worsens disease because oxidants inactivate remaining A1AT and recruit neutrophils.
pearl: A1AT deficiency: lower-lobe panacinar emphysema plus liver disease. Mechanism is unopposed elastase; biopsy shows PAS-positive hepatocyte globules.

optionExplanations.A: Unopposed neutrophil elastase is correct. Loss of A1AT removes the main elastase inhibitor, so elastase digests alveolar septa diffusely through the acinus, producing panacinar emphysema and reduced elastic recoil.
optionExplanations.B: Carbon particle deposition around respiratory bronchioles describes smoking-related centriacinar emphysema. That pattern affects upper lobes and is linked to macrophage-mediated inflammation, not PAS-positive hepatocyte globules.
optionExplanations.C: Type I hypersensitivity with reversible bronchoconstriction describes asthma. Asthma can reduce FEV1/FVC, but it does not cause lower-lobe panacinar emphysema or liver PAS-positive globules.
optionExplanations.D: Pulmonary vascular remodeling causes pulmonary hypertension, not primary emphysema. It can complicate chronic lung disease, but it does not explain alveolar wall destruction or hepatic A1AT accumulation.

subject: Neuroscience
testedConcept: Subarachnoid hemorrhage - nimodipine prevents delayed cerebral ischemia from vasospasm
physicianTask: Patient Care: Pharmacotherapy
memoryAnchor: SAH thunderclap headache. Complication days 3-14: vasospasm. Nimodipine improves outcomes by preventing delayed cerebral ischemia.
commonTrap: Do not confuse nimodipine with acute blood pressure treatment. It is used after aneurysmal SAH to reduce delayed ischemic deficits from vasospasm.
stem: A 49-year-old woman develops the worst headache of her life and collapses. BP is 174/96 mmHg. CT head shows blood in the basal cisterns, and angiography reveals a ruptured anterior communicating artery aneurysm. She undergoes coil embolization. On hospital day 2, which medication reduces risk of delayed neurologic deficits?

options.text: Nimodipine to reduce delayed cerebral ischemia from arterial vasospasm
Alteplase to lyse subarachnoid clot and restore cerebrospinal fluid flow
Mannitol to prevent hydrocephalus by decreasing cerebrospinal fluid production
Warfarin to prevent embolization from the treated intracranial aneurysm

explanation: Aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage can be complicated by cerebral vasospasm, typically days 3-14 after bleeding. Breakdown products from subarachnoid blood irritate cerebral arteries, causing vasoconstriction, reduced perfusion, delayed cerebral ischemia, and new neurologic deficits. Nimodipine is a dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker given to patients with aneurysmal SAH to improve neurologic outcomes by reducing delayed ischemic deficits from vasospasm. Definitive aneurysm treatment prevents rebleeding, but nimodipine addresses the delayed vasospasm complication.
pearl: SAH complications: rebleeding early, hydrocephalus, and vasospasm days 3-14. Nimodipine is standard after aneurysmal SAH.

optionExplanations.A: Nimodipine is correct because it reduces delayed cerebral ischemia after aneurysmal SAH. The target is arterial vasospasm caused by blood breakdown products in the subarachnoid space, not clot lysis or anticoagulation.
optionExplanations.B: Alteplase is contraindicated in intracranial hemorrhage. Although subarachnoid blood can impair CSF flow, thrombolysis would worsen bleeding rather than prevent vasospasm.
optionExplanations.C: Mannitol treats elevated intracranial pressure by osmotic diuresis, but it does not prevent hydrocephalus by lowering CSF production. It also does not target delayed vasospasm after SAH.
optionExplanations.D: Warfarin would increase bleeding risk after subarachnoid hemorrhage. Coil embolization treats the aneurysm; anticoagulation is not used to prevent aneurysm embolization and would be dangerous here.

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: Celiac disease - anti-tTG IgA and dermatitis herpetiformis
physicianTask: Patient Care: Diagnosis
memoryAnchor: Celiac disease: anti-tTG IgA, villous atrophy, crypt hyperplasia, intraepithelial lymphocytes. Dermatitis herpetiformis has IgA deposits in dermal papillae.
commonTrap: Crohn disease can cause diarrhea and weight loss, but dermatitis herpetiformis and anti-tTG antibodies point to gluten-sensitive enteropathy.
stem: A 34-year-old woman has chronic diarrhea, bloating, and 8-kg weight loss. Hemoglobin is 9.8 g/dL with MCV 72 fL. She has intensely pruritic grouped vesicles on the elbows and knees. Anti-tissue transglutaminase IgA is positive. Duodenal biopsy shows villous atrophy, crypt hyperplasia, and intraepithelial lymphocytes. Which immune process causes the rash?

options.text: Granular IgA deposition in dermal papillae associated with gluten-sensitive enteropathy
Linear IgG deposition along the basement membrane zone of the epidermis
IgG antibodies against desmoglein causing loss of keratinocyte adhesion
T-cell mediated destruction of melanocytes in the basal epidermal layer

explanation: This patient has celiac disease with dermatitis herpetiformis. Celiac disease is an immune-mediated reaction to gluten in genetically susceptible patients, often HLA-DQ2 or DQ8. Tissue transglutaminase enzyme deamidates gliadin peptides, increasing their immunogenicity and leading to T-cell activation, immune injury, villous atrophy, and malabsorption. Dermatitis herpetiformis presents with pruritic grouped vesicles on extensor surfaces and is caused by granular IgA antibody deposition in dermal papillae. Direct immunofluorescence of skin biopsy confirms the diagnosis. Both intestinal and skin disease improve with a gluten-free diet; dapsone can rapidly treat rash symptoms.
pearl: Celiac rash = dermatitis herpetiformis = granular IgA in dermal papillae. Best serology is anti-tTG IgA, with total IgA level.

optionExplanations.A: Granular IgA in dermal papillae is correct. Dermatitis herpetiformis is the cutaneous manifestation of celiac disease and classically affects extensor surfaces with intensely pruritic grouped vesicles.
optionExplanations.B: Linear IgG along the basement membrane describes bullous pemphigoid. That disease causes tense bullae in older adults and is not associated with villous atrophy or anti-tTG antibodies.
optionExplanations.C: IgG against desmoglein causes pemphigus vulgaris with flaccid bullae, oral erosions, and a positive Nikolsky sign. This does not explain malabsorption or granular IgA deposition.
optionExplanations.D: T-cell destruction of melanocytes causes vitiligo. It produces depigmented patches, not grouped vesicles, iron deficiency anemia, diarrhea, and celiac serologies.

subject: Endocrinology
testedConcept: Primary hyperaldosteronism - suppressed renin with hypertension and hypokalemic alkalosis
physicianTask: Patient Care: Laboratory and Diagnostic Studies
memoryAnchor: Conn syndrome: hypertension, hypokalemia, metabolic alkalosis, high aldosterone, low renin. Aldosterone increases ENaC sodium reabsorption and potassium/hydrogen secretion.
commonTrap: Renal artery stenosis has high renin and high aldosterone. Primary aldosteronism has suppressed renin because aldosterone is autonomous.
stem: A 43-year-old woman has resistant hypertension despite three medications. BP is 168/102 mmHg. Labs show sodium 146 mEq/L, potassium 2.8 mEq/L, bicarbonate 34 mEq/L, plasma aldosterone elevated, and plasma renin activity suppressed. Which renal tubular effect explains the electrolyte pattern?

options.text: Increased principal cell sodium reabsorption with potassium secretion in the collecting duct
Decreased proximal tubular bicarbonate reabsorption causing metabolic acidosis
Increased thick ascending limb sodium chloride reabsorption with calcium retention
Decreased distal convoluted tubule sodium chloride reabsorption causing hyperkalemia

explanation: Primary hyperaldosteronism causes autonomous aldosterone secretion, often from an adrenal adenoma or bilateral adrenal hyperplasia. Aldosterone acts on mineralocorticoid receptors in principal cells of the collecting duct, increasing ENaC expression and Na/K ATPase activity. Sodium reabsorption creates a lumen-negative potential that promotes potassium secretion through ROMK channels and hydrogen ion secretion by alpha-intercalated cells. The result is hypertension, hypokalemia, and metabolic alkalosis. Renin is suppressed because extracellular volume expansion reduces RAAS activation.
pearl: Primary hyperaldosteronism: high aldosterone, low renin, hypertension, hypokalemia, metabolic alkalosis. Screen with aldosterone-renin ratio.

optionExplanations.A: Increased principal cell sodium reabsorption is correct. Aldosterone increases ENaC and Na/K ATPase activity, creating a lumen-negative collecting duct that drives potassium and hydrogen secretion.
optionExplanations.B: Decreased proximal bicarbonate reabsorption would cause a proximal renal tubular acidosis with metabolic acidosis, not metabolic alkalosis. It also would not explain suppressed renin with high aldosterone.
optionExplanations.C: Thick ascending limb sodium chloride reabsorption involves NKCC2 and loop diuretic physiology. It is not the aldosterone-sensitive segment responsible for potassium wasting in primary hyperaldosteronism.
optionExplanations.D: Decreased distal convoluted tubule NaCl reabsorption resembles thiazide effect or Gitelman physiology and can activate RAAS. This patient has autonomous aldosterone with suppressed renin, not primary DCT salt wasting.

subject: Microbiology
testedConcept: Pseudomonas exotoxin A - ADP-ribosylation of EF-2
physicianTask: Medical Knowledge: Applying Foundational Science Concepts
memoryAnchor: Exotoxin A from Pseudomonas and diphtheria toxin both ADP-ribosylate EF-2, stopping protein synthesis. Pseudomonas: burns, CF, ventilators, blue-green pigment.
commonTrap: Endotoxin causes shock through lipid A and macrophage cytokines, but exotoxin A specifically blocks protein synthesis through EF-2.
stem: A 67-year-old man in a burn unit develops fever and hypotension. Wound culture grows an oxidase-positive gram-negative rod with blue-green pigment and grape-like odor. WBC is 19,800/uL and lactate is 4.1 mmol/L. One virulence factor produced by this organism inactivates elongation factor 2. Which molecular action is involved?

options.text: ADP-ribosylation of elongation factor 2 causing inhibition of protein synthesis
Cleavage of SNARE proteins preventing acetylcholine release at neuromuscular junctions
Overactivation of adenylate cyclase causing chloride and water secretion in intestine
Superantigen-mediated cross-linking of MHC class II molecules and T-cell receptors

explanation: Pseudomonas aeruginosa is an oxidase-positive gram-negative rod associated with burns, cystic fibrosis, ventilator-associated pneumonia, hot tubs, and catheter infections. It produces blue-green pigments and a grape-like odor. Exotoxin A uses ADP-ribosylation to inhibit elongation factor 2, blocking ribosomal protein synthesis and causing cell death. This mechanism is similar to diphtheria toxin. Pseudomonas also has endotoxin, elastase, phospholipase C, bacteremia risk, antibiotic resistance mechanisms, and biofilm formation, but the EF-2 question specifically points to exotoxin A.
pearl: Pseudomonas exotoxin A and diphtheria toxin both ADP-ribosylate EF-2. Burn wound plus blue-green pigment strongly suggests Pseudomonas.

optionExplanations.A: ADP-ribosylation of EF-2 is correct. Exotoxin A inactivates elongation factor 2 and shuts down host protein synthesis, leading to tissue injury and necrosis.
optionExplanations.B: SNARE cleavage describes botulinum or tetanus toxin. Those toxins affect neurotransmitter release, not protein synthesis, and they are not produced by an oxidase-positive blue-green burn pathogen.
optionExplanations.C: Adenylate cyclase overactivation describes cholera toxin or heat-labile ETEC toxin. That mechanism causes watery diarrhea rather than burn wound sepsis from Pseudomonas.
optionExplanations.D: Superantigen cross-linking describes toxic shock syndrome toxin or streptococcal pyrogenic exotoxins. Pseudomonas exotoxin A instead acts enzymatically on EF-2.

subject: Pharmacology
testedConcept: SGLT2 inhibitors - euglycemic ketoacidosis mechanism
physicianTask: Patient Care: Pharmacotherapy
memoryAnchor: SGLT2 inhibitors cause glucosuria, reduce insulin need, increase glucagon, and can trigger euglycemic DKA. Also genital mycotic infections.
commonTrap: Normal-ish glucose does not exclude ketoacidosis in patients taking SGLT2 inhibitors. The drug wastes glucose in urine while ketosis continues.
stem: A 58-year-old man with type 2 diabetes and heart failure starts empagliflozin. Two weeks later, after poor oral intake from gastroenteritis, he presents with nausea and tachypnea. Glucose is 174 mg/dL, bicarbonate is 12 mEq/L, anion gap is 24, beta-hydroxybutyrate is elevated, and urine glucose is positive. What best explains this complication?

options.text: Increased urinary glucose loss lowering insulin levels while promoting ketogenesis
Inhibition of mitochondrial beta-oxidation causing lactic acidosis without ketosis
Increased pancreatic insulin secretion causing hypoglycemia and adrenergic symptoms
Delayed gastric emptying causing dehydration and isolated contraction alkalosis

explanation: SGLT2 inhibitors block glucose reabsorption in the proximal tubule, causing glucosuria and lowering plasma glucose independent of insulin. During fasting, illness, or reduced carbohydrate intake, lower insulin levels and relatively higher glucagon promote lipolysis and hepatic ketogenesis. Because glucose is lost in urine, serum glucose may be only mildly elevated, producing euglycemic diabetic ketoacidosis. The high anion gap metabolic acidosis, elevated beta-hydroxybutyrate, tachypnea, and positive urine glucose after empagliflozin are classic. Treatment includes insulin, dextrose, fluids, and stopping the SGLT2 inhibitor.
pearl: SGLT2 inhibitors can cause euglycemic DKA: high anion gap ketosis with glucose often under 250 mg/dL.

optionExplanations.A: Urinary glucose loss with reduced insulin and increased ketogenesis is correct. SGLT2 inhibition masks hyperglycemia by wasting glucose in urine while the hormonal state still favors ketone production.
optionExplanations.B: Mitochondrial beta-oxidation inhibition would reduce ketone formation, not increase beta-hydroxybutyrate. Lactic acidosis without ketosis does not fit the elevated ketones in this case.
optionExplanations.C: Increased insulin secretion describes sulfonylureas or meglitinides, not SGLT2 inhibitors. Empagliflozin acts in the kidney and does not directly stimulate pancreatic beta cells.
optionExplanations.D: Delayed gastric emptying is associated with GLP-1 receptor agonists or diabetic gastroparesis. It does not explain high anion gap ketosis with glucosuria after SGLT2 inhibitor use.

subject: Biostatistics
testedConcept: Lead-time bias - apparent survival improvement without delayed death
usmleContentArea: Biostatistics, Epidemiology/Population Health, & Interpretation of the Medical Literature
physicianTask: Practice-Based Learning and Improvement
memoryAnchor: Lead-time bias: earlier diagnosis increases measured survival time from diagnosis even if death occurs at the same time. Mortality rate is better than survival time for screening benefit.
commonTrap: Length-time bias means screening detects slower-growing disease. Lead-time bias means the clock starts earlier.
stem: A clinical study enrolls 18,000 patients in a new CT screening program, and each patient with pancreatic cancer has disease detected at an earlier stage. Mean age is 62 years. Five-year survival after diagnosis increases from 8% to 24%, but disease-specific mortality per 100,000 people is unchanged after 8 years. Median age at death and treatment protocols are unchanged. Which bias best explains the apparent survival benefit?

options.text: Lead-time bias from starting the survival clock earlier after screening diagnosis
Length-time bias from preferential detection of slow-growing less aggressive tumors
Recall bias from differential memory of exposure among cases and controls
Observer bias from researchers knowing which subjects received screening

explanation: Lead-time bias occurs when screening detects disease earlier without changing the time of death. Survival measured from diagnosis appears longer because the diagnosis date moves earlier, even though mortality is unchanged. In screening studies, disease-specific mortality is a more meaningful endpoint than five-year survival because survival time can be artificially inflated by earlier detection. The unchanged mortality rate and unchanged median age at death prove that patients are not living longer; they simply know about the cancer for a longer period.
pearl: Screening benefit should reduce mortality, not just increase survival after diagnosis. Earlier diagnosis alone creates lead-time bias.

optionExplanations.A: Lead-time bias is correct because screening starts the diagnostic clock earlier while death occurs at the same time. This increases measured survival from diagnosis without reducing mortality.
optionExplanations.B: Length-time bias is related but different. It occurs when screening preferentially finds slower-growing disease with a longer asymptomatic phase. Here the stem emphasizes unchanged mortality and earlier clock start.
optionExplanations.C: Recall bias occurs in retrospective studies when cases remember exposures differently than controls. This screening program outcome does not depend on patient memory of exposure.
optionExplanations.D: Observer bias occurs when knowledge of group assignment influences outcome assessment. Mortality and age at death are objective outcomes and do not explain the survival-mortality mismatch.

subject: Ethics
testedConcept: Medical error disclosure - disclose facts, apologize, and explain prevention
physicianTask: Professionalism, Legal, and Ethical Principles
memoryAnchor: Medical error disclosure: tell the patient/family what happened, apologize, explain consequences and next steps, and describe prevention. Do not blame others or hide uncertainty.
commonTrap: Fear of litigation is never a reason to conceal an error. Disclosure should be prompt, factual, compassionate, and system-focused.
stem: A 66-year-old hospitalized man receives 10 times the intended insulin dose because two medication concentrations were stored in similar vials. Glucose falls to 34 mg/dL, causing a brief seizure. He recovers after dextrose. Vitals are stable. His daughter asks what happened. Which response is most appropriate?

options.text: Disclose the overdose, apologize, explain treatment given, and describe prevention steps
Say only that his glucose unexpectedly dropped because no permanent injury occurred
Tell the daughter to speak with the nurse because the physician did not administer it
Avoid discussing the event until hospital legal counsel gives permission

explanation: Physicians should promptly disclose medical errors to patients or authorized surrogates. Disclosure includes the known facts, expected consequences, treatment provided, an apology or expression of regret, and steps being taken to prevent recurrence. The response should avoid blame-shifting and should not conceal the error because the patient recovered. Transparent disclosure supports autonomy, trust, patient safety, and systems-based risk reduction. The prevention mechanism is system redesign: separating look-alike vials, barcode scanning, double-checks for high-alert medications, and medication safety reporting to reduce recurrence.
pearl: Medical error disclosure is direct and compassionate: what happened, what it means, what treatment was done, apology, prevention plan, and patient safety mechanism review.

optionExplanations.A: Full disclosure is correct. The patient experienced harm from a medication error, so the family should receive a factual explanation, apology, treatment summary, and prevention plan.
optionExplanations.B: Minimizing the event is inappropriate because it hides the actual overdose. Lack of permanent injury does not remove the ethical duty to disclose a harmful medical error.
optionExplanations.C: Blaming or redirecting to the nurse is wrong. Physicians remain responsible for honest communication with patients and families, and disclosure should be team-based rather than blame-based.
optionExplanations.D: Waiting for legal counsel is not appropriate when immediate disclosure is required. Risk management can help, but legal concern does not justify delaying or concealing the facts.

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: Ovarian torsion - venous obstruction before arterial compromise
usmleContentArea: Female and Transgender Reproductive System & Breast
physicianTask: Patient Care: Diagnosis
memoryAnchor: Ovarian torsion: sudden unilateral pelvic pain, enlarged ovary, decreased venous flow first; arterial flow may persist early. Risk with ovarian mass >5 cm.
commonTrap: Normal arterial Doppler does not rule out torsion because venous and lymphatic obstruction occur first and dual arterial supply can preserve arterial flow early.
stem: A 29-year-old woman has several brief episodes of right pelvic pain followed by persistent severe pain with nausea and vomiting. Temperature is 37.1 C, WBC is 12,600/uL, and pregnancy test is negative. Ultrasound shows a 6.5-cm ovarian cyst with an enlarged edematous right ovary. Doppler detects arterial inflow but no venous flow. Which mechanism best explains why the imaging does not exclude an evolving vascular emergency?

options.text: Twisting of the ovarian pedicle causing venous obstruction before arterial compromise
Ruptured ectopic pregnancy causing hemoperitoneum despite negative pregnancy test
Pelvic inflammatory disease causing bilateral tubo-ovarian abscess formation
Endometrioma bleeding cyclically due to ectopic endometrial gland activation

explanation: Ovarian torsion occurs when the ovary twists around the infundibulopelvic and utero-ovarian ligaments. Ovarian masses or cysts larger than 5 cm increase risk. Venous and lymphatic outflow are obstructed first because veins are low-pressure and compressible, leading to ovarian edema and enlargement. Arterial inflow may persist early due to higher pressure and dual arterial supply from ovarian and uterine arteries. Therefore preserved arterial Doppler flow does not exclude torsion. Surgical detorsion is urgent to preserve ovarian function.
pearl: Ovarian torsion: sudden unilateral pain, vomiting, enlarged edematous ovary. Venous flow disappears first; arterial flow can remain early.

optionExplanations.A: Venous obstruction before arterial compromise is correct. Twisting compresses low-pressure veins first, causing congestion and edema while arterial flow may still be detectable early.
optionExplanations.B: Ruptured ectopic pregnancy requires pregnancy, and the test is negative. It also would not explain an enlarged edematous ovary with absent venous Doppler flow around a large cyst.
optionExplanations.C: Pelvic inflammatory disease usually causes fever, cervical motion tenderness, bilateral adnexal pain, and infectious discharge. It is not the best answer for sudden unilateral pain with torsion Doppler findings.
optionExplanations.D: Endometrioma causes chronic cyclic pelvic pain and a cyst with ground-glass appearance. It does not cause acute absent venous flow from twisting of the ovarian pedicle.

subject: Immunology
testedConcept: Common variable immunodeficiency - impaired B cell differentiation with low immunoglobulins
physicianTask: Patient Care: Diagnosis
memoryAnchor: CVID: recurrent sinopulmonary infections after childhood, low IgG/IgA/IgM, normal B cell count but poor plasma cell differentiation. Risk autoimmune disease and lymphoma.
commonTrap: X-linked agammaglobulinemia presents in male infants with absent B cells. CVID often presents later with normal B cell numbers but defective differentiation.
stem: A 26-year-old woman has recurrent sinusitis and pneumonia since college. She also has chronic diarrhea from Giardia. Labs show IgG 220 mg/dL, IgA low, IgM low, and normal total B-cell count by flow cytometry. HIV test is negative. Which mechanism best explains her disease?

options.text: Failure of B cells to differentiate into antibody-secreting plasma cells
Failure of pre-B cells to mature due to Bruton tyrosine kinase mutation
Defective oxidative burst in neutrophils causing catalase-positive infections
Absent thymic development causing profound T-cell lymphopenia and hypocalcemia

explanation: Common variable immunodeficiency presents in adolescence or adulthood with recurrent sinopulmonary infections, Giardia, low immunoglobulins, and impaired antibody responses. B-cell numbers can be normal, but differentiation into plasma cells is defective, causing low IgG with low IgA and/or IgM. The immune mechanism is failure of B-cell activation and maturation into antibody-secreting plasma cells despite preserved cell counts. Patients are at increased risk for autoimmune disease, granulomatous disease, and lymphoma. Treatment is immunoglobulin replacement. The later onset and normal B-cell count distinguish CVID from X-linked agammaglobulinemia.
pearl: CVID: low immunoglobulins with normal B cells but poor plasma cell differentiation. Think recurrent sinopulmonary infections plus Giardia in a young adult.

optionExplanations.A: Defective plasma cell differentiation is correct. CVID has quantitatively present B cells that fail to become effective antibody-secreting plasma cells, leading to low IgG and low IgA or IgM.
optionExplanations.B: BTK mutation causes X-linked agammaglobulinemia with absent mature B cells, usually in boys after maternal IgG wanes around 6 months. This patient is an adult woman with normal B-cell count.
optionExplanations.C: Defective oxidative burst causes chronic granulomatous disease, with catalase-positive infections and abnormal dihydrorhodamine testing. Immunoglobulin levels are not the central abnormality.
optionExplanations.D: Absent thymic development describes DiGeorge syndrome, with T-cell deficiency, conotruncal heart defects, abnormal facies, and hypocalcemia. It does not match normal B cells with pan-hypogammaglobulinemia.

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: Aortic regurgitation - eccentric hypertrophy from volume overload
physicianTask: Medical Knowledge: Applying Foundational Science Concepts
memoryAnchor: Aortic regurgitation = volume overload -> eccentric LV hypertrophy and widened pulse pressure. Aortic stenosis = pressure overload -> concentric hypertrophy.
commonTrap: Students mix up AS and AR. Stenosis is pressure overload; regurgitation is volume overload.
stem: A 41-year-old man with bicuspid aortic valve has exertional dyspnea. BP is 154/48 mmHg, pulse is bounding, and a blowing early diastolic murmur is heard along the left sternal border. Echocardiography shows severe aortic regurgitation and left ventricular dilation. Which myocardial adaptation is expected?

options.text: Sarcomeres added in series causing eccentric hypertrophy from volume overload
Sarcomeres added in parallel causing concentric hypertrophy from pressure overload
Myocyte disarray with dynamic obstruction due to septal hypertrophy
Right ventricular hypertrophy caused by chronic hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction

explanation: Aortic regurgitation causes volume overload because blood flows back from the aorta into the left ventricle during diastole. The LV must accommodate both normal pulmonary venous return and regurgitant volume. Chronic volume overload increases chamber radius and wall stress; compensation occurs by adding sarcomeres in series, producing eccentric hypertrophy with chamber dilation. The widened pulse pressure and bounding pulses result from increased stroke volume and rapid diastolic runoff into the LV. Aortic stenosis, by contrast, causes pressure overload and concentric hypertrophy.
pearl: Volume overload adds sarcomeres in series -> eccentric hypertrophy. Pressure overload adds sarcomeres in parallel -> concentric hypertrophy.

optionExplanations.A: Sarcomeres in series with eccentric hypertrophy is correct for chronic volume overload. Aortic regurgitation increases LV end-diastolic volume, causing chamber dilation and compensatory eccentric growth.
optionExplanations.B: Sarcomeres in parallel with concentric hypertrophy describes pressure overload, especially aortic stenosis or hypertension. It is not the best answer for regurgitant volume overload with widened pulse pressure.
optionExplanations.C: Myocyte disarray with dynamic obstruction describes hypertrophic cardiomyopathy. HCM has systolic murmur changes with Valsalva, not a blowing early diastolic murmur of aortic regurgitation.
optionExplanations.D: Right ventricular hypertrophy from hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction describes cor pulmonale. The lesion here is left-sided aortic valve regurgitation causing LV volume overload.

subject: Neuroscience
testedConcept: Brown-Sequard syndrome - ipsilateral motor/proprioception loss and contralateral pain loss
physicianTask: Patient Care: Diagnosis
memoryAnchor: Brown-Sequard: ipsilateral corticospinal and dorsal column loss below lesion; contralateral pain/temp loss starts 1-2 levels below due to spinothalamic crossing.
commonTrap: Pain and temperature cross in the spinal cord after ascending a few levels, while dorsal column fibers cross in the medulla.
stem: A 33-year-old man is stabbed in the left back at T10. BP is 126/78 mmHg and HR is 88/min. Exam shows left leg weakness with hyperreflexia, Babinski sign, and loss of vibration and proprioception below the umbilicus. Pain and temperature sensation are decreased on the right beginning around T12. MRI confirms left hemisection of the spinal cord. Which tract finding explains this pattern?

options.text: Ipsilateral corticospinal and dorsal column injury with contralateral spinothalamic loss
Bilateral dorsal column injury with preserved motor and pain-temperature pathways
Central cord injury damaging crossing anterior white commissure fibers only
Anterior spinal artery infarction sparing corticospinal tract function bilaterally

explanation: Brown-Sequard syndrome results from hemisection of the spinal cord. The corticospinal tract crosses in the medullary pyramids, so spinal cord injury causes ipsilateral upper motor neuron weakness below the lesion. Dorsal column fibers for vibration and proprioception also ascend ipsilaterally and cross in the medulla, so they are lost ipsilaterally below the lesion. Spinothalamic pain and temperature transport fibers enter the cord, ascend or descend one to two segments in Lissauer tract, then cross in the anterior white commissure; therefore hemisection causes contralateral neurologic pain and temperature loss beginning below the lesion. The diagnosis depends on tract-crossing mechanism, neurologic localization, and preservation or loss of specific sensory pathways.
pearl: Brown-Sequard = ipsilateral motor and dorsal column loss, contralateral pain/temp loss below lesion.

optionExplanations.A: Ipsilateral corticospinal and dorsal column injury with contralateral spinothalamic loss is correct. The pattern follows where each tract crosses: motor and proprioception already remain ipsilateral in the cord, while pain/temp crosses segmentally.
optionExplanations.B: Bilateral dorsal column injury would cause bilateral vibration and proprioception loss without the unilateral motor and contralateral pain-temperature pattern. It does not explain hemisection findings.
optionExplanations.C: Central cord injury damages crossing spinothalamic fibers and produces bilateral segmental pain-temperature loss, often in a cape-like pattern. It does not cause ipsilateral UMN weakness plus dorsal column loss below T10.
optionExplanations.D: Anterior spinal artery infarction affects corticospinal and spinothalamic tracts bilaterally while sparing dorsal columns. This is opposite of the unilateral hemisection pattern described.

subject: Pharmacology
testedConcept: Clozapine-associated severe neutropenia — immediate interruption and infection evaluation
physicianTask: Patient Care: Pharmacotherapy
memoryAnchor: Clozapine treats refractory schizophrenia and reduces suicidality, but monitor ANC for agranulocytosis. Other risks: seizures, myocarditis, metabolic syndrome, sialorrhea.
commonTrap: All antipsychotics can cause metabolic effects or EPS, but clozapine has the signature agranulocytosis monitoring requirement.
stem: A 28-year-old man with treatment-resistant schizophrenia starts clozapine after adequate trials of two other antipsychotics. Four weeks later he develops fever, sore throat, and painful swallowing. Temperature is 38.6 C, WBC is 1,900/uL, and absolute neutrophil count is 420/uL. Blood cultures are obtained. Which immediate medication plan is most appropriate while this complication is evaluated and treated?

options.text: Interrupt clozapine and urgently manage severe neutropenia while following serial absolute neutrophil counts
Continue clozapine unchanged because infection symptoms are expected during early treatment
Reduce the clozapine dose but continue treatment until the absolute neutrophil count falls below 100/uL
Start risperidone immediately while continuing clozapine to prevent psychiatric relapse

explanation: An ANC of 420/uL is severe neutropenia in a patient taking clozapine. This adverse effect causes loss of innate immune protection and therefore creates a high risk of rapidly progressive infection. Immediate management is to interrupt clozapine because continued exposure can worsen the neutropenia; the patient also needs urgent infection evaluation and treatment while ANC is followed closely. Hematology input and granulocyte colony-stimulating factor may be appropriate depending on clinical severity. Routine ANC monitoring is intended to detect this complication before infection becomes life-threatening. Continuing or merely reducing clozapine is unsafe at this count, and adding another antipsychotic does not address the hematologic emergency.
pearl: Clozapine is reserved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia but requires ANC surveillance. Fever or sore throat with severe neutropenia requires immediate interruption and urgent infection management.

optionExplanations.A: The ANC is below 500/uL, indicating severe neutropenia with symptoms concerning for infection. Clozapine must be interrupted while urgent infection evaluation, treatment, and serial ANC monitoring proceed.
optionExplanations.B: Fever and sore throat are not expected benign effects of clozapine. Continuing the drug during severe neutropenia risks progression to invasive infection and sepsis.
optionExplanations.C: Dose reduction is insufficient at an ANC of 420/uL. The medication should be interrupted rather than continued until an arbitrarily lower threshold is reached.
optionExplanations.D: Adding risperidone does not reverse clozapine-associated neutropenia, and continuing clozapine leaves the hematologic trigger in place. Stabilizing the infection and neutrophil count takes priority.

subject: Pathology
testedConcept: Acute pancreatitis - hypocalcemia from fat saponification
physicianTask: Medical Knowledge: Applying Foundational Science Concepts
memoryAnchor: Pancreatitis releases lipase -> fat necrosis -> calcium binds free fatty acids -> saponification -> hypocalcemia.
commonTrap: Hypocalcemia in pancreatitis is not mainly from low PTH; it is calcium sequestration in necrotic fat.
stem: A 44-year-old man with heavy alcohol use has severe epigastric pain radiating to the back. Temperature is 38.2 C, HR is 122/min, lipase is 2,900 U/L, calcium is 7.1 mg/dL, and CT abdomen shows pancreatic inflammation with peripancreatic fat stranding. Which mechanism explains the low calcium?

options.text: Binding of calcium to free fatty acids released during enzymatic fat necrosis
Decreased parathyroid hormone secretion from autoimmune parathyroid destruction
Increased renal calcium excretion due to loop diuretic activation of NKCC2
Deposition of immune complexes in glomeruli causing urinary calcium wasting

explanation: Acute pancreatitis causes premature activation of pancreatic enzymes. Lipase breaks down peripancreatic fat into free fatty acids, which bind calcium and form chalky calcium soaps in a process called fat saponification. This calcium sequestration causes hypocalcemia and is associated with more severe pancreatitis. Alcohol and gallstones are common causes. CT findings of pancreatic inflammation and peripancreatic fat stranding plus elevated lipase support the diagnosis. Treatment is aggressive IV fluids, pain control, nutrition, and management of complications.
pearl: Pancreatitis hypocalcemia = fat necrosis plus calcium saponification. Low calcium is a marker of severe disease.

optionExplanations.A: Calcium binding to free fatty acids is correct. Pancreatic lipase injures fat, releasing fatty acids that bind calcium and precipitate as soaps, lowering serum calcium.
optionExplanations.B: Autoimmune parathyroid destruction can cause hypocalcemia, but it is not the mechanism in acute pancreatitis. There is no evidence of low PTH or autoimmune polyendocrine disease.
optionExplanations.C: Loop diuretics increase urinary calcium excretion by blocking NKCC2, but no loop diuretic use is described. The timing with pancreatitis and fat necrosis points to saponification.
optionExplanations.D: Immune complex glomerulonephritis does not explain hypocalcemia in pancreatitis. It would produce nephritic urine findings rather than calcium precipitation in necrotic fat.
